adv_u01.text: Unit 1 - Present Continuous and Present Simple (1): Stative verbs
adv_u01.note: I think vs. I'm thinking - two meanings
adv_u01.exp:
Unit 1 (Advanced) — Present Continuous and Present Simple with stative verbs: dual interpretation.

Stative verbs are usually in Simple, but many allow Continuous with a shift in meaning. Think: I think you're right (opinion) ↔ I'm thinking about your offer (considering). See: I see what you mean (understand) ↔ I'm seeing a specialist (meeting/consulting). Have: I have three brothers (possess) ↔ I'm having lunch (process). Taste/smell: The soup tastes salty (quality) ↔ She's tasting the soup (actively testing). Feel: I feel it's unfair (believe) ↔ I'm feeling dizzy (physical state, Simple also accepted).

Register: Continuous with stative verbs lends the statement dynamism, often a conversational tone. Pragmatics: I'm loving it — an advertising slogan, emphatic, not neutral. Formal writing usually stays with Simple for facts and opinions. This layer distinguishes C1 from B2: the ability to consciously switch form for nuance.
adv_u01.exc:
Stative verbs (know, believe, belong, own, contain) are usually not used in Continuous.

But think, have, see, feel, taste change meaning: I'm thinking (considering) vs. I think (believe).
mistakes: I am knowing him → I know him — know is stative.
I am having a car → I have a car — ownership — simple.
This is tasting good → This tastes good — perception — simple.

adv_u02.text: Unit 2 – Present Continuous and Present Simple (2)
adv_u02.note: Temporary vs. permanent situations - advanced cases
adv_u02.exp:
Unit 2 (Advanced) — temporary vs permanent and the emotional shades of Continuous.

Present Continuous is used not only for "right now":
  • Temporary period: I'm living with my parents while my flat is being renovated (permanent — I live with my parents).
  • Emotional colouring with always, constantly, forever: She's always losing her keys — always loses her keys (irritation, uncharacteristic of neutral Simple).
  • Developing trends: The climate is getting warmer.
  • Vivid narrative in the present (telling an anecdote): So he walks in and he's waving his arms…
Simple — for permanent states, scientific truths, schedules, general characteristics: Water boils at 100°C; The Earth orbits the Sun.

Pragmatics: the choice of tense is a stylistic instrument. Continuous with always conveys expressiveness; in academic writing such forms are out of place. The ability to switch between descriptive Simple and engaged Continuous is a marker of advanced command.
adv_u02.exc:
always + Continuous = annoying habit: She's always complaining.

Continuous stresses temporariness: I'm living in Paris (temporarily) vs. I live in Paris (permanently).
mistakes: The Earth is going round the sun → The Earth goes round the sun — scientific fact — simple.
He always is complaining → He is always complaining — order: to be + always + -ing.
I'm usually working late → I usually work late — habit — simple.

adv_u03.text: Unit 3 – Past Simple and Present Perfect
adv_u03.exp:
Unit 3 (Advanced) — Past Simple vs Present Perfect: context, register, usage.

The basic rule "specific past → Simple, connection to present → Perfect" is complicated by contextual factors. Discourse structure: when breaking news, Perfect: A man has been arrested in connection with the robbery. Then details in Simple: He was arrested at his home this morning. This "headline — unfold" pairing is typical of news and reports.

Regional variation: AmE more freely uses Past Simple with just, already, yet: Did you eat yet? (AmE) vs Have you eaten yet? (BrE). In academic BrE, Perfect is required with these adverbs.

Shift of perspective: It's the first/second/only time + Present Perfect: It's the first time I've seen snow. Signal words: ever, never, so far, up to now, recently, lately, since → Perfect; ago, in 1999, yesterday, last week, when → Simple.

Example: I've lived in Rome for ten years (up to now) ↔ I lived in Rome for ten years (period completed). In narrative, Simple prevails; Perfect breaks the narrative, returning to the present.
adv_u03.exc: AmE more often uses Past Simple where BrE prefers Present Perfect: Did you eat yet? (AmE) vs. Have you eaten yet? (BrE).
mistakes: I have seen him yesterday → I saw him yesterday — specific past — Past Simple.
When have you arrived? → When did you arrive? — when → Past Simple.
I live here since 2010 → I have lived here since 2010 — since → Perfect.

adv_u04.text: Unit 4 - Past Continuous and Past Simple
adv_u04.exp:
Unit 4 (Advanced) — Past Continuous and Past Simple in complex narrative.

Past Continuous sets the background, Past Simple — point events: The sun was setting and birds were singing as we walked home. Interruption: I was reading when the phone rang. Parallel extended actions: While I was cooking, she was setting the table.

Stylistic functions:
  • Softening/diplomacy: I was wondering if you could help me — sounds more polite than I wonder.
  • Unrealised intention: I was going to call you, but I forgot (I was going to, but).
  • Recurring annoying actions: He was always turning up late.

Limitations: stative verbs (know, believe, understand, belong) are usually not put in Continuous, even in backgrounding use: I knew him then (not was knowing).

The choice between Simple and Continuous in the past determines the narrator's "camera": the point of the event or the duration of the process.
adv_u04.exc: Stative verbs are usually in Past Simple, even as "background": I knew him then (not was knowing).
mistakes: When I came in, she read a book (background) → she was reading a book — background action — Continuous.
I was knowing the answer → I knew the answer — stative — simple.
While I cooked, the phone rang → While I was cooking, the phone rang.

adv_u05.text: Unit 5 - Past Perfect and Past Simple
adv_u05.exp:
Unit 5 (Advanced) — Past Perfect as a means of marking precedence.

Formula: had + V3. Its purpose is to show that one past event preceded another: When I arrived, the meeting had already started.

When required: with by the time: By the time we reached the theatre, the play had ended. In reported speech with a shift from Past Simple/Present Perfect: She said she had seen him. With reversed event order (effect before cause in the text): He was exhausted. He had run ten miles.

When not needed: if after/before/when already set the order: After he left, I called a taxi — here Past Simple is sufficient.

Literary register: Past Perfect is often used in narrative fiction: She had always wanted to travel. In conversation, native speakers often manage with Past Simple if the order is clear from context.

Example: I didn't recognise her — she had changed so much (the change occurred before the non-recognition).
adv_u05.exc:
Past Perfect is not needed if the order is obvious from after, before, when: After he left, I called.

With by the time Past Perfect is required: By the time we arrived, they had gone.
mistakes: When I arrived, he left (= before me) → When I arrived, he had left — earlier — Past Perfect.
I had ate already → I had eaten already — 3rd form.
By the time she came, I finished → I had finished.

adv_u06.text: Unit 6 – Present Perfect Continuous and Present Perfect
adv_u06.note: Emphasis on process vs. on the result
adv_u06.exp:
Unit 6 (Advanced) — Present Perfect Continuous vs Present Perfect: process vs result.

PPC (have been + -ing) focuses on duration, process, recent activity: I've been painting the kitchen (perhaps not finished yet; the process itself or its traces matter). PP (have + V3) focuses on result, quantity, completion: I've painted three rooms (result).

Both possible with verbs of duration (live, work, study, teach, wait) when describing a current state: I've lived / I've been living here for ten years. With stative verbs — Perfect only: I've known her for years (not PPC).

Pragmatics: I've been trying to reach you — with an apologetic/explanatory shade. Have you been crying? — traces are visible. Someone has been using my computer — signs of interference.

Signals: for, since, all day, recently, lately — fitting with both forms; how many, twice, three times — only with Perfect (counting results). How long usually pulls toward PPC to emphasise process.
adv_u06.exc:
Stative verbs are not used in PPC: I've known him for years (not been knowing).

PPC emphasises process, Perfect — result: I've been painting (ongoing) vs. I've painted the room (done).
mistakes: I've been knowing her → I've known her — stative.
I've read three books this week (process focus) — for quantity — Perfect; for process — I've been reading.
She has been finished → She has finished.

adv_u07.text: Unit 7 - Past Perfect Continuous, Past Perfect and Past Continuous
adv_u07.note: Three past tenses in one narrative
adv_u07.exp:
Unit 7 (Advanced) — three past levels: Past Continuous, Past Perfect, Past Perfect Continuous in a coherent narrative.

Past Continuous — scene/background; Past Perfect — a completed preceding event; Past Perfect Continuous (had been + -ing) — an ongoing preceding action with a causal connection.

Typical narrative pattern: She looked exhausted. She had been working since 6 a.m. and hadn't eaten all day when the call finally came. Here PPC explains her state, Past Perfect fixes the fact "hadn't eaten", Past Simple marks the point event.

PPC vs Past Perfect: When I arrived, they had had dinner (fact: dinner completed) ↔ When I arrived, they had been having dinner (was ongoing up to the moment, possibly not yet finished). The roads were wet because it had rained (fact) ↔ because it had been raining (duration emphasised).

Restriction: stative verbs do not form PPC: I had known him, not had been knowing. Register: PPC is common in fictional and biographical prose, rarer in academic text.
adv_u07.exc:
Past Perfect Continuous is not used with stative verbs.

Past Perfect Continuous emphasises duration up to a point in the past: She had been waiting for an hour when he arrived.
mistakes: She had been knowing him → She had known him.
He was tired because he ran → he had been running — cause up to the moment — PPC.
When I came, she had been leaving (action done) → she had left.

adv_u08.text: Unit 8 - Present and Past time: overview
adv_u08.note: Systematic review of the present and past
adv_u08.exp:
Unit 8 (Advanced) — review of present and past tenses: systematic choice.

The decision is made along four axes:
  1. Moment/period: point → Simple; duration → Continuous.
  2. Completion: completed with present relevance → Perfect; detached from the present → Past Simple.
  3. Process vs result: Continuous vs Perfect.
  4. Connection with the present: yes → Perfect; no → Past.
These axes intersect: Present Perfect Continuous = period + process + connection.

Register deviations:
  • Media headlines often replace Perfect with Simple: Plane crashes in storm.
  • Legal/official style prefers Perfect: The defendant has admitted…
  • In fictional narrative, past dominates; Present Perfect appears in dialogue/inner speech.

Typical pitfalls: since/for require Perfect; ago/a specific date require Past Simple; when-questions about an exact moment — Past Simple (When did you arrive?).

Summary rule: Perfect is a "bridge" between past and present; Past Simple is "closed" past; Continuous in any zone is "camera zooming in on process".
adv_u08.exc: In news headlines, Past Simple often replaces Present Perfect: Plane crashes in storm.
mistakes: I am here since morning → I have been here since morning — since → Perfect.
She has left an hour ago → She left an hour ago — ago → Past Simple.
Did you ever be there? → Have you ever been there?

adv_u09.text: Unit 9 – Will and be going to
adv_u09.note: Subtleties of choosing between will and be going to
adv_u09.exp:
Unit 9 (Advanced) — will vs be going to: beyond the basic rule.

The basic contrast — "spontaneous vs planned" — is only one axis. At C1 the following are important:
  • Type of evidence for the prediction: be going to relies on visible signs (Look at those clouds — it's going to rain); will — on general knowledge/experience/inference (It will probably rain — it usually does in April).
  • Plans: be going to — intention formed before the moment of speech; will — a decision made right now: — The phone's ringing. — I'll get it.
  • Register: will is more neutral in formal/written; be going to is colloquial, especially in AmE.
  • Promises, offers, threats, refusals: only will/won't: I'll help you. I won't tell anyone.

Subtleties: was/were going to often conveys an unrealised intention: I was going to call, but I forgot. There will be for a forecast (There will be rain tomorrow), but there is going to be for an expected event (There's going to be trouble).
adv_u09.exc:
will for spontaneous decisions and predictions; be going to for plans and visible signs.

Both can predict, but going to relies on current facts: Look at those clouds — it's going to rain.
mistakes: I will meet her tomorrow (plan) → I'm going to meet her / I'm meeting her — plan.
The phone rings — I'm going to get it → I'll get it — spontaneous.
Look, it will rain → it's going to rain — signs.

adv_u10.text: Unit 10 – Present Simple and Continuous for the future
adv_u10.exp:
Unit 10 (Advanced) — present tenses for the future.

Present Simple for the future — only for schedules, programmes, fixed dates, which are outside the speaker's control: The flight leaves at 7.45. The conference begins on Monday. The film starts at 8. Also in time/condition subordinate clauses: When he arrives, I'll tell him; If it rains, we'll stay home.

Present Continuous for the future — for personal arrangements with a specific set-up (a call, booking, invitation): I'm meeting Ann at 6. We're having dinner with the Smiths on Friday. An arrangement/planning is required.

Shades of fixedness: The train leaves at 5 (schedule — almost a law) > I'm leaving at 5 (firm intention/arrangement) > I'm going to leave at 5 (intention, but less formalised) > I'll leave at 5 (decision/promise right now).

Restriction: Present Continuous for the future is unsuitable for predictions about weather/nature: NOT It's raining tomorrow, but It's going to rain / It will rain. For public transport schedules Continuous is incorrect: The train leaves at 6, not is leaving.
adv_u10.exc:
Present Simple for the future — only for schedules: The train leaves at 6.

Present Continuous — for personal arrangements: I'm meeting Tom at 5.
mistakes: I meet Tom tomorrow (arranged) → I'm meeting Tom tomorrow — arrangement — Continuous.
The film is starting at 8 (schedule) → The film starts at 8 — schedule — Simple.
When will he comes? → When does he come? / When is he coming?

adv_u100.note: Such was / So great was / Hardly had I…
adv_u100.exp:
Unit 100 — inversion (lesson 2): so/such, hardly, no sooner.

Phenomenon: a second set of inverted constructions — extremely formal, often with a literary tinge.

Patterns:
  • So + adj/adv + be/aux + subj + that-clause: So great was his anger that he left.
  • Such + be + subj (+ that): Such was the force of the explosion that windows shattered.
  • Hardly/Scarcely + had + subj + V3 + when…: Hardly had I arrived when the phone rang.
  • No sooner + had + subj + V3 + than…: No sooner had she spoken than he replied.
  • Inversion in conditionals: Had I known… Were I you… Should you need… (see Unit 85).
Critical pairs: Hardly/Scarcely … when (not than); No sooner … than (not when).

Register: literary narration, official documents, oratorical speech.

Contrast: the neutral equivalent — As soon as I arrived, the phone rang. The inverted version dramatises the sequence of events.
mistakes: No sooner he had left when it rained. → No sooner had he left than it rained.
Hardly I had arrived than → Hardly had I arrived when…
Such was a his anger → Such was his anger that… — no article.

adv_u11.text: Unit 11 - Future Continuous and Future Perfect (Continuous)
adv_u11.note: I'll be working / I'll have finished / I'll have been working
adv_u11.exp:
Unit 11 (Advanced) — Future Continuous, Future Perfect and Future Perfect Continuous.

Future Continuous (will be + -ing): a process at a given point in the future (This time tomorrow I'll be flying to Tokyo); a planned/expected action in the course of events (I'll be seeing her later — "I'll see her" as part of the natural flow); a polite question about plans without pressure (Will you be using the car tomorrow? — less pushy than Do you need the car?).

Future Perfect (will have + V3): an action will be completed by a point in the future. Markers: by + time, by the time + clause. By 2030 I'll have graduated. By the time you arrive, we'll have finished dinner.

Future Perfect Continuous (will have been + -ing): a process will be ongoing up to a point in the future. In June, I'll have been working here for ten years. Length is emphasised.

Register and restrictions: Future Perfect Continuous — a relatively rare form, usual in biographical and anniversary contexts. Stative verbs don't allow Continuous: I will have known him for 20 years, not will have been knowing. Future Continuous reduces pressure in requests — an important pragmatic move in business correspondence.
adv_u11.exc:
Future Perfect — usually with by + time: By 2030 I'll have graduated.

Future Continuous can mean "a planned/expected action in the future": I'll be seeing him tomorrow.
mistakes: At 5 tomorrow I will work → I will be working — process at a specific moment.
By next year I will finish → I will have finished — by a moment — Perfect.
I will have been knowing → I will have known — stative.

adv_u12.note: The Prime Minister is to visit France.
adv_u12.exp:
Unit 12 (Advanced) — the construction be to + infinitive.

Formula: am/is/are/was/were + to + V. Primarily written, formal register (newspapers, official announcements, diplomacy, instructions).

Meanings:
  • Official plan/arrangement: The Prime Minister is to visit Japan next week.
  • Order, instruction: You are to report to security by 9 a.m.
  • Prohibition: No one is to enter the room.
  • Condition (in if-clauses): If we are to succeed, we must act now.
  • Fate (literary): He was to become a great artist.

Past: was/were to + V can mean both a realised intention (was to and did) and an unrealised one (was going to, but). For "should have done, but didn't" use was/were to have + V3: He was to have arrived yesterday (was to, but did not arrive).

In conversational speech this form is almost never encountered — its niche is official style.
adv_u12.exc:
be to + inf is used in news and official contexts: The President is to visit Japan.

Also in conditionals: If we are to succeed, we must....
mistakes: I am to meet my friend (personal plan) → I'm meeting my friend — be to is not for personal plans.
The PM is visit France → The PM is to visit France — to required.
He was to died young → He was to die young — infinitive without -ed.

adv_u13.text: Unit 13 - Other ways of expressing the future
adv_u13.note: be about to / be due to / be bound to
adv_u13.exp:
Unit 13 (Advanced) — additional means of expressing the future.

Be about to + inf — "on the verge": The train is about to leave. Often with just: I was just about to call you. Very near future, without calendar anchoring.

Be due to + inf / be due + time — "on schedule, expected": The flight is due to arrive at 18.00; The book is due out in May. Neutral-official register.

Be bound to + inf — "surely, inevitably": She's bound to win — she's the best. Confident prediction.

Be likely/unlikely to + inf — probability: It's likely to rain tonight. Alternative: It's likely that it will rain.

Be on the point/verge/brink of + -ing — on the brink of: The company is on the verge of bankruptcy.

Be set to + inf — ready/due (newspaper style): Prices are set to rise.

These constructions enrich the repertoire and differ in degree of certainty and temporal proximity: be about to (immediately) → be due to (on schedule) → be likely to (probably) → be bound to (almost certainly).
adv_u13.exc: be about to = on the verge; be due to = on schedule; be bound to = sure to happen; be on the verge/point of + -ing.
mistakes: I'm about to leaving → I'm about to leave — infinitive.
The train is due arrive → The train is due to arrive — to is required.
He is bound fail → He is bound to fail.

adv_u14.note: She said she would come. He was going to resign.
adv_u14.exp:
Unit 14 (Advanced) — Future in the Past: looking from the past into the future.

When the narrative is led in the past but a future relative to that point must be indicated, special forms are used.

Would + V — "was destined/would turn out/happened later": In 1920 she published her first novel. It would become a bestseller.

Was/were going to + V — intention in the past, often unrealised: He was going to apologise, but he changed his mind.

Was/were to + V — fateful, predetermined in advance: He was to become president ten years later. The perfect form was to have + V3 emphasises non-realisation: The meeting was to have taken place on Monday, but was cancelled.

Past Continuous — for planned events: He was leaving the next day.

Was about to + V — was on the verge: She was about to speak when the door opened.

The key area of application is reported speech (He said he would come), biographical narratives and fiction. Future in the Past distinguishes a mature narrator able to project a future perspective into the past.
adv_u14.exc:
Future in the Past: would, was/were going to, Past Continuous, was/were to.

was going to often = was planning but didn't do: I was going to call, but forgot.
mistakes: He said he will come → He said he would come — shift.
I was going call → I was going to call — to.
She was to died → She was to die.

adv_u15.text: Unit 15 – Can, could, be able to, be allowed to
adv_u15.exp:
Unit 15 (Advanced) — ability and permission: can, could, be able to, be allowed to, manage to.

Ability in the past: could — general ability overall (She could read at the age of four). For a single specific successful action prefer was/were able to, managed to, succeeded in -ing: The door was locked, but I managed to open it (NOT could open). Exception — negatives and with verbs of perception (see, hear, smell, understand, remember): I couldn't see him in the dark — perfectly correct.

Permission: can — neutral-conversational; may — formal-polite; be allowed to — emphasises an external rule (You're not allowed to smoke here).

Epistemic vs deontic: can simultaneously denotes ability ("I can"), permission ("it is allowed"), possibility ("it happens": It can be cold here in winter). Could also expresses mild probability (That could be true).

Register: be able to is indispensable where modals don't work: before another modal (I'd like to be able to swim), in an infinitive/gerund (being able to drive), in the perfect (I've been able to solve it).
adv_u15.exc:
could — general ability in the past; for a single successful action — was/were able to / managed to: I was able to escape (not could escape).

But in negatives/with perception — couldn't ok: I couldn't see him.
mistakes: Yesterday I could win the match → I was able to win / managed to win — specific event.
He is allowed entering → He is allowed to enter — to.
I can to swim → I can swim — no to.

adv_u16.text: Unit 16 – Will, would and used to
adv_u16.exp:
Unit 16 (Advanced) — will, would, used to for habits and typical patterns.

Will (present) — characteristic, typical behaviour or property: Oil will float on water. He'll sit for hours, staring out of the window. With stress on will it can express irritation: He WILL keep interrupting me!

Would (past) — repeated actions in the past, narrative typical episodes: Every summer we would go to the seaside. She would always bring me flowers. An important restriction: would does not combine with stative meanings: it's impossible to say *I would have a red bike (state of possession); for such cases — used to.

Used to + inf — both actions and states in the past that are no longer true: I used to live in Oxford. She used to have long hair. Contrast with the present is built into the meaning.

Register: narrative would is typical of fiction and memoir prose, lending rhythm to reminiscences. Used to is neutral. Would always/forever + V is often tinted with nostalgia or slight irritation.
adv_u16.exc: used to — past only; would — repeated actions in the past, but not states: would visit ok, but would have a car.
mistakes: I would live in Paris (state) → I used to live in Paris — states — used to.
He use to smoke → He used to smoke.
Did he used to? → Did he use to? — after did — use.

adv_u17.exp:
Unit 17 (Advanced) — may and might: probability and permission.

Epistemic meaning (probability): a graded scale of certainty: must (certain) > will (strong assumption) > should (expect) > may (possibly, ~50%) > might/could (maybe, lower). She may be in her office. He might come later.

Past: may/might have + V3 — "possibly did": She may have left already. Might have done also expresses reproach/unrealised possibility: You might have told me! — you could have told me!

Deontic meaning (permission): May I come in? — formally polite. May in permissive rules: Students may use the library until 10. Might in this meaning is rare and archaic.

Differences may vs might: might often sounds less confident and more hypothetical; in conditional contexts might is almost obligatory: If you asked him, he might help. In a neutral statement both forms are interchangeable. May not = possibly not; might not — similarly, shade of less probability.
adv_u17.exc:
may/might have + V3 = possibly did in the past.

might often sounds less confident than may.

In formal requests may I is more polite than can I.
mistakes: He may to come → He may come — no to.
She might is right → She might be right — infinitive.
He may came → He may have come — for past.

adv_u18.note: Internal vs. external coercion on C1
adv_u18.exp:
Unit 18 (Advanced) — must and have (got) to: obligation and inference.

Deontic (obligation): must — subjective, internal, or the speaker as authority: I must call my mother (decided myself); Students must wear uniforms (rule from speaker/institution). Have to / have got to — external obligation, rule from outside: I have to work on Saturdays (the boss demands). In AmE have to displaces must in almost all contexts.

Past: must has no past form in deontic use — had to is used: I had to stay late yesterday.

Epistemic (inference): must expresses a strong assumption: He must be tired — he's been working all day. Past: must have + V3: She must have forgotten.

Negation — a key pitfall:
  • mustn't = prohibition ("must not"): You mustn't smoke here.
  • don't have to = absence of necessity ("don't have to"): You don't have to come.
  • can't — negation of epistemic must: He can't be serious (= it can't be).

Register: must is more formal, written; have to is neutral-conversational; have got to is conversational.
adv_u18.exc:
must — internal conviction; have to — external obligation.

Negation: mustn't = prohibition; don't have to = no necessity.
mistakes: You mustn't come (= no need) → You don't have to come — no necessity.
I must to go → I must go.
I must called her yesterday → I had to call — past obligation — had to.

adv_u19.text: Unit 19 - Needn't, don't need to and don't have to
adv_u19.exp:
Unit 19 (Advanced) — three ways to say "don't need": needn't, don't need to, don't have to.

Needn't + V (modal, without to) — absence of necessity from the speaker's viewpoint: You needn't wait — I'll catch up. More common in BrE, formal.

Don't need to + V (lexical) — absence of necessity as a fact: You don't need to book in advance. Neutral.

Don't have to + V — absence of obligation: You don't have to answer if you don't want to. The most frequent in speech.

Key difference in the past:
  • Needn't have + V3 = did in vain: You needn't have cooked — we've eaten (the food was prepared but unnecessarily).
  • Didn't need to + V = there was no necessity (usually didn't do it either): I didn't need to go to the bank (and didn't go).
Context is often needed to understand whether the action was performed.

Pragmatics: needn't have carries a shade of "a pity you did this, it was superfluous"; this is a subtle point, important in essays and dialogues.
adv_u19.exc: needn't + V = not obliged; needn't have + V3 = did in vain; didn't need to + V = there was no need (and usually didn't do).
mistakes: You needn't to go → You needn't go — needn't without to.
I needn't went → I needn't have gone — past — have V3.
She doesn't need go → She doesn't need to go — don't need + to.

adv_u20.text: Unit 20 – Should, ought to and had better
adv_u20.exp:
Unit 20 (Advanced) — should, ought to, had better: advice and recommendations.

Should / ought to — almost synonyms, weak moral or rational obligation: You should/ought to see a doctor. Ought to is slightly more formal and more often refers to an objective norm; should is more universal. Negation: shouldn't / ought not to. Questions with ought to are rare — almost always should.

Had better (+ V, without to) — urgent advice in a specific situation, often with a hint of negative consequences: You'd better leave now, or you'll miss the train. Negation: had better not: You'd better not be late. This is NOT a general norm, but an ad hoc recommendation.

Past (reproach):
  • should/ought to have + V3 = should have done, but didn't: You should have told me.
  • shouldn't/ought not to have + V3 = shouldn't have, but did: I shouldn't have eaten so much.

Register: ought to feels slightly more archaic in AmE; in BrE alive. Had better — colloquial, with a shade of urgency, unsuitable for neutral polite advice (for that use You might want to / It might be a good idea to).
adv_u20.exc:
had better — urgent advice, often with a threat; should — neutral advice.

ought to = should (a bit more formal); in negation: ought not to.
mistakes: You had better to go → You had better go — no to.
I would better go → I had better go — had, not would.
You should to call → You should call.

adv_u21.text: Unit 21 - Linking verbs: be, appear, seem, become, get
adv_u21.note: She seems tired. Didn't become angry. It got dark.
adv_u21.exp:
Unit 21 (Advanced) — linking (copular) verbs.

A linking verb connects the subject with the nominal part of the predicate (adjective or noun), which describes the subject rather than an action. After a linking verb an adjective is placed, not an adverb.

Static links: be, seem, appear, look, feel, sound, smell, taste, remain, stay, keep: She seems happy. This cheese tastes odd. He remained silent.

Change-of-state links:
  • become — universal, often with a noun/adjective: He became a doctor.
  • get — colloquial, with an adjective: It's getting dark; I got tired.
  • grow — gradual: grow old, grow tired.
  • turn — sudden/colour: The leaves turned yellow; He turned pale.
  • go — negative/unnatural: go bad, go blind, go mad.
  • come — positive outcome: dreams come true, wishes come true.
  • fall — sudden: fall ill, fall silent, fall asleep.

Context: look good (linking, looks good) ↔ look carefully (action, look attentively). The same verbs can be both linking and lexical — the distinction is by syntactic position.
adv_u21.exc:
After linking verbs — an adjective, not an adverb: She looks tired, not tiredly.

get often = become: It's getting dark.
mistakes: She seems tiredly → She seems tired — adjective.
He became to angry → He became angry — no to.
The milk turned to sour → The milk turned sour.

adv_u22.note: Passive voice: functions and selections on the C1
adv_u22.exp:
Unit 22 (Advanced) — passive voice: functions and choice.

Formula: be + V3 in all tenses. Three discourse reasons to choose the passive:
  1. Agent unknown/unimportant: My bike has been stolen.
  2. Agent obvious or generalised: She was arrested yesterday (by the police, of course).
  3. Information focus: the sentence topic is the object of the action: The Sistine Chapel was painted by Michelangelo.

Academic and official register actively uses the passive for impersonality: The experiment was conducted under controlled conditions.

Get-passive — a colloquial variant, often with a shade of surprise or negativity: She got fired last week; We got caught in the rain. With get "accidental/emotional" events more often appear.

Limitations: intransitive verbs don't form passive (happen, arrive, die, come, go, fall): impossible *It was happened. Transitive "states of possession" verbs (have, lack, possess, fit, suit, resemble) are also usually without passive.

By vs with: by — agent (written by Dickens); with — instrument (cut with a knife); filled with (filled with sth) — a special case.
adv_u22.exc:
Not all verbs form passive (e.g. have meaning "own", fit, suit).

by — agent; with — instrument: cut by him with a knife.
mistakes: The book was wrote → was written — V3.
It was happened → It happened — happen can't be passivised.
He was died → He died — die can't be passivised.

adv_u23.note: Being done / to be done / having been done
adv_u23.exp:
Unit 23 (Advanced) — passive non-finite forms.

Passive infinitive — (to) be + V3: The report needs to be finished by Monday. This problem has to be solved. Typical after modals and verbs like need, want, have.

Passive gerund — being + V3: She hates being criticised. He remembers being praised as a child. Used when the implicit subject is the object of the action.

Perfect passive forms:
  • Perfect passive infinitive: to have been + V3: He claims to have been robbed.
  • Perfect passive gerund: having been + V3: Having been warned, she was careful.

Passive participle (Past Participle) is used as a reduced subordinate clause: The letter written yesterday is on the desk (= which was written); Being followed, she walked faster (= because she was being followed).

Register: passive -ing and infinitive forms are typical of formal/academic text. Conversational style often replaces them with active voice or a subordinate clause: After I was told → Having been told / After being told. Command of these forms is a C1+ sign.
adv_u23.exc: Passive gerund: being done. Passive infinitive: to be done. Perfect passive gerund: having been done.
mistakes: I hate be criticized → I hate being criticized — -ing.
This needs to done → This needs to be done.
After been told → After being told / Having been told.

adv_u24.text: Unit 24 - Different ways to use Passive
adv_u24.note: Passive with two objects and other advanced cases
adv_u24.exp:
Unit 24 (Advanced) — non-standard passive constructions.

Passive with two objects. Verbs give, send, offer, tell, show, teach, pay, lend, promise take two objects → two passive forms: She gave me a present → I was given a present (by her) / A present was given to me. The form with a person as subject is usually preferable in conversation — it is more topical.

Exception verbs: explain, describe, suggest, mention, announce, say do not allow a personal subject in the passive: The rule was explained to me, NOT *I was explained the rule.

Impersonal passive with it: It is said/believed/thought that… — typical of news and academic style: It was decided that the meeting would be postponed. Alternative — personal passive (see Unit 25).

Get-passive: colloquial, often with negative connotations: He got sacked; We got soaked.

Pseudo-passive / descriptive passive: state must be distinguished from action: The door was open (state) ↔ The door was opened by Tom (action). An adjective after be is not passive, though outwardly similar.

Causative have/get: I had/got my hair cut — "I had my hair cut", a passive idea with active form.
adv_u24.exc: Verbs with two objects can have two passives: She was given a book / A book was given to her. The first (with a person as subject) is preferred.
mistakes: A book was given her → A book was given to her — to needed.
I was explained the rule → The rule was explained to me — explain doesn't allow person-subject.
He was said that… → He was told that… / It was said that… — say ≠ tell.

adv_u25.text: Unit 25 – Reporting with Passive: It is said that…
adv_u25.note: It is believed / said / reported / thought that…
adv_u25.exp:
Unit 25 (Advanced) — reporting with the passive: It is said that… / He is said to…

For impersonal reporting of opinions and information — in news, academic texts, reviews — two synonymous constructions are used:
  1. It + be + V3 + that-clause: It is said that she's the best in her field.
  2. Subject + be + V3 + to-infinitive: She is said to be the best in her field.
Both carry the meaning "it is generally known/asserted that…", without naming the source.

Verbs of this type: say, believe, think, consider, know, report, claim, allege, suppose, understand, expect, find, rumour.

Infinitive tenses:
  • Present/general: He is said to live in Paris (lives now).
  • Past: to have + V3: He is said to have lived in Paris (lived earlier).
  • Duration: to be + -ing: He is said to be living in Paris (lives now, ongoing).
  • Perfect continuous: to have been + -ing: He is said to have been living there for ten years.

Register and distance: the construction signals caution and detachment — the author does not confirm but retells. Indispensable in journalism: The suspect is alleged to have entered through the window.
adv_u25.exc:
Two structures: It is said that he is rich = He is said to be rich.

For the past: He is said to have left.
mistakes: He is said that he is rich → He is said to be rich / It is said that he is rich.
He is said to left → He is said to have left — past — have V3.
It said that… → It is said that….

adv_u26.text: Unit 26 – Wh-questions: who, whom, which, how, whose
adv_u26.note: Complex questions with prepositions and who/whom choices
adv_u26.exp:
Unit 26 (Advanced) — complex wh-questions: who/whom, which, how, whose, what.

Who vs whom: in modern English who dominates; whom is strictly formal, required only immediately after a preposition: To whom should I address the complaint? In conversation the preposition moves to the end: Who should I address the complaint to?

Which vs what: which — choice from a limited set (Which of these books do you prefer?); what — open question without a set (What do you want to read?).

Whose — question about possession: Whose bag is this?

How + adj/adv for degree: How tall is he? How often do you go there? How long have you lived here?

Indirect/embedded questions use direct word order, without do/does/did: Do you know where he lives? (NOT where does he live). This rule is often broken even by advanced learners.

What…like vs How: What is she like? — what is her character? How is she? — how is she (feeling)? What does she look like? — what does she look like? How does it work? — how does it work?

Pragmatics: whom in written complaints and formal addresses preserves old-fashioned authority. In email correspondence who is more common.
adv_u26.exc:
whom — formal objective case; in informal speech — who: Who did you see?.

Preposition at the end (informal) or before whom (formal): To whom did you speak?.
mistakes: Who did you give it? → Who(m) did you give it to? — preposition needed.
Whose is this car yours? → Is this car yours? / Whose car is this?.
How is she like? → What is she like? — character — what.

adv_u27.text: Unit 27 - Negative questions; echo questions; questions with chat-clauses
adv_u27.note: Isn't she lovely? She's leaving, is she?
adv_u27.exp:
Unit 27 (Advanced) — non-standard question types.

Negative questions (Isn't she lovely? Didn't you know?) express not a request for information, but an emotion: surprise, annoyance, confirmation. Haven't we met before? — it seems we've met? Why didn't you tell me? — a reproach. In exclamatory intonation: Isn't it beautiful! — how beautiful!

Echo questions repeat part of the preceding utterance with a rising intonation, expressing surprise or a request to repeat: — I'm moving to Tokyo. — You're moving WHERE? In the question — the usual word order of a statement with emphatic stress.

Declarative questions — a form of statement, but with rising intonation and a question function: You've finished already? They express surprise or a request for confirmation.

Tag questions: confirmation of an expected answer. Rules: positive statement + negative tag (She's Russian, isn't she?); negative + positive (You don't smoke, do you?). Special cases: I am → aren't I?; Let's → shall we?; imperative → will/would/can you?

Pragmatics: a falling intonation on the tag — "I know, confirm it"; rising — "is it really so?".
adv_u27.exc:
Negative questions express surprise/emotion: Isn't she lovely!.

Tag questions: positive → neg tag, neg → pos tag.
mistakes: You like it, don't you like? → …, don't you? — short form.
He is tired, isn't it? → …, isn't he? — pronoun matches the subject.
Let's go, don't we? → Let's go, shall we?.

adv_u28.text: Unit 28 - Different types of objects after verbs
adv_u28.exp:
Unit 28 (Advanced) — complements after verbs: types of complementation.

Verbs select the structure of their complement. Main patterns:
  • Verb + object + noun (object complement): They elected him president; We call him Bob.
  • Verb + object + adjective: She painted the door red; I find this book fascinating; He drove me crazy.
  • Verb + object + infinitive (with to): She asked him to help; I want you to listen.
  • Verb + object + bare infinitive (after make, let, have + verbs of perception): Let me go; I saw him leave.
  • Verb + object + -ing: I heard her singing; I saw them running.
  • Verb + object + past participle: I want it done by noon; She had her car repaired.

Difference full vs partial perception: I saw him cross the street (saw the whole thing) vs I saw him crossing the street (saw him in process).

Semantics of the "object predicative": elect, appoint, name, call, consider, find, declare, prove, keep, leave + object + complement form a "small sentence" within the main one: I consider him [a friend] / [honest] / [to be reliable].

Register: consider/find sb to be + adj is more formal than without to be.
adv_u28.exc: Some verbs require object + complement: elect him president, paint it red, consider him a friend.
mistakes: They elected him as president (in formal BrE ok, but often) → They elected him president — without as.
She painted the wall in red → She painted the wall red.
I find him as rude → I find him rude.

adv_u29.note: She gave me a book. / She gave a book to me.
adv_u29.exp:
Unit 29 (Advanced) — verbs with two objects (ditransitive).

Two objects are governed by verbs of giving, transfer, information: give, send, offer, show, teach, tell, lend, buy, make, cook, get, pay, promise.

Two syntactic patterns:
  1. Verb + Indirect Object (Recipient) + Direct Object (Thing): I gave Tom the book.
  2. Verb + Direct Object + to/for + Recipient: I gave the book to Tom.

To vs for: to with give, send, tell, show, pay, offer, lend, promise, hand, pass, read; for with buy, get, make, cook, find, order, save, leave (recipient is a beneficiary, not a recipient proper).

Information structure: order choice is tied to topic/focus: new information usually at the end. I gave Tom the book — new = the book. I gave the book to Tom — new = Tom. With pronouns the second order is often required: Give it to me (rather than *give me it in AmE).

Exception verbs — only the to-pattern: explain, describe, suggest, announce, mention, introduce, admit, confess, report. Impossible *explain me the rule — only explain the rule to me. Suggest requires a that-clause or -ing: She suggested that I go / She suggested going.
adv_u29.exc: explain, describe, suggest do not take a person-object: explain it to me, not explain me it.
mistakes: He explained me the rule → He explained the rule to me.
She suggested me to go → She suggested (that) I (should) go.
Give to me the book → Give me the book / Give the book to me.

adv_u30.note: Remember / understand / decide: what form and why
adv_u30.exp:
Unit 30 (Advanced) — verb + -ing or + to-infinitive: systematic overview.

Gerund only (-ing): enjoy, avoid, finish, mind, miss, practise, suggest, consider, deny, admit, delay, postpone, resist, risk, imagine, involve, keep, fancy, can't help, can't stand, give up, put off, feel like. I enjoy reading; She suggested going; We can't afford to waste time.

Infinitive only (to + V): want, hope, decide, plan, expect, promise, refuse, offer, manage, afford, agree, fail, seem, appear, tend, pretend, intend, threaten, choose, learn, deserve. I hope to see you soon.

Both forms with different meanings:
  • remember/forget + -ing = past memory; + to V = future obligation: I remember locking the door vs Remember to lock the door.
  • stop + -ing = cease a process; + to V = stop in order to: He stopped smoking vs He stopped to smoke.
  • regret + -ing = regret a past action; + to inform/say/tell = formally report with regret: We regret to inform you…
  • go on + -ing = continue the same; + to V = move on to something else: He went on talking vs He went on to become a minister.

Both forms without meaning difference: begin, start, continue, bother, love, like, hate, prefer (with slight shades: -ing general pleasure, to V specific intention).
adv_u30.exc:
remember/forget/regret + -ing — past; + to — future/obligation.

stop + -ing — cease; + to — stop in order to.
mistakes: I remember to lock the door (I did it) → I remember locking it — past — -ing.
He stopped to smoke (= quit) → He stopped smoking — gave up.
I regret to tell him yesterday → I regret telling him.

adv_u31.note: begin doing/to do: cases without difference and with difference
adv_u31.exp:
Unit 31 (Advanced) — gerund vs infinitive, special constructions.

Verbs of perception + object + bare inf / -ing:
  • see/hear/watch/feel/notice + sb + V — completed event: I saw her cross the street (saw the whole crossing).
  • + sb + -ing — in process: I saw her crossing the street (saw during the crossing).

Need/want/require/deserve + -ing with passive meaning: The car needs servicing = needs to be serviced; This shirt wants ironing. The -ing and passive infinitive forms are interchangeable.

Idiomatic phrases after -ing: It's worth + -ing; It's no use / no good + -ing; There's no point (in) + -ing; Have difficulty/trouble + -ing; Spend/waste time + -ing; Be busy + -ing.

Try to V vs try -ing: try to V = attempt, make an effort; try -ing = try as an experiment: Try to open the door (try hard to open) vs Try opening it (try it, maybe it'll open).

Mean to V vs mean -ing: mean to = intend (I meant to call you); mean -ing = involve, entail (Being a parent means making sacrifices).

Be used to + -ing (be accustomed) vs used to + V (used to do) — entirely different constructions, a frequent error.
adv_u31.exc:
No difference: begin/start/continue + -ing / to V.

With difference: try to (make an effort) vs. try -ing (try as experiment); mean to (intend) vs. mean -ing (entail).
mistakes: I tried to open the window (= experimented) → I tried opening the window — experiment.
It means to wait → It means waiting — entails.
I didn't mean hurting you → I didn't mean to hurt you.

adv_u32.exp:
Unit 32 (Advanced) — reported speech: full overview.

Backshift when the reporting verb is in the past: Present → Past, Past → Past Perfect, Present Perfect → Past Perfect, will → would, can → could, may → might, must → had to/must (remains). «I am tired,» she said → She said (that) she was tired.

Backshift is optional if the statement is still true: He said the Earth is round. Also if the reporting verb is in the Present: She says she is tired.

Deixis switches: this → that, here → there, now → then, today → that day, tomorrow → the next day/the following day, yesterday → the day before, last week → the week before, next month → the following month. Pronouns — by context: «I love my job,» → He said he loved his job.

Say vs tell: tell requires an addressee (She told me (that)…); say — without addressee or with to + sb: She said to me.

That is often omitted in speech: She said she was tired.

Register: in academic and journalistic writing reporting verbs are chosen consciously: claim, allege, argue, suggest, admit, deny, insist, maintain — each carries an evaluative shade.
adv_u32.exc:
Present → Past, will → would, can → could, must → had to.

Shift is not required if the fact remains true: She said the Earth is round.
mistakes: He said he is tired → He said he was tired — tense shift.
She told that she left → She said (that) she had left / She told me that… — tell requires object.
He asked where do I live → He asked where I lived — direct word order.

adv_u33.note: Questions, commands, exclamations in indirect speech
adv_u33.exp:
Unit 33 (Advanced) — reported questions, commands, exclamations.

Reported wh-questions: direct word order, without auxiliary do/does/did, without question mark: «Where do you live?» → She asked (me) where I lived.

Reported yes/no questions: introduced by if or whether: «Are you coming?» → He asked if/whether I was coming. Whether is more formal; required with or not at the end: I don't know whether or not to go.

Commands and requests: tell/ask/order/warn/advise/beg/urge + sb + (not) to V: «Don't be late!» → She told me not to be late; «Please help me,» → He asked me to help.

Offers and advice: suggest + -ing / suggest that + clause; recommend + -ing / + that; propose + -ing / + that. She suggested going for a walk / She suggested (that) we go for a walk. The subjunctive form that + base V is preserved in formal AmE: He insisted that she be there.

Exclamations: exclaim, cry, shout + that: «What a mess!» → She exclaimed that it was a mess.

Pragmatics: choice of reporting verb conveys the tone of the original utterance: snap, mutter, whisper, protest, concede — not just "said", but with manner description.
adv_u33.exc:
Questions in reported: no inversion and no do/does/did: She asked if I was ready.

Commands: tell/ask sb to do.
mistakes: She asked me do I want tea → She asked if I wanted tea.
He told me don't go → He told me not to go.
She said me to sit → She told me to sit.

adv_u34.note: I wonder what she meant. The question is who…
adv_u34.exp:
Unit 34 (Advanced) — wh-clauses as nominal elements of the sentence.

A wh-clause (what, who, where, when, why, how, whether, which, whose + clause) functions as a noun and occupies its position.

As object: I don't know what he wants; Tell me where you've been; She wondered how he had managed it.

As subject: What she said surprised everyone; How they found us remains a mystery. Typical of formal register.

After prepositions: It depends on what you want; We argued about why it happened.

After nouns (after question, issue, fact, reason, idea): The question of who should lead is unresolved; I have no idea why she left.

With it-extraposition: It is unclear whether she'll come; It's amazing how fast he learns. This technique is common in academic texts to move a long clause to the end.

Key rule — direct word order inside the clause: I don't know where he lives (NOT *where does he live). A slip in this rule gives the learner away.

Whether vs if: whether is universal (subject, after preposition, before to V); if — only as object: I don't know if/whether he'll come, but only Whether he comes is uncertain.
adv_u34.exc: In wh-clauses word order is affirmative: I wonder what she meant (not what did she mean).
mistakes: I don't know where is he → I don't know where he is.
Tell me what did you do → Tell me what you did.
The question is who will come? (embedded) → The question is who will come.

adv_u35.text: Unit 35 – Reporting and alternation of tenses
adv_u35.note: When to shift time and when not to
adv_u35.exp:
Unit 35 (Advanced) — choosing tenses in reported speech: when to shift, when not to.

Backshift is required when the reporting verb is in the past and the content is tied to that moment or may no longer be true: «I'm busy,» she said → She said she was busy (perhaps no longer busy now).

Shift is optional if:
  • The information is still relevant: She said she is/was pregnant (still pregnant).
  • It's a general truth/permanent fact: He said water boils at 100°C.
  • The reporting verb is in Present/Present Perfect/Future: She says/has said/will say she is tired.
  • A small time gap between event and retelling.

Shift is impossible with Past Perfect (nowhere further to shift) and with modals would, could, might, should, ought to, had better, used to — they remain.

Subtle semantic nuance: He said he works in London (signalling: still the case) vs He said he worked in London (neutral, without comment on current situation). The choice of form is the author's stance of the retelling regarding truth.

Register: in news and academic texts strict backshift is more common; in conversation speakers freely use the present for fresh, lively information.
adv_u35.exc: Shift is not required: if it's still relevant, a general truth, or the reporting verb is in the Present: He says he is tired.
mistakes: She says she was tired (still true, present reporting) → She says she is tired.
He told me water boiled at 100° → …water boils at 100° — general truth.
I thought she is smart → I thought she was smart — past reporting — shift.

adv_u36.note: She suggested going / She proposed that we go
adv_u36.exp:
Unit 36 — reporting suggestions, orders and intentions.

Phenomenon: conveying the wishes and plans of a third party through special verbs with different complements.

Structures:
  • suggest/recommend/propose + -ing or + that-clause (often with subjunctive/should): She suggested going / She suggested (that) we go.
  • order/tell/urge/advise/warn/beg + obj + to-inf: He urged us to reconsider.
  • say/claim + to-inf for intentions: She said she intended to resign.
Register: a that-clause is more formal than the gerund. Recommend, advise allow both options. Contrast: suggest NEVER takes obj + to-inf (unlike tell, advise). At C1 it is important to see the difference between advise sb to do (a direct instruction to a person) and advise doing (a general recommendation).
adv_u36.exc: Suggest does not accept the to + inf construction with an object: suggest me to go → suggest (that) I go / suggest going.
mistakes: She suggested me to leave. → She suggested (that) I leave / suggested leaving. — suggest + ing or that-clause.
He proposed me a plan. → He proposed a plan to me. — propose does not allow a double object.
They ordered that he leaves. → They ordered that he leave / to leave. — after order use subjunctive or infinitive.

adv_u37.note: She said she could / would / might / should
adv_u37.exp:
Unit 37 — modal verbs in reported speech.

Phenomenon: the backshift of modals in reported speech is not symmetrical: some verbs shift, others remain.

Shifts: will→would, can→could, may→might, shall→should, must (obligation)→had to. No change: would, could, should, might, ought to, used to, had better.

Examples:
  • "I can help." → She said she could help.
  • "You must leave." → He said I had to leave.
  • "It must be true." → He said it must be true. (epistemic must is preserved).
Contrast: must meaning logical deduction usually does not change; meaning obligation — had to. When speech and event are simultaneous the shift may be absent: She says she will come.
adv_u37.exc: Must expressing obligation usually becomes had to; expressing logical deduction it stays must: He said it must be true.
mistakes: He said he must go yesterday. → He said he had to go. — obligation must changes to had to.
She said she can swim. → She said she could swim. — shift can → could in reported speech.
He told that he would come. → He said (that) he would come. — tell requires an object.

adv_u38.text: Unit 38 – Reporting with nouns and adjectives
adv_u38.note: His claim that… / Her refusal to… / the fact that…
adv_u38.exp:
Unit 38 — reporting through nouns and adjectives.

Phenomenon: other people's words and attitudes are conveyed through compact nominal and adjectival constructions — typical of academic and journalistic style.

Structures:
  • Noun + that-clause: claim, belief, suggestion, admission, denial, acknowledgement, announcement: The claim that he lied was unfounded.
  • Noun + to-inf: refusal, decision, willingness, reluctance, intention: Her refusal to cooperate surprised us.
  • Adjective + that-clause/to-inf: adamant that, reluctant to, insistent that: He was adamant that he was right.
Register: nominalisation raises formality. Contrast: the fact that without of; after advice/news — only that-clause, not an infinitive.
adv_u38.exc: After the fact that no of is needed: the fact of that → the fact that. After news / advice — a that-clause, not a to-inf.
mistakes: His refusal of help us → His refusal to help us — refusal + to-inf.
The claim of he was innocent → The claim that he was innocent — claim + that-clause.
An advice that he gave → The advice (that) he gave — advice uncountable.

adv_u39.text: Unit 39 – Should in that-clauses; present subjunctive
adv_u39.note: It's important that he should come / that he come
adv_u39.exp:
Unit 39 — should in that-clauses and the present subjunctive.

Phenomenon: after verbs and expressions of demand/importance a special form is used: either should + inf (BrE), or the base form of the verb without -s (subjunctive, AmE and formal).

Triggers: verbs suggest, recommend, insist, demand, require, propose, request, urge; adjectives essential, important, vital, crucial, necessary, imperative; nouns demand, recommendation, insistence.

Examples:
  • I insist that he be present. (AmE) / …that he should be present. (BrE)
  • It is essential that she not miss the meeting. (note: not before the verb)
Contrast: insist that + indicative = stating a fact (He insisted that he was right); + subjunctive/should = demand (He insisted that she leave). The register of the subjunctive is written, academic.
adv_u39.exc: In BrE should is common, in AmE — the base form (subjunctive): I suggest (that) he be present.
mistakes: It is essential that he comes. → It is essential that he come / should come. — subjunctive after essential.
I demand that she is here. → I demand that she be here. — base form after demand.
He insisted that she went. → He insisted that she go / should go. — after insist on requirement.

adv_u40.text: Unit 40 – Agreement between subject and verb (1)
adv_u40.note: Problems of subject and predicate agreement
adv_u40.exp:
Unit 40 — subject-verb agreement (part 1).

Phenomenon: choosing the number of the verb with complex or formally ambiguous subjects.

Rules:
  • Collective nouns (team, government, family, committee): BrE — singular or plural depending on meaning (the team are arguing = individuals), AmE — only singular.
  • Neither…nor / Either…or: agreement with the nearest subject: Neither the students nor the teacher was aware.
  • Subject = infinitive/clause: singular: To err is human. What matters is effort.
  • Distances, sums, time as a single whole: singular: Ten miles is a long walk. £500 is enough.
Exceptions: news, mathematics, physics, politics, economics — formally plural but take a singular verb; police, cattle, people — always plural. Contrast: a pair of trousers is / trousers are — depends on the structure.
adv_u40.exc:
The news / mathematics / politics — formally plural but with a singular verb.
Police / cattle — always plural.
mistakes: The news are bad. → The news is bad. — news singular.
Police is coming. → Police are coming. — police plural.
Everyone are here. → Everyone is here. — everyone singular.

adv_u41.text: Unit 41 – Agreement between subject and verb (2)
adv_u41.exp:
Unit 41 — subject-verb agreement (part 2).

Phenomenon: continuation of the topic — quantitative expressions and indefinite pronouns.

Rules:
  • A number of + pl. noun = plural verb: A number of students were absent.
  • The number of + pl. noun = singular verb: The number of applicants is rising.
  • Fractions and percentages: agreement by the reference noun: Half of the cake is gone / Half of the students are here.
  • Indefinite everyone, someone, nobody, each, either, neither — singular: Everyone is ready.
Register: formally neither of them knows; in colloquial speech know is found. Contrast: a lot of and plenty of — agreement by the noun (a lot of water is / a lot of people are).
adv_u41.exc: A number of + plural verb; the number of + singular verb.
mistakes: A number of students was absent. → A number of students were absent. — a number of + plural.
The number of errors are rising. → The number of errors is rising. — the number = singular.
Neither of them know. → Neither of them knows. — neither formal singular.

adv_u42.text: Unit 42 – Agreement between subject and verb (3)
adv_u42.note: Agreement with relative clauses and complex subjects
adv_u42.exp:
Unit 42 — subject-verb agreement (part 3).

Phenomenon: agreement in relative clauses and with complex numerical/group subjects.

Rules:
  • In one of the X who/that + V: the verb is plural because the antecedent is X (plural): She is one of those people who care deeply.
  • In the only one of the X who/that: the verb is singular: He's the only one of us who knows.
  • Numerals as a sum/measure: singular: Three million pounds was raised.
  • The majority/minority of: agreement by the noun: The majority of voters support…
Contrast: more than one + singular verb (contrary to the meaning): More than one student has complained. Formal academic style requires a clear choice of reference.
adv_u42.exc: In one of the X who/that + V the verb is plural: He is one of the people who work here.
mistakes: He's one of those who likes tea. → …who like tea. — antecedent plural.
Either you or I is wrong. → Either you or I am wrong. — agreement with the nearest subject.
The team are winning the cup (AmE). → The team is winning. — in AmE collective = singular.

adv_u43.text: Unit 43 – Compound nouns and noun phrases
adv_u43.note: Car park / a two-hour delay / state-of-the-art
adv_u43.exp:
Unit 43 — compound nouns and noun phrases.

Phenomenon: complex nominal formations in formal texts. A compact alternative to prepositional-attributive constructions.

Structures:
  • Compounds: car park, science fiction, bookshop — spelling (one word/hyphen/separate) does not follow a general rule.
  • Attributive position: the first noun is singular, often hyphenated: a ten-minute walk, a five-year-old child, a two-hour meeting.
  • Postmodification through of: a walk of ten minutes (less compact).
Examples:
  • A state-of-the-art facility.
  • Student accommodation problems. (chain of three nouns)
Register: long chains are typical of newspapers and academic style. Contrast: a woman doctor (type) vs a doctor's office (possession) — different relations.
adv_u43.exc: In compound nouns the first word is usually singular: a two-hour delay, not a two-hours delay.
mistakes: A five-years-old boy → A five-year-old boy — no -s in attributive position.
A cars park → A car park — the first noun is singular.
An information centre (if meant as "centre of informations") → An information centre — information uncountable.

adv_u44.note: a friend of mine / one person I know
adv_u44.exp:
Unit 44 — a/an and one.

Phenomenon: both words can indicate "one" but have different functions: article vs. numeral/selective pronoun.

Differences:
  • A/an — neutral indefinite article: I need a pen.
  • One — emphasis on number or selection: I need one pen, not two.
  • One + noun = "some/a certain": One day you will understand.
  • A/an is obligatory with professions, units of measure: She's a teacher. £50 an hour.
Idioms with one: one day, one morning, one of these days, one and the same. Contrast: Wait a minute (neutral) vs Wait one minute! (emphasis, irritation). In time/price expressions — only a/an: twice a week, not twice one week.
adv_u44.exc: One stresses "exactly one"; a/an is neutral: Wait a minute vs Wait one minute (emphasis).
mistakes: I have one brother and two sister. → …two sisters. — plural after a number greater than 1.
She is a my friend. → She is a friend of mine. — a double determiner is not allowed.
One day or another → One day or other / some day. — fixed expression.

adv_u45.text: Unit 45 - A/an, the and zero article (1)
adv_u45.note: Basic principles for choosing an article on C1
adv_u45.exp:
Unit 45 — a/an, the and zero article (part 1).

Phenomenon: systematisation of articles through the categories of definiteness, specificity and generalisation.

Types of reference:
  • Specific vs. generic: The tiger is endangered (the species as a whole) / A tiger is dangerous (any representative of the type) / Tigers are endangered (plural generalisation, the most neutral).
  • Thematic progression: a introduces something new, the continues: I saw a man. The man was tall.
  • Unique objects: the sun, the moon, the president (of…).
Examples:
  • The computer has changed society. (as an invention)
  • She plays the violin. (instrument with the)
Contrast: in scientific style the plural without an article (Tigers are…) is preferred over the tiger. Geographical names: the Alps, the Netherlands, the UK — exceptions to memorise.
adv_u45.exc: With proper names there is usually no article, but: the Netherlands, the UK, the Alps.
mistakes: I love the nature. → I love nature. — abstract concepts without the.
She plays the football. → She plays football. — sports without an article.
He goes to the bed early. → He goes to bed early. — fixed expression without article.

adv_u46.text: Unit 46 - A/an, the and zero article (2)
adv_u46.exp:
Unit 46 — a/an, the and zero article (part 2).

Phenomenon: articles with abstract and uncountable nouns; shifts in meaning when the article changes.

Rules:
  • General concept — zero article: Life is short. Love conquers all.
  • Specified concept — the: The life of a monk is hard. The love of money is the root of all evil.
  • A type/instance — a/an: She has a deep love of music.
Fixed expressions without an article: at war, in love, by chance, on fire, at risk, in hospital, at school, by car.

Examples:
  • Happiness is hard to define.
  • He felt a happiness he had never known.
Contrast: Man is mortal (formal generalisation) vs Humans are mortal (neutral).
adv_u46.exc: Man/woman without an article to generalise about humanity: Man is mortal (formal).
mistakes: The life is hard. → Life is hard. — generalisation without article.
Dogs are the man's best friend. → Dogs are man's best friend. — fixed.
The happiness is important. → Happiness is important. — abstract without article.

adv_u47.text: Unit 47 - A/an, the and zero article (3)
adv_u47.exp:
Unit 47 — articles in thematic groups.

Phenomenon: conventional use of articles with institutions, transport, illnesses, meals, musical instruments.

Rules:
  • Institution by function — no article: in hospital (as a patient), at school (as a student), in prison (as an inmate), in church (at a service).
  • Same place as a physical object — with the: at the hospital (came to visit), near the school.
  • Transport with by — zero: by train, by bus, by plane, but on the train, in the car.
  • Illnesses: usually zero (cancer, diabetes); infections/ailments — a (a cold, a headache); in AmE — the flu, the measles.
  • Instruments: play the piano; sports: play football.
Contrast: BrE go to hospital vs AmE go to the hospital. Go to bed (to sleep) vs sit on the bed (on the bed as an object).
adv_u47.exc: Go to hospital (BrE, as a patient) vs go to the hospital (AmE / as a visitor).
mistakes: He's in the prison. → He's in prison. — as an inmate.
I go to the work by bus. → I go to work by bus. — by + transport without article.
She has flu. (AmE) → She has the flu. — in AmE with the.

adv_u48.exp:
Unit 48 — some and any at C1 level.

Phenomenon: the choice depends not only on sentence type but also on meaning (referential vs. non-referential).

Rules:
  • Some — a known but unspecified quantity/object: I read it in some book.
  • Any in affirmative = "any, without exception": Any student can apply.
  • Some in questions — when expecting agreement/polite offer: Would you like some tea?
  • Any in questions and negatives — neutral: Is there any milk?
Idioms: some day, somehow, somewhere (indefiniteness in the future); anyhow, anyway (in any case).

Contrast: Pick any card (any) vs Pick some cards (a few). If you have any questions (any) vs If you have some questions (some specific ones already formed).
adv_u48.exc: Any in affirmative = "any": Pick any card. Some in questions when expecting "yes": Would you like some tea?
mistakes: I don't have some money. → I don't have any money. — negative any.
Is there some milk? (neutral question) → Is there any milk?
Anybody can't do it. → Nobody can do it / Anybody can do it. — double negative.

adv_u49.text: Unit 49 – No, none (of) and not any
adv_u49.note: No solution / none of them / not any reason
adv_u49.exp:
Unit 49 — no, none (of) and not any.

Phenomenon: three ways of expressing absence, with different registers and syntax.

Differences:
  • No + noun — categorical, emphatic, often in headlines and formal texts: There are no seats left.
  • Not any — a neutral conversational equivalent: There aren't any seats left.
  • None — a stand-alone pronoun, without a noun: How many came? — None.
  • None of + def. noun phrase: None of the students passed.
Agreement: none of + pl. — formally singular (None of them was ready), colloquially plural (None of them were ready).

Contrast: No smoking (prohibition) ≠ Not smoking (absence of action). You cannot combine them: I have no any money is a double-negative error.
adv_u49.exc: After none of + plural formally sing., but plural is acceptable: None of us is/are ready.
mistakes: I have no any money. → I have no money / don't have any money. — cannot combine.
None of them was invited (colloq.) → None of them were invited. — plural more common in conversation.
No of them came. → None of them came. — before of use none.

adv_u50.text: Unit 50 – Much (of), many (of), a lot of, lots (of)
adv_u50.exp:
Unit 50 — much (of), many (of), a lot of, lots (of).

Phenomenon: quantitative expressions with major register differences.

Register scale:
  • A great deal of / a large amount of / much — formal (written style): A great deal of effort was required.
  • A lot of / plenty of — neutral, universal.
  • Lots of / loads of / tons of — conversational: We had loads of fun.
Rules: much in affirmatives is avoided — replaced by a lot of. In negatives and questions much/many are neutral: Do you have much time? Not much. Many of + def.: Many of the problems remain unsolved.

Agreement: a lot of water is / a lot of people are — by the noun.

Contrast: much is acceptable in affirmatives with modifiers: so much time, too much noise, as much as possible.
adv_u50.exc: Much in affirmatives is rare: avoid I have much money → I have a lot of money.
mistakes: I have much friends. → I have many / a lot of friends. — countable.
A lot of water are needed. → A lot of water is needed. — agreement with water.
Lots of informations → Lots of information. — uncountable.

adv_u51.note: Subtle differences, formal and colloquial contexts
adv_u51.exp:
Unit 51 — all (of), whole, every, each.

Phenomenon: four "all-encompassing" quantifiers with different logic.

Differences:
  • All — the entire set, collectively: All the students passed.
  • Whole — wholeness, no exceptions, emphasis on unity: The whole class passed. / the whole day.
  • Every — distributive, each without exception (3+ items): Every student got a certificate.
  • Each — individualises, often 2+ items with attention to differences: Each student received a different task.
Postposition: They all arrived / They each received a copy (after a pronoun).

Contrast: all day = the whole day (24 hours), but every day = each day in a series. Each of them knows (always singular). Every does not take of — you need every one of.
adv_u51.exc: All / whole: all the day (rare) vs the whole day. With time the whole is more common.
mistakes: Every students → Every student — singular.
Each of them know. → Each of them knows. — each sing.
All of information → All the information / all of the information.

adv_u52.note: Fewer mistakes vs. less money (and the with fewer error)
adv_u52.exp:
Unit 52 — few, little, less, fewer.

Phenomenon: quantitative opposition depending on noun type and connotation.

Rules:
  • Few — with countable plural: few friends (few, negative). A few — positive (some quantity): a few friends.
  • Little — with uncountable: little time (little). A little — positive: a little time.
  • Fewer — comparative of few: fewer cars.
  • Less — comparative of little: less traffic.
Examples:
  • He has few real friends.
  • She has a little patience left.
Conversational usage: less people instead of fewer people is widespread but considered an error in the written norm. Formal equivalents of few/little: scarcely any, hardly any. Contrast: quite a few (unexpectedly many, positive) vs few (few).
adv_u52.exc: Less with uncountables; fewer with countables. Colloquial less cars occurs but is not in the written norm.
mistakes: Less people came. → Fewer people came. — countable.
A few water → A little water. — uncountable.
Little friends → Few friends — countable.

adv_u53.exp:
Unit 53 — relative pronouns.

Phenomenon: distinguishing defining and non-defining relative clauses and choosing the pronoun.

Rules:
  • Defining (no commas, necessary information): who/that/which/whom/whose. Object whom is often dropped in speech.
  • Non-defining (with commas, additional information): only who/whom/which/whose — that is IMPOSSIBLE.
  • Object who/that/which is dropped in defining: The book (which) I read.
  • Which referring to a whole sentence (non-defining only): She was late, which annoyed me.
Register: whom is formal; who is conversational as an object.

Examples:
  • My sister, who lives in Paris, is a doctor. (I have one sister, clarification)
  • The sister who lives in Paris is a doctor. (one of several sisters, distinction)
Contrast: commas change the meaning of the statement.
adv_u53.exc: In non-defining clauses that is not allowed: My sister, that lives in NY → My sister, who lives in NY.
mistakes: The book what I read → The book (that/which) I read. — what is not used as a relative.
The man which helped me → The man who helped me. — who for a person.
My brother, that is a doctor → My brother, who is a doctor.

adv_u54.text: Unit 54 – Whose, when, whereby and other relative words
adv_u54.note: The system whereby… / the reason why…
adv_u54.exp:
Unit 54 — whose, when, whereby and other relative words.

Phenomenon: an extended set of relative words typical of academic and legal style.

Words:
  • Whose — possession (for people and things): A country whose economy is growing.
  • Whereby = by which: A system whereby students apply online.
  • Wherein = in which (very formal).
  • Whereupon — "and after that": He nodded, whereupon the meeting ended.
  • When — for time: The day when we met.
  • Where — for place: The town where I grew up.
  • Why — for reason: The reason why he left.
Register: whereby/wherein/whereupon — only formal written style.

Contrast: where already includes the preposition — you cannot say the place where I was born in. The reason why is conversational; formally the reason for which.
adv_u54.exc: Whose can also refer to things: a country whose population is growing.
mistakes: The reason because → The reason why / that / for which.
The place where I was born in → The place where I was born. — where already includes "in".
The day when we met on → The day (when) we met.

adv_u55.note: The company for which / the person to whom
adv_u55.exp:
Unit 55 — prepositions in relative clauses.

Phenomenon: the position of the preposition is a key register marker between formal and conversational style.

Variants:
  • Conversational: preposition at the end, who/that or omission: The person (who/that) I spoke to.
  • Formal: preposition before whom/which: The person to whom I spoke.
Restrictions: after a preposition who or that is not allowed — only whom/which. You cannot drop the pronoun if the preposition is before it.

Examples:
  • The company for which I work is expanding. (formal)
  • The topic we were talking about was interesting. (conv.)
Register: phrasal verbs with a preposition require keeping it (look after → the child I look after). Contrast: academic writing prefers of which/in which/to whom; e-mail and speech — preposition at the end.
adv_u55.exc: Formally: the man to whom I spoke; informally: the man (who) I spoke to.
mistakes: The company which I work → The company which I work for / for which I work.
The person to who I wrote → The person to whom I wrote. — after to — whom.
The issue about that we talked → The issue (which/that) we talked about.

adv_u56.text: Unit 56 – Noun phrases in a sentence (Lesson 1)
adv_u56.exp:
Unit 56 — noun phrases (lesson 1).

Phenomenon: noun phrases are a key tool for compact packaging of information in formal texts.

Structure: determiner + pre-modifier(s) + head noun + post-modifier(s).
  • Premodifiers: adjectives (beautiful), nouns (car park), participles (running water), attributive chains.
  • Postmodifiers: relative clause (…that I read), prepositional phrase (…on the table), infinitive (the first to arrive), participle phrase (people living abroad).
Order of adjectives: opinion → size → age → shape → colour → origin → material → purpose: a lovely little old round red Italian wooden dining table.

Example: The recently published book on climate change by the leading scientist.

Contrast: academic style prefers nominalisation (the publication of the book) over verbal clauses (when they published the book).
adv_u56.exc: In formal speech noun phrases can be very long thanks to post-modification.
mistakes: The book interesting → The interesting book. — simple adj before noun.
A man tall and dark → A tall, dark man.
The girl her hair is long → The girl whose hair is long.

adv_u57.exp:
Unit 57 — noun phrases (lesson 2): postmodification.

Phenomenon: reduced forms of relative clauses are an economical academic tool.

Types of postmodifiers:
  • Participial: the woman standing there = who is standing there; the car parked outside = which is parked outside.
  • Infinitival: the first person to arrive; the only one to notice.
  • Prepositional phrases: the man in the blue shirt.
  • Apposition: my friend Anna, a talented pianist.
Examples:
  • The issues discussed at the meeting remain unresolved.
  • Letters to be posted should be left here.
Contrast: the man running (active action now) vs the man who runs (regularly). A to be-infinitive postmodifier has a meaning of necessity/futurity.
adv_u57.exc: Participial and infinitival postmodifiers: the man standing there / the first to arrive.
mistakes: The man who is sitting there → The man sitting there. — reduction.
The problems which are discussed → The problems discussed.
Letters to posting → Letters to post / to be posted.

adv_u58.note: Leaving the room, she… / Not knowing what to do…
adv_u58.exp:
Unit 58 — adverbial participle clauses (lesson 1).

Phenomenon: condensing subordinate clauses through -ing and -ed forms. An important marker of formal writing.

Types:
  • Active -ing (simultaneity/consequence): Seeing the danger, she acted quickly.
  • Passive -ed: Exhausted by the climb, they rested.
  • Perfect having + V3 (precedence): Having finished the report, she left.
  • Negative: Not knowing what to do, he called for help.
Critical rule: the subject of the clause = the subject of the main sentence, otherwise dangling participle.
