Meanings: time, reason, condition, concession (from context).

Contrast: Being tired, I went to bed (reason) vs When tired, I get irritable (condition). Formal style prefers participles; conversational — full clauses.
adv_u58.exc: A participle clause must have the same subject as the main clause.
mistakes: Walking home, the rain started. → While I was walking home, the rain started. — dangling participle.
Being tired, the bed looked inviting. → Being tired, I found the bed inviting.
Not knowing the answer, the teacher explained. → Not knowing the answer, I asked the teacher.

adv_u59.exp:
Unit 59 — adverbial participle clauses (lesson 2).

Phenomenon: advanced patterns: participles with conjunctions and independent (absolute) constructions.

Structures:
  • Participle + conjunction: While working on the project, she discovered… / When asked, he refused. / If treated early, the illness is curable.
  • Absolute construction (independent subject): The meeting (being) over, everyone left. / Weather permitting, we'll go hiking.
  • With-constructions: With the project completed, we can relax.
Register: absolute constructions are characteristic of literary and official style.

Examples:
  • All things considered, it was a success.
  • The sun having set, we headed home.
Contrast: having done stresses completion before the main action, doing — simultaneity.
adv_u59.exc: Perfect participle having done stresses completion before the main-clause action.
mistakes: Having finished, he is leaving. → Having finished, he left. — tense agreement.
Finished the work, he went home. → Having finished the work, he went home.
Being arrived, we had dinner. → Having arrived, we had dinner.

adv_u60.exp:
Unit 60 — reflexive pronouns at C1.

Phenomenon: reflexive pronouns have several functions and are often confused with each other and personal pronouns.

Functions:
  • True reflexive (subject = object): She hurt herself.
  • Emphatic (intensification): I did it myself.
  • After the preposition by = "alone/on one's own": She lives by herself.
  • Idiomatic: help yourself, enjoy yourself, make yourself at home, behave yourself.
When NOT used: with verbs of self-care (wash, shave, dress, hide) — the reflexive is usually dropped: She dressed quickly.

Contrast: They love each other (reciprocal, A→B and B→A) vs They love themselves (each himself). After with: I took my keys with me, not with myself (unless emphatic). Between you and me, not myself — a typical hypercorrection.
adv_u60.exc: After wash, dress, shave, hide the reflexive is usually dropped: I washed, not I washed myself.
mistakes: She washed herself and dressed herself. → She washed and dressed.
I and my friend saw us in the mirror. → My friend and I saw ourselves in the mirror.
They talked themselves. → They talked to each other.

adv_u61.note: One as a pronoun / general pronoun / one another
adv_u61.exp:
Unit 61 — one/ones as a substitute and general pronoun.

Phenomenon: one has two roles: replacement of a countable noun and a generalisation "people in general".

Functions:
  • Noun replacement: I prefer the red one. / The ones on the left are mine.
  • Generalising (formal): One should always be polite. Conversational equivalent — you.
  • One another = reciprocal (more formal than each other): They support one another.
Restrictions: one/ones is not used with uncountables (this music, not this one) and after possessives (my book, not my one — except colloquial BrE).

Examples:
  • Which jacket? — The blue one.
  • One never knows what may happen.
Contrast: academic style prefers one as a generaliser; conversational — you.
adv_u61.exc: One as a generalising pronoun is formal: One should be careful.
mistakes: I need a red one pen. → I need a red one / a red pen. — one replaces the noun.
Give me the blue ones book. → Give me the blue one.
They helped themselves one to another. → They helped one another / each other.

adv_u62.note: I think so / I hope so / so they say
adv_u62.exp:
Unit 62 — so as a clause substitute.

Phenomenon: so replaces a whole that-clause, avoiding repetition.

Verbs: think, hope, believe, suppose, expect, imagine, guess, be afraid, seem, appear, say, tell.

Examples:
  • Will he come? — I think so.
  • Is it raining? — I'm afraid so.
  • So I've heard.
Negation — two patterns:
  • I don't think so (usually for think, believe, suppose, expect, imagine).
  • I think not / I hope not / I'm afraid not (for hope, be afraid, guess, say — negation only via not).
Contrast: Say so (confirm) vs say that (introduce new information). If so — "if that is the case"; if not — "if not". You cannot say I think it as a clause replacement.
adv_u62.exc: I'm afraid so / not; but not I'm afraid it.
mistakes: I think it. → I think so. — so replaces that-clause.
I hope no. → I hope not.
I don't think so that he will come. → I don't think he will come. — redundant.

adv_u63.note: She did so immediately. Such was his anger.
adv_u63.exp:
Unit 63 — do so and such.

Phenomenon: do so is a formal substitute for the verb group; such is a means of intensification, especially in inversion.

Do so / do it / do that:
  • Do so — formal, exact replacement of a verb with complements: She was asked to sign the document, which she did so willingly.
  • Do it — neutral: Did you tell him? — Yes, I did it yesterday.
  • Do that — with emphasis on the action.
Such:
  • Such + a/an + adj + noun: It was such a beautiful day.
  • Inversion (very formal): Such was his anger that he slammed the door.
  • Such as = for example: fruits such as apples.
Register: do so and inverted such — academic and literary style. Contrast: so + adj (so beautiful) vs such + a + adj + noun (such a beautiful day).
adv_u63.exc: Do so is formal; in conversation — do it/that.
mistakes: Such a was the noise → Such was the noise — no article in inversion.
He asked me to leave and I did it so. → …and I did so.
It was such nice day. → It was such a nice day. — such a + sing. count.

adv_u64.note: She can swim and so can he / Ellipsis
adv_u64.exp:
Unit 64 — ellipsis of auxiliary verbs.

Phenomenon: dropping the verb group while keeping the auxiliary — key to text cohesion and spoken naturalness.

Patterns:
  • Agreement with so + aux + subject: She likes jazz, and so does he.
  • Agreement in the negative neither/nor + aux + subject: I don't smoke, and neither does she.
  • Contrast: He works hard, but she doesn't. (work hard omitted)
  • Short answers: Will you come? — I might.
Inversion: after so/neither/nor inverted word order is obligatory: So do I, not So I do. So I do = confirmation ("indeed!").

Examples:
  • I've never been to Japan. — Neither have I.
  • Tom passed, but Sam didn't.
Contrast: Me too (conversational) = So do I (more formal). After the modal be — So am I.
adv_u64.exc: So/neither/nor + auxiliary + subject: So do I, not So I do.
mistakes: I can swim and he can too swim. → …and so can he.
She doesn't smoke and neither I do. → …and neither do I. — inversion.
I'm tired. Me too am. → I'm tired. So am I / Me too.

adv_u65.note: I'd like to come if I can.
adv_u65.exp:
Unit 65 — ellipsis of to-infinitives.

Phenomenon: keeping to while dropping the infinitive group — important for the naturalness of speech.

Structures:
  • Stranded to: I'd love to come but I'm not able to.
  • In conditions and polite evasions: I would help if I wanted to.
  • Answers: Are you going? — I'd like to.
Verbs that often allow to-ellipsis: want, plan, hope, need, try, mean, intend, would like, used to.

Rules:
  • You cannot repeat a second to: I'd like to, but I can't to → I can't.
  • After can/must/will and other modals — ellipsis without to: I can if you want me to.
Contrast: I don't want to (omission of the action object) vs I don't want (just "don't want", without reference). Ellipsis is obligatory in connected dialogue — without it the reply sounds pedantic.
adv_u65.exc: After want, would like, try you can omit to: I'd love to, not I'd love to come.
mistakes: I'd like to, but I can't to. → I'd like to, but I can't. — the second to is superfluous.
He told me do it. → He told me to do it. — tell + to-inf.
Would you like? — Yes, I'd like. → Yes, I'd like to. — keep to.

adv_u66.exp:
Unit 66 — position of the adjective.

Phenomenon: restrictions on attributive (before noun) and predicative (after a linking verb) use. Some adjectives change meaning depending on position.

Groups:
  • Only attributive: main, chief, former, mere, outright, sheer, nuclear, atomic: the main reason, not the reason is main.
  • Only predicative: afraid, alive, alone, asleep, awake, aware, content, glad, ill, well, unable: The child is asleep, but a sleeping child (not asleep child).
  • Change of meaning: the present members (current) vs the members present (those present); a certain person (some) vs I'm certain (sure); my old friend (long-standing) vs my friend is old (aged).
Examples:
  • The late Mr Smith — deceased (attributive);
  • He's late — being late (predicative).
Contrast: position is not stylistics but semantics. Postposition is typical of fixed expressions (time immemorial, body politic).
adv_u66.exc: Alive, alone, afraid, asleep, awake — only predicative: an alive fish → a living fish.
mistakes: The afraid child → The frightened child.
The present members (meaning "those present") → The members present — postposition changes meaning.
An asleep baby → A sleeping baby.

adv_u67.exp:
Unit 67 — gradable and non-gradable adjectives (lesson 1).

Phenomenon: adjectives split into relative (allow degrees) and absolute (already express a limit state). The choice of intensifier depends on the type.

Gradable: cold, hot, big, tired, interesting, important. Intensifiers: very, quite, rather, fairly, a bit, slightly, extremely, incredibly.

Non-gradable:
  • Absolute: perfect, unique, impossible, essential, dead, alive, correct, wrong.
  • Extreme: freezing, boiling, exhausted, starving, furious, terrified, delicious, brilliant.
  • Classifying: nuclear, digital, annual, wooden.
Intensifiers for non-gradable: absolutely, completely, totally, utterly, quite (meaning "entirely").

Examples:
  • It was absolutely freezing.
  • The film was very interesting.
Contrast: very cold (gradable) vs absolutely freezing (already the limit). Quite with gradable = "fairly", with non-gradable = "entirely".
adv_u67.exc: Very does not combine with non-gradable: very perfect → absolutely perfect.
mistakes: Very delicious → Absolutely delicious. — non-gradable.
Absolutely good → Very good. — gradable.
Very impossible → Completely impossible.

adv_u68.exp:
Unit 68 — gradable and non-gradable adjectives (lesson 2).

Phenomenon: pragmatics of intensifier choice and fixed collocations typical of C1.

Collocations (fixed pairs):
  • Bitterly cold/disappointed.
  • Highly successful/unlikely/controversial.
  • Utterly ridiculous/exhausted.
  • Deeply concerned/sorry.
  • Perfectly reasonable/fine.
Downtoners: a bit, slightly, somewhat, rather, fairly — only with gradable.

Register:
  • Formal: highly, extremely, considerably.
  • Conversational: really, pretty, dead (BrE), well (BrE colloq).
Examples:
  • The results were highly encouraging.
  • She was bitterly disappointed by the outcome.
Contrast: very unique is taboo in the written norm, although heard in speech. Quite unique is acceptable in the sense of "truly unique".
adv_u68.exc: Collocations are strictly fixed: utterly ridiculous, bitterly cold, highly successful.
mistakes: Highly cold → Bitterly cold. — fixed collocation.
Absolutely tired → Completely exhausted. — with non-gradable.
Very unique → Quite unique / unique. — unique non-gradable.

adv_u69.text: Unit 69 – Participle adjectives and compound adjectives
adv_u69.exp:
Unit 69 — participial and compound adjectives.

Phenomenon: a productive way of creating adjectival modifiers from verbs and word combinations.

Participle adjectives:
  • -ing — active property causing an emotion: a boring lecture (the lecture is boring — it causes boredom).
  • -ed — passive/resulting state: a bored audience.
Compound adjectives:
  • Hyphenated before a noun: a well-known writer, a ten-year-old girl, state-of-the-art technology.
  • Without hyphen after a linking verb: The writer is well known.
  • Productive patterns: N + -ed (big-eyed, left-handed); adj + N + -ed (good-natured); adv + V3 (air-conditioned, purpose-built); adj + V-ing (good-looking, slow-moving).
Examples:
  • A thought-provoking book.
  • A soft-spoken diplomat.
Contrast: -ing vs -ed are regularly confused: I'm interested (I am interested) vs it's interesting (this is interesting).
adv_u69.exc: -ing describes the cause of the emotion; -ed — the experienced state: boring film / bored viewer.
mistakes: I am boring with this lesson. → I am bored with this lesson. — state = -ed.
The film was bored. → The film was boring.
A well-know artist → A well-known artist. — compound with -ed.

adv_u70.note: Eager to learn / capable of doing / certain that
adv_u70.exp:
Unit 70 — adjectives with to-inf, -ing and that-clause.

Phenomenon: adjectives require specific complements; mixing them leads to errors.

Patterns:
  • Adj + to-inf: able, eager, reluctant, willing, likely, unlikely, due, liable, keen, happy, ready: She was eager to learn.
  • Adj + prep + -ing: capable of doing, fond of reading, interested in learning, afraid of failing, tired of waiting, good at singing.
  • Adj + that-clause: sure that, certain that, aware that, convinced that, surprised that, sorry that, glad that.
  • Evaluative with formal it: It is kind of you to help. It is difficult for us to decide.
Examples:
  • I'm afraid of flying.
  • He's keen to start / keen on starting. — double pattern for keen.
Contrast: sorry to + inf (about a future action) vs sorry for -ing (about a past action). Good at, not good in. Prepositions with adjectives are part of the C1 lexical minimum.
adv_u70.exc: Keen on + ing, but keen to + inf: keen on swimming / keen to learn.
mistakes: Capable to swim → Capable of swimming. — capable + of + -ing.
Interested to music → Interested in music.
Afraid to spiders → Afraid of spiders.

adv_u71.text: Unit 71 – Adjectives and adverbs: subtle differences
adv_u71.note: He spoke loudly. He is a loud speaker.
adv_u71.exp:
Unit 71 — subtle differences between adjectives and adverbs.

Phenomenon: many words have two forms — with -ly and without — with different meanings, which is critical at C1.

Pairs:
  • Hard (diligently) / hardly (barely): He works hard / He hardly works.
  • Late (late) / lately (recently): She arrived late / I've been busy lately.
  • Near (close) / nearly (almost): Come near / I nearly fell.
  • High (high, physically) / highly (very): Birds fly high / highly respected.
  • Deep (deep, physically) / deeply (deeply, emotional): dig deep / deeply concerned.
  • Wide (wide) / widely (widely, everywhere).
Adjectives after linking verbs: feel, look, sound, taste, smell, seem, appear, become, get — adj, not adv: She looks happy, not happily.

Contrast: good/well — the classic case: He speaks good English (adj to English) vs He speaks English well (adv to speaks).
adv_u71.exc: Hard/fast/late — same form for adj and adv; hardly, lately — different meanings.
mistakes: He works hardly. → He works hard. — hardly = "barely".
She arrived lately. → She arrived late. — lately = "recently".
He speaks good English well. → He speaks English well.

adv_u72.note: Comparison on C1: ellipse and special constructions
adv_u72.exp:
Unit 72 — comparative and superlative at C1.

Phenomenon: complex comparison patterns: parallel structures, emphatic intensifiers, ellipsis.

Structures:
  • Doubling for intensification: more and more expensive, colder and colder.
  • Parallel the + comp…, the + comp…: The more you practise, the better you get.
  • Superlative + Present Perfect: the best film I've ever seen.
  • Comparative intensifiers: much, far, a lot, considerably, significantly, slightly, a bit, rather + comp: far better, significantly higher.
Irregular: good/well → better → best; bad/badly → worse → worst; far → further/farther → furthest/farthest; little → less → least.

Examples:
  • This is by far the most interesting book.
Contrast: further (abstract — further details) vs farther (distance, mainly AmE). Double comparison (more better) is taboo.
adv_u72.exc: Two syllables ending in -y/-ow/-er often take -er/-est: narrower, cleverer.
mistakes: More taller → Taller. — double comparative.
The most best → The best. — double superlative.
More good → Better.

adv_u73.note: Than expected / as planned / as good as it gets
adv_u73.exp:
Unit 73 — comparative phrases and clauses.

Phenomenon: reduced and elliptical comparative constructions for economical expression.

Structures:
  • Incomplete: than expected, than planned, than usual, than before: The results were better than expected.
  • As…as: as soon as possible, as good as new, as often as not.
  • The same as / different from / similar to.
  • No sooner…than: No sooner had we arrived than it started raining.
  • Idioms: more or less, sooner or later, as good as it gets.
  • Rather than — conjunction: Rather than complain, she took action.
Examples:
  • She's not as clever as she thinks.
  • It's more difficult than it looks.
Register: formally — full than I am; conversationally — than me. Contrast: different from (standard) / different to (BrE) / different than (AmE).
adv_u73.exc: The more… the more: The more he studies, the better he becomes.
mistakes: He is taller than me tall. → He is taller than I am / than me.
As soon as possible as → As soon as possible.
More expensive as → More expensive than.

adv_u74.note: Position of adverb in a sentence: beginning, middle, end
adv_u74.exp:
Unit 74 — position of the adverb (lesson 1).

Phenomenon: English has strict rules for adverb position with subtle semantic effects.

Three positions:
  • Front (beginning): emphasis, comment: Suddenly, the lights went out.
  • Mid (middle) — before the main verb, after be, after the first auxiliary: She always arrives early. / She is always on time. / She has always lived here.
  • End (end) — the neutral position for adverbs of manner, place, time.
Rules for mid-position: frequency (often, always, usually, rarely, never), certainty (probably, definitely, certainly), focus (just, only, even), completeness (already, still, yet).

Examples:
  • He has probably forgotten.
  • I only saw him yesterday.
Contrast: She's never late / She is never late — after be. In a short answer the emphasis can change the rule: Yes, she always has.
adv_u74.exc: Frequency adverbs — usually before the main verb, but after be: She is often late.
mistakes: I always am tired. → I am always tired. — after be.
She speaks always loudly. → She always speaks loudly.
He goes usually to work by bus. → He usually goes to work by bus.

adv_u75.exp:
Unit 75 — position of the adverb (lesson 2): several adverbials.

Phenomenon: when there are several adverbials at the end of a sentence, a clear order applies.

Order: Manner (how) → Place (where) → Time (when).

Examples:
  • She sang beautifully in the theatre last night.
  • He works hard at the office every day.
Exceptions and nuances:
  • A time adverbial can be fronted: Last night, she sang beautifully in the theatre.
  • Verbs of motion: place right after the verb: She went home quickly, usually not quickly home.
  • Several adverbials of the same type — from more general to more specific: every Tuesday at 3 p.m.
Rule: a direct object is not separated from the verb by an adverbial: She speaks English fluently, not She speaks fluently English.

Contrast: I always play tennis in the park on Sundays — mid-position frequency + MPT.
adv_u75.exc: Order: manner → place → time. She sang beautifully in the theatre last night.
mistakes: I go every day to work. → I go to work every day. — time at the end.
She plays in the park tennis. → She plays tennis in the park. — object before adverbial.
He drives quickly always. → He always drives quickly.

adv_u76.text: Unit 76 – Adverbs of place, direction, regularity and time
adv_u76.exp:
Unit 76 — adverbs of place, direction, frequency and time.

Phenomenon: classification of adverbs by type of meaning and features of each group.

Groups:
  • Place: here, there, everywhere, nowhere, somewhere, indoors, outdoors, abroad, upstairs.
  • Direction: home, away, forward, backward, up, down.
  • Time: now, then, soon, recently, yesterday, today, tomorrow, already, still, yet.
  • Frequency: always, usually, often, frequently, sometimes, occasionally, rarely, seldom, hardly ever, never.
Inversion after here/there: with a noun — inversion: Here comes the bus!; with a pronoun — normal order: Here she comes.

Examples:
  • I've been abroad recently.
  • She rarely complains.
Register: hardly ever (conversational), seldom (formal). Contrast: home and abroad — adverbs that don't take a preposition: go home, not go to home.
adv_u76.exc: Here/there trigger inversion: Here comes the bus (but not with a pronoun: Here she comes).
mistakes: Here comes he. → Here he comes. — with a pronoun, no inversion.
Recently I have see him. → Recently I have seen him / I have seen him recently.
I go there to home. → I go home. — home is already an adverb.

adv_u77.text: Unit 77 – Degree adverbs and focus adverbs
adv_u77.note: very / extremely / even / just / only / almost
adv_u77.exp:
Unit 77 — degree and focus adverbs.

Phenomenon: two important classes — one modifies intensity, the other narrows reference and is critically position-dependent.

Degree adverbs: very, extremely, incredibly, really, quite, rather, fairly, pretty, somewhat, a bit, slightly. Before adj/adv: very quickly, extremely happy.

Focus adverbs: only, just, even, also, too, mainly, especially, particularly, merely, simply. Their position determines what is highlighted.

Examples of positional shifts:
  • Only she told him the truth. (only she did, no one else)
  • She only told him the truth. (she just told, did nothing else)
  • She told only him the truth. (only him, not others)
  • She told him only the truth. (only truth, nothing else)
Register: rather with negative collocations (rather disappointing); quite ambiguous (BrE "fairly", AmE "completely").

Contrast: fairly good (okay) < quite good (good) < very good.
adv_u77.exc: Only/even/just — position changes meaning: I only saw Tom vs I saw only Tom.
mistakes: I almost never meet him. (more often) → I hardly ever meet him.
<s>Only he wants to help.</s> (when meant as "only he") can be read ambiguously; specify the position.
He is very asleep. → He is fast / sound asleep.

adv_u78.text: Unit 78 – Comment adverbs and viewpoint adverbs
adv_u78.exp:
Unit 78 — comment and viewpoint adverbs.

Phenomenon: these adverbs modify the whole sentence, expressing the speaker's attitude or limiting the area of application.

Comment adverbs (evaluation of the situation): fortunately, unfortunately, surprisingly, obviously, clearly, naturally, admittedly, undoubtedly, hopefully, presumably, apparently.

Viewpoint adverbs (point of view/area): personally, frankly, honestly, technically, scientifically, politically, financially, theoretically.

Position: usually at the beginning of the sentence, set off by a comma; less often — in the middle.

Examples:
  • Frankly, I disagree with his approach.
  • Technically, that's not allowed.
  • Unfortunately, the meeting was cancelled.
Register: critically important in academic and business writing for hedging and author positioning.

Contrast: She spoke frankly (manner adverb — how she spoke) vs Frankly, she's wrong (comment — the author's attitude).
adv_u78.exc: Comment adverbs are usually at the beginning + comma: Frankly, I disagree.
mistakes: Personal, I think so. → Personally, I think so.
Hopefully he comes. → Hopefully, he will come. — with future.
Obvious, he is right. → Obviously, he is right.

adv_u79.note: When / as soon as / once / until / by the time
adv_u79.exp:
Unit 79 — adverbial clauses of time.

Phenomenon: time conjunctions with different nuances and the iron rule — no future tense after them.

Conjunctions and shades:
  • When — general time relation.
  • As soon as / the moment (that) / immediately — immediate sequence.
  • Once — after completion (= once): Once you finish, we can go.
  • Until / till — up to what moment.
  • By the time — by the time, often with Perfect: By the time he arrived, we had eaten.
  • While / as — simultaneity.
  • Before / after — sequence.
Iron rule: Present instead of Future, Past Perfect instead of would-have: I'll call when I arrive, not when I will arrive.

Examples:
  • By the time you read this, I will have left.
  • As soon as she heard the news, she called him.
Contrast: when in interrogatives — future is possible (When will you come?).
adv_u79.exc: After when, as soon as, once, until, by the time — Present instead of Future.
mistakes: I'll call you when I will arrive. → …when I arrive.
Until you won't finish → Until you finish.
By the time he will come → By the time he comes.

adv_u80.text: Unit 80 - Giving reasons: as, because; for and with
adv_u80.note: As / because / since / for / with - all methods of cause
adv_u80.exp:
Unit 80 — giving reasons: as, because, since, for, with.

Phenomenon: five means with differences in register and position.

Differences:
  • Because — neutral, new information, answers "why?": We stayed in because it was raining.
  • As / since — a known/obvious reason, often at the beginning: Since it's late, let's stop.
  • For — formal-literary, new information, only after the main clause: He was anxious, for she was late.
  • With + noun / -ing — accompanying reason: With everyone watching, I got nervous.
  • Because of / due to / owing to / on account of + noun/-ing: prepositional.
Register: for as a reason is bookish, almost archaic; due to — formal; because — universal.

Contrast: because + clause, because of + noun. Due to traditionally attributive (delays due to fog), but in modern style also acceptable adverbially.
adv_u80.exc: For as a reason is formal/literary: He was worried, for she was late.
mistakes: Because of he was late → Because he was late / Because of his lateness.
As/since in the middle sometimes sounds unnatural; because is more neutral.
With her smart, she succeeded. → With her being smart / Being smart, she succeeded.

adv_u81.text: Unit 81 – Purposes and results: in order to, so as
adv_u81.note: She left in order to avoid argument.
adv_u81.exp:
Unit 81 — purpose and result.

Phenomenon: distinguishing purpose (intention) and result (consequence) constructions.

Purpose:
  • To-inf (same subject): She left early to catch the train.
  • In order to / so as to — more formal: He worked hard in order to succeed.
  • So that / in order that + clause (different subjects, often with can/could/would): He whispered so that nobody could hear.
  • Negative purpose: so as not to / in order not to: He left early so as not to be late.
Result:
  • So + adj/adv + that: It was so cold that we stayed in.
  • Such + (a/an) + adj + noun + that: It was such a shock that he fainted.
  • So much/many/little/few + noun + that.
Contrast: so that = purpose or result depending on context: He spoke clearly so that everyone could understand (purpose) vs He spoke so clearly that everyone understood (result). for + to-inf is not allowed: I came for to see you → to see you.
adv_u81.exc: So as not to / in order not to to negate purpose: He left early so as not to miss the train.
mistakes: I came for to see you. → I came to see you. — for + inf not allowed.
So that he can't hear (if purpose) → so that he couldn't hear / so as not to let him hear.
For not being late → In order not to be late / So as not to be late.

adv_u82.text: Unit 82 – Contrast clauses: although, while, while, whereas
adv_u82.note: Although / even though / while / whereas / despite
adv_u82.exp:
Unit 82 — contrast: although, while, whilst, whereas, despite.

Phenomenon: two types: concessive (despite) and contrastive (in opposition).

Concessive conjunctions:
  • Although / though / even though + clause: Although it was cold, we went out. Even though — intensifier.
  • Despite / in spite of + noun/-ing/the fact that: Despite the cold, we went out / Despite being cold…
Contrastive:
  • While / whilst / whereas — juxtaposing two ideas: While he prefers tea, she likes coffee. Whilst — BrE more formal.
Linking adverbs: however, nevertheless, nonetheless, yet, still, on the other hand — join two sentences: It was cold; however, we went out.

Register: though at the end of a sentence — conversational: It's cold. I'll go, though.

Contrast: Although and but do not combine: not Although it rained, but we went. Despite requires a noun phrase, not a clause.
adv_u82.exc: Despite / in spite of + noun/-ing, not + clause: despite he was tired → despite being tired / although he was tired.
mistakes: Despite of the rain → Despite the rain / In spite of the rain.
Although tired, but he went. → Although tired, he went. — without but.
Whereas he is tall, his brother short. → Whereas he is tall, his brother is short.

adv_u83.exp:
Unit 83 — conditional sentences (lesson 1): types 0, 1, 2.

Phenomenon: three basic types of real and hypothetical conditions.

Types:
  • Zero (always-true laws): If + Present, Present: If you heat water to 100°C, it boils.
  • First (real future): If + Present, will/can/may…: If it rains, we'll stay in.
  • Second (unreal present/unlikely future): If + Past, would/could/might…: If I had more time, I'd travel more.
Variants in the main clause: will, can, may, might, should, must, be going to — express different modalities. If you see him, tell him (imperative).

Rule: after if never will in standard conditions.

Examples:
  • If I were you, I would apologize.
  • Should you need help, call me. (formal inversion for Type 1.)
Contrast: If you will is possible in polite requests (If you will excuse me) — a separate meaning.
adv_u83.exc: In Zero/First Conditional will is not allowed after if: if you will come → if you come.
mistakes: If I will have time, I'll call. → If I have time, I'll call.
If he comes, I tell him. → …I'll tell him.
If I would be rich → If I were rich. — Type 2.

adv_u84.exp:
Unit 84 — conditional sentences (lesson 2): Type 3 and mixed.

Phenomenon: conditions fully referring to the past and combined ones with different temporal reference.

Third Conditional: If + Past Perfect, would have + V3. Unrealised past: If I had studied harder, I would have passed.

Mixed Conditionals:
  • Past cause → present consequence: If I had taken the job, I would be rich now.
  • Present property → past consequence: If she weren't so careless, she wouldn't have broken it.
Register/style: inverted formal variant: Had I known, I would have helped.

Examples:
  • If they hadn't warned us, we might be dead. (mixed)
  • We would have arrived earlier if the train hadn't been delayed.
Contrast: Type 3 — complete regret about the past; mixed — separates the time of cause and consequence.
adv_u84.exc: Mixed type: If I had studied, I would be a doctor now.
mistakes: If I would have known → If I had known.
If she didn't miss the bus, she would be here. (if the cause is in the past) → If she hadn't missed the bus, she would be here now.
I would passed if I had studied. → I would have passed if I had studied.

adv_u85.text: Unit 85 - If I were you; imagine he were...
adv_u85.exp:
Unit 85 — the were-subjunctive and inversion in conditionals.

Phenomenon: formal alternatives to ordinary conditionals, characteristic of business, legal and literary style.

Subjunctive were: in all persons in unreal conditions: If I were you… If she were here… If he were to… Colloquially was is allowed, except for the fixed If I were you.

Inversion without if:
  • Type 2: Were + subject + to-inf: Were she to call, I would answer.
  • Type 3: Had + subject + V3: Had I known, I would have helped.
  • Polite condition: Should + subject + inf: Should you require assistance, please contact us.
Fixed: I wish I were… Imagine he were… As it were (so to speak).

Examples:
  • Should the weather deteriorate, the event will be postponed. (business)
  • Were it not for your help, I would have failed.
Contrast: inverted forms are economical and prestigious in formal communication.
adv_u85.exc: If I were you — formal; if I was you is acceptable colloquially.
mistakes: If I was the boss, I would change things. (formally) → If I were the boss…
I wish I was taller. (formally) → I wish I were taller.
Imagine he was here. → Imagine he were here. — subjunctive.

adv_u86.text: Unit 86 – If not and unless; if and whether
adv_u86.note: Unless it rains / I don't know whether
adv_u86.exp:
Unit 86 — if not, unless; if vs. whether.

Phenomenon: often-confused conjunctions with different semantic restrictions.

Unless vs. if not:
  • Unless = except if, advances the one possible exception: I won't go unless you come.
  • Unless cannot replace "if X doesn't happen" in the subjunctive: I'd be upset if you didn't come, not unless you came.
If vs. whether:
  • Whether in indirect questions (alternative to if, more formal): I don't know whether/if he'll come.
  • After a preposition — only whether: It depends on whether…
  • Before a to-inf — only whether: I'm not sure whether to go.
  • With or not adjacent — only whether…or not: whether or not she comes.
  • As subject — only whether: Whether he comes is unclear.
Examples:
  • Unless it rains, we'll have a picnic.
  • The question is whether they will agree.
Contrast: double negation with unless is an error: unless you don't come.
adv_u86.exc: Unless = except if; do not use it with unlikely/unreal conditions: Unless I were you → If I weren't you.
mistakes: Unless you don't come → Unless you come. — unless is already negative.
I don't know if or not he will come. → I don't know whether or not he will come.
Whether you like, tell me. → Whether you like it or not, tell me.

adv_u87.text: Unit 87 - How to combine parts of a complex sentence
adv_u87.exp:
Unit 87 — linking words and text organisation.

Phenomenon: connectors provide logical coherence of text — a key to academic and business style.

Functional groups:
  • Addition: moreover, furthermore, in addition, besides, what's more.
  • Contrast: however, nevertheless, nonetheless, on the other hand, in contrast, conversely, on the contrary (to correct — not = "on the contrary").
  • Consequence: therefore, consequently, as a result, thus, hence, accordingly.
  • Exemplification: for instance, for example, such as, namely, in particular.
  • Summarising: in short, in brief, to sum up, overall, in conclusion.
  • Time: meanwhile, subsequently, previously, simultaneously.
Punctuation: connectors usually require a comma after them and a semicolon/full stop before: It rained; however, we went out.

Contrast: on the contrary (refutation) ≠ on the other hand (alternative point of view). Besides is more conversational than moreover. Thus and hence are formal, typical of scientific prose.
adv_u87.exc: However / therefore / moreover connect sentences through ; or a full stop: It rained; however, we went out.
mistakes: It rained, however we went out. → It rained; however, we went out.
Therefore of the rain → Because of the rain / Therefore, we stayed in.
In contrary → On the contrary / In contrast.

adv_u88.text: Unit 88 – Prepositions of place and movement
adv_u88.note: Along / across / past / through - subtle differences
adv_u88.exp:
Unit 88 — prepositions of place and motion.

Phenomenon: subtle differences between similar prepositions, critical for accuracy of description.

Pairs:
  • Across — crossing a surface, from one edge to another: walk across the street/field.
  • Through — through a three-dimensional space surrounded on the sides: walk through the forest/tunnel/crowd.
  • Along — along a linear object: walk along the river/road.
  • Past — past: walk past the shop.
  • Towards — in the direction of (not necessarily reaching): walk towards the door.
  • Over — on top of, across an obstacle: jump over the fence / fly over the city.
  • Under / beneath / below — differences in nuance of position.
  • Against — physically opposite or metaphorically "against".
Examples:
  • The dog ran across the lawn.
  • The river flows through the valley.
Contrast: above/over — position higher; over can also mean "across an obstacle", while above means only a static position.
adv_u88.exc: Across = crossing a surface; through = inside/through a three-dimensional space.
mistakes: Walk across the forest → Walk through the forest.
Swim through the river → Swim across the river.
Pass by the bridge (if along the bridge) → Pass over the bridge.

adv_u89.note: Between two / among many - and exceptions
adv_u89.exp:
Unit 89 — between and among.

Phenomenon: not a mechanical rule of "two/three+", but a logic of clear relationships against an indefinite group.

Rules:
  • Between — with clearly defined, individualised participants (even 3+): a treaty between the three countries, choose between five options.
  • Among — with an indefinite group of 3+ or when the focus is membership in a set: among the crowd, among friends.
  • Amongst — a literary variant of among.
Idioms: between you and me, among other things, between a rock and a hard place, divide X between Y and Z.

Examples:
  • The disagreement between the three partners…
  • She was among the finalists.
Contrast: divide between (to each individually) vs distribute among (distribute around a group). After between — object case: between you and me, not between you and I.
adv_u89.exc: Among is also used with two if they are part of a group: among friends.
mistakes: Between three people → Among three people (usually), but between for clear pairs.
Divide among the two of them → Divide between the two of them.
Among you and me → Between you and me.

adv_u90.note: At noon / by then / over the years / throughout
adv_u90.exp:
Unit 90 — prepositions of time at C1.

Phenomenon: an extended set of time prepositions with subtle shades of duration and boundaries.

Advanced prepositions:
  • Over — throughout (emphasising the process): over the last decade, over the years.
  • Throughout — through all of, without exceptions: throughout history, throughout the meeting.
  • Within — within (no later than): within 24 hours, within a week.
  • By — by a moment (deadline): by Friday.
  • Until / till — until a moment (duration): stay until 5.
  • Towards / toward — closer to the end of a period: towards the end of May.
  • Prior to — before (formal): prior to the meeting.
  • Following — after (formal): following the announcement.
Examples:
  • The report must be submitted within two weeks.
  • Support grew steadily throughout the campaign.
Contrast: by Friday (no later than) vs until Friday (duration). In + period (in 2023) vs on + day (on Monday) vs at + point (at noon).
adv_u90.exc: By = by a specific moment; until = up to it (process). stay by 5 → stay until 5.
mistakes: Finish until Friday. → Finish by Friday.
In Monday → On Monday.
At the morning → In the morning.

adv_u91.text: Unit 91 - The preposition except and its variants
adv_u91.note: Except / except for / apart from / but
adv_u91.exp:
Unit 91 — except and variants of exclusion.

Phenomenon: differences between except, except for, apart from, but, besides.

Differences:
  • Except (for) — exception: Everyone came except (for) Tom. Except for is preferred at the beginning of a sentence and after general nouns.
  • Apart from — double function: (1) exception = except for; (2) addition = in addition to: Apart from being expensive, it's slow.
  • But meaning "except" — after all, every, no, nobody, nothing, anybody: nobody but me, nothing but the truth.
  • Besides — in addition (not exclusion): Besides Tom, Anna also came. (Tom came AND Anna too.)
Examples:
  • All the shops were open except (for) the bakery.
  • Apart from English, she speaks French.
Contrast: except (excludes) vs besides (adds) — typical confusion. Forms except of, except from are errors.
adv_u91.exc: Except for often at the beginning; except — in the middle after generalising all, every, no.
mistakes: Everyone except of Tom → Everyone except (for) Tom.
Except he is late, he is nice. → Apart from being late, he is nice.
Nobody but me knew. (colloq. "but me" acceptable) — in formal speech except (for) me.

adv_u92.exp:
Unit 92 — prepositions after verbs.

Phenomenon: verbs govern specific prepositions; changing the preposition changes the meaning.

Verbs with a changing preposition:
  • Agree with (someone) / agree to (a proposal) / agree on (something specific): I agree with you / I agreed to the plan / We agreed on Friday.
  • Talk to (whom) / talk about (what) / talk over (discuss).
  • Charge with (accuse) / charge for (charge payment).
  • Apply to (address) / apply for (apply for).
  • Consist of / in / with — different meanings.
Verbs without a preposition (typical error — add an extra one): discuss, enter, marry, approach, lack, reach, contact, answer, attack, attend, resemble: discuss the issue, not discuss about.

Examples:
  • She applied for the position.
  • The success depends on effort.
Contrast: listen to (needs a preposition), but hear (without a preposition). Explain to me, not explain me — obligatory to.
adv_u92.exc: Discuss / enter / marry / approach do not take a preposition: discuss about → discuss.
mistakes: Explain me this. → Explain this to me.
Listen music → Listen to music.
Depend of → Depend on.

adv_u93.note: Effect on / cause of / exception to / attitude towards
adv_u93.exp:
Unit 93 — prepositions after nouns.

Phenomenon: nouns form fixed collocations with prepositions, especially important for the written C1 level.

Collocations:
  • Reason for, cause of, effect on, impact on, influence on.
  • Solution to, answer to, response to, approach to, key to.
  • Increase in, decrease in, change in, rise in, fall in.
  • Attitude to(wards), reaction to, objection to.
  • Lack of, shortage of, absence of, advantage of, disadvantage of.
  • Exception to, alternative to, access to.
  • Difference between, contrast between, connection between.
  • Relationship with, connection with, problem with, trouble with.
After the preposition: a noun or gerund: the reason for postponing the meeting.

Examples:
  • There has been a significant increase in applications.
  • We need a solution to this problem.
Contrast: many L1 pairs translate with a single preposition, but in English they differ: Russian "reason for X" → reason for X, not reason of X.
adv_u93.exc: Reason for / solution to / attitude towards / increase in — fixed collocations.
mistakes: The reason of the delay → The reason for the delay.
Solution of the problem → Solution to the problem.
Attitude to him (acceptable) / attitude towards him — equivalent; attitude against is an error.

adv_u94.exp:
Unit 94 — phrasal verbs (two-word and three-word).

Phenomenon: idiomatic combinations of a verb with particle(s), often opaque in meaning.

Types:
  • Intransitive (no object): break down, take off, come round.
  • Separable transitive — with a noun before or after the particle, with a pronoun — only between: turn the TV on / turn on the TV / turn it on, not turn on it.
  • Inseparable transitive: look after (the kids), come across (a book), get over (illness).
  • Three-word (always inseparable): look forward to, come up with, put up with, run out of, get away with, make up for, catch up with, stand up for.
After a three-word: a noun or gerund: I look forward to seeing you.

Register: phrasal verbs are conversational; their formal equivalents are usually of Latin origin: put off = postpone, look into = investigate, find out = discover.

Contrast: get over (recover) vs get on with (get along). C1 requires active command of 200+ phrasal verbs.
adv_u94.exc: Three-word phrasal verbs are inseparable: put up with him, not put him up with.
mistakes: Look after for the kids → Look after the kids.
I put up with it him → I put up with him.
Turn the TV on it → Turn it on. — pronoun between the verb and the particle.

adv_u95.text: Unit 95 - There is, there was, etc.
adv_u95.note: There + extended structures in academic text
adv_u95.exp:
Unit 95 — constructions with there is/was/has been.

Phenomenon: there as a formal subject — a central tool for introducing new information.

Basic patterns: There is/are + NP + location/time: There is a problem with the system.

Extended C1 constructions:
  • There appears/seems to be: There seems to be a misunderstanding.
  • There is said/believed/thought/known to be: There are believed to be several causes.
  • There is bound/likely/certain to be: There is likely to be opposition.
  • There tends to be / there happens to be / there used to be.
  • Idioms: There is no point in doing, there is no need to, there is no way, there is no knowing/telling.
Agreement: with the nearest noun: There is a book and two pens.

Examples:
  • There's no point in arguing.
  • There have been several complaints.
Contrast: there introduces existence; it — used to refer to a clause: It is reported that…, not There is reported…
adv_u95.exc: There is + sing.; there are + pl. In conversation: there's three of us (non-standard).
mistakes: It is a book on the table. → There is a book on the table.
There is many people. → There are many people.
There has been reported that… → It has been reported that… — for reports use it.

adv_u96.text: Unit 96 - It as a formal subject (lesson 1)
adv_u96.note: It is clear that / It was decided that
adv_u96.exp:
Unit 96 — formal it (lesson 1).

Phenomenon: it as a dummy subject, allowing a heavy clause to be moved to the end — key to objective, impersonal style.

Constructions:
  • It + be + adj + that-clause: It is clear/obvious/important/likely that he's right.
  • It + be + adj + to-inf: It is important to consider all options.
  • It + be + V3 + that-clause: It is reported/said/believed/assumed that…
  • It + was + decided/agreed/arranged + that (often with should): It was agreed that all members should attend.
  • It + takes + time + to-inf: It takes effort to learn a language.
Function: shifts focus to the action/evaluation, avoids a cumbersome subject.

Examples:
  • It is widely acknowledged that climate change is accelerating.
  • It is essential that everyone be informed.
Register: the basis of academic and news style. Contrast: It is (with a clause) vs There is (existence).
adv_u96.exc: It formal subject with a that-clause / to-inf. Do not confuse with referential it.
mistakes: Is clear that he is right. → It is clear that he is right.
That he lied is obvious (formal). → It is obvious that he lied.
It was decided he to go. → It was decided that he should go.

adv_u97.text: Unit 97 - It as a formal subject (lesson 2)
adv_u97.note: It seems / It appears / It turns out
adv_u97.exp:
Unit 97 — formal it (lesson 2): linking verbs.

Phenomenon: a group of verbs presenting conclusions, assumptions and coincidences in a formal register.

Patterns:
  • It seems/appears + (that-clause / as if / as though): It seems that he was right / It seems as if nobody cared.
  • It turns out / it emerges / it transpires + that: It turns out she was lying.
  • It happens / so happens that: As it happens, I know her personally.
  • It follows that / it means that / it implies that — conclusions.
  • With to-inf: He seems to be tired / He appears to know the answer.
Examples:
  • It would seem that the plan has failed. (hedged)
  • It so happens that I have the key.
Register: it transpires/emerges — highly formal; it turns out — neutral.

Contrast: He seems to be tired (direct subject) vs It seems (that) he is tired (formal it) — synonymous but structurally different.
adv_u97.exc: It seems/appears + that-clause or to-inf: He seems to be tired / It seems that he is tired.
mistakes: It seems he to be late. → He seems to be late / It seems he is late.
It turns that he lied. → It turns out that he lied.
It happens me to know him. → I happen to know him.

adv_u98.note: Cleft sentences: What I need is… / It was Tom who…
adv_u98.exp:
Unit 98 — cleft sentences.

Phenomenon: highlighting key information by reformatting the sentence — a powerful rhetorical tool.

Types:
  • It-cleft: It + be + focused element + that/who-clause: It was Maria who called me (not someone else); It was yesterday that I saw him (specifically yesterday); It is precision that matters.
  • Wh-cleft (pseudo-cleft): What/Where/Why + clause + be + focus: What I need is a break. What she did was (to) resign.
  • Reversed wh-cleft: A break is what I need.
  • All-cleft: All I want is peace. All you have to do is call.
Examples:
  • It wasn't the money that upset him — it was the principle.
  • What we need now is a new strategy.
Function: contrastive focus, refutation, emphasis.

Contrast: the base Maria called me is neutral; It was Maria who called me — emphasis on Maria. Reverse inversion is rare.
adv_u98.exc: Cleft constructions: What I need is rest / It was Tom who broke it.
mistakes: <s>What I need is to rest and to eat</s> — acceptable, but mind parallelism.
It was Tom that did broke it. → It was Tom who broke it.
What I want is you come. → What I want is for you to come.

adv_u99.note: Never have I seen / Only then did I realize
adv_u99.exp:
Unit 99 — inversion (lesson 1).

Phenomenon: reverse subject-aux order after a fronted negative or restrictive element — a marker of a high formal register.

Inversion triggers:
  • Negative/semi-negative adverbs: Never, Rarely, Seldom, Hardly, Scarcely, Little, Nowhere.
  • Only + adverbial: Only then, only after, only by, only when, only if.
  • Not only…(but also).
  • At no time, under no circumstances, in no way, on no account.
Structure: Triggering element + aux + subject + main verb (as in a question): Never have I seen such chaos.

Examples:
  • Rarely does one find such dedication.
  • Only after the meeting did I understand.
  • Not only did he arrive late, but he also forgot the papers.
Register: literary, oratorical, academic. In speech it sounds archaic or emphatic.

Contrast: I have never seen it (neutral) vs Never have I seen it (emphatic).
adv_u99.exc: Inversion with negative adverbs: Never have I seen / Only then did I realise. Question order.
mistakes: Never I have seen such a thing. → Never have I seen such a thing.
Only then I realised. → Only then did I realise.
Rarely he comes. (formal) → Rarely does he come.

el_u01.text: Unit 1 – Am, is, are and Present Simple
el_u01.note: am / is / are · I work, she works
el_u01.exp:
Unit 1 — the verb to be in the present and the basic form of the Present Simple. Used to describe facts, properties and habitual actions.

Structure:
  • I am / he, she, it is / we, you, they are
  • Present Simple: I/you/we/they work, he/she/it works (+ ending -s/-es)
Examples:
  • 'I am a student.'
  • 'She is my sister.'
  • 'They work in a bank.'
  • 'He speaks French.'
Short forms: I'm, he's, she's, it's, we're, they're. To be describes a state/possession, while the Present Simple describes regular actions.
el_u01.exc: The pronoun I is always combined only with am, never with is/are. Short forms: I'm, he's, she's, it's, we're, they're. In spoken English, am not is not contracted (there is no form amn't); we use I'm not.
mistakes: I is a student. → I am a student. — with I always use am.
She work every day. → She works every day. — in the third person singular, add -s.
He are tired. → He is tired. — with he/she/it use is.
They is happy. → They are happy. — with we/you/they use are.

el_u02.note: Is she a teacher? Are they ready?
el_u02.exp:
Unit 2 — questions and negatives with the verb to be. In questions am/is/are goes before the subject (inversion); in negatives we add not.

Structure:
  • ? Am/Is/Are + subject + …?
  • — subject + am/is/are + not …
  • Short answers: Yes, I am. / No, I'm not.
Examples:
  • 'Is she a teacher?'
  • 'Are you tired?'
  • 'I am not ready.'
  • 'Where are the keys?'
Contractions: isn't, aren't. After question words (what, where, who) is/are follows immediately.
el_u02.exc: In negative short answers we use contractions isn't/aren't, but in affirmative short answers contractions are not used: Yes, I am (not Yes, I'm). There is no negative contraction with am — we say I'm not.
mistakes: You are a doctor? → Are you a doctor? — in questions are comes before the subject.
Yes, I'm. → Yes, I am. — no contraction in affirmative short answers.
Where she is? → Where is she? — after a question word is/are follows immediately.
Is they ready? → Are they ready? — with they you need are.

el_u03.note: She is reading. They are working.
el_u03.exp:
Unit 3 — Present Continuous for actions happening at the moment of speaking or in the current period.

Formula: am/is/are + V-ing.
Markers: now, at the moment, right now, Look!, Listen!

Examples:
  • 'I am reading a book now.'
  • 'She is cooking dinner.'
  • 'The children are playing in the garden.'
  • 'Look! It is raining.'
Spelling of -ing: write→writing (drop e), sit→sitting (double the consonant), lie→lying (ie→y). Also used for temporary situations: I am staying with my aunt this week.
el_u03.exc: Stative verbs (know, like, want, love, understand, need, believe) are usually not used in the Continuous. When adding -ing: lie→lying, die→dying (ie→y); sit→sitting, run→running (doubling the consonant); write→writing (-e is dropped).
mistakes: I am knowing the answer. → I know the answer. — stative verbs are not used in the Continuous.
She is writeing a letter. → She is writing a letter. — -e is dropped before -ing.
He running now. → He is running now. — the auxiliary is is needed.
They are siting here. → They are sitting here. — the final consonant is doubled.

el_u04.text: Unit 4 - Questions in the Present Continuous
el_u04.note: Are you working? What is she doing?
el_u04.exp:
Unit 4 — questions and negatives in the Present Continuous. The auxiliary am/is/are is moved to the front.

Structure:
  • ? Am/Is/Are + subject + V-ing …?
  • — subject + am/is/are + not + V-ing …
  • Wh-question: Wh- + am/is/are + subject + V-ing?
Examples:
  • 'Are you working now?' — Yes, I am. / No, I'm not.
  • 'What is she doing?'
  • 'Where are the kids playing?'
  • 'He isn't sleeping.'
Unlike the Simple, there is no auxiliary do here — be itself plays that role.
el_u04.exc: In questions with stative verbs the Continuous is not used: Do you know? (not Are you knowing?). In short answers contractions are only used in the negative: Yes, I am / No, I'm not.
mistakes: What you are doing? → What are you doing? — the auxiliary goes before the subject.
Where he is going? → Where is he going? — direct word order is not allowed in a question.
Are you knowing him? → Do you know him? — know is not used in the Continuous.
Yes, I'm. → Yes, I am. — no contraction in an affirmative short answer.

el_u05.note: I work. She works. Do you work?
el_u05.exp:
Unit 5 — Present Simple for regular actions, habits, general facts and schedules.

Formula: I/you/we/they + V1; he/she/it + V + -s/-es.
Markers: always, usually, often, sometimes, never, every day, on Mondays.

Examples:
  • 'I drink coffee every morning.'
  • 'She works in a hospital.'
  • 'Water boils at 100 °C.'
  • 'The train leaves at 6 p.m.'
Third-person spelling: after -s/-sh/-ch/-x/-o → -es (watches, goes); after consonant + y → -ies (study→studies).
el_u05.exc: Special third-person forms: go→goes, do→does, have→has. After -s, -sh, -ch, -x, -o → -es: watches, washes, goes. After consonant + y → -ies: study→studies, but play→plays (after a vowel).
mistakes: She have a car. → She has a car. — in the third person have becomes has.
He studys English. → He studies English. — after a consonant y changes to ies.
My brother go to school. → My brother goes to school. — after o you need -es.
She watch TV every evening. → She watches TV every evening. — after -ch add -es.

el_u06.text: Unit 6 - Negations in the Present Simple (I don't)
el_u06.note: I don't like it. She doesn't work.
el_u06.exp:
Unit 6 — negatives in the Present Simple using don't / doesn't.

Formula:
  • I/you/we/they + do not (don't) + V1
  • he/she/it + does not (doesn't) + V1
After doesn't the main verb loses its -s ending.

Examples:
  • 'I don't like fish.'
  • 'She doesn't speak Chinese.'
  • 'They don't live here.'
  • 'My brother doesn't work on Sundays.'
Unlike to be, ordinary verbs require the auxiliary do/does in the negative.
el_u06.exc: After doesn't/don't the main verb is in the bare infinitive only — without -s and without a past form. In speech we use contracted forms; the full do not/does not sounds formal or emphatic.
mistakes: She doesn't works here. → She doesn't work here. — after doesn't use the bare infinitive.
I no like coffee. → I don't like coffee. — the negative is formed with don't.
He don't know. → He doesn't know. — with he/she/it use doesn't.
They doesn't speak English. → They don't speak English. — with they use don't.

el_u07.text: Unit 7 - Questions in the Present Simple
el_u07.note: Do you work? Does she like it?
el_u07.exp:
Unit 7 — questions in the Present Simple with do / does.

Formula:
  • ? Do/Does + subject + V1 …?
  • Wh-question: Wh- + do/does + subject + V1?
  • Short answer: Yes, I do. / No, she doesn't.
Examples:
  • 'Do you speak English?'
  • 'Does he like music?'
  • 'Where do they live?'
  • 'What does she do?'
Exception: when who/what is the subject, no auxiliary is needed: Who lives here?
el_u07.exc: After does the main verb is in the bare infinitive without -s. With who/what as the subject no auxiliary is needed: Who lives here? (not Who does live here?).
mistakes: Does she works? → Does she work? — after does use the bare infinitive.
Where she lives? → Where does she live? — you need the auxiliary does.
Do he like tea? → Does he like tea? — with he use does.
Yes, I like. → Yes, I do. — the auxiliary is repeated in a short answer.

el_u08.text: Unit 8 - Present Simple vs Present Continuous
el_u08.note: I work every day. I'm working now.
el_u08.exp:
Unit 8 — comparison of Present Simple and Present Continuous.

Simple — regular/permanent actions, facts: always, usually, every day.
Continuous — at the moment of speaking or a temporary situation: now, at the moment, today, this week.

Examples:
  • 'I usually drive to work, but today I'm taking the bus.'
  • 'She speaks three languages.' (fact)
  • 'She is speaking to her boss.' (right now)
Stative verbs (know, like, want, need, believe, love) are used only in the Simple, even if the situation is "now".
el_u08.exc: Some verbs change meaning in the Continuous: I think (believe) / I'm thinking (pondering); I have (own) / I'm having lunch (eating); I see (perceive) / I'm seeing him (dating).
mistakes: I am knowing her. → I know her. — a stative verb uses the Simple.
Look! He runs. → Look! He is running. — the action is happening right now.
She is going to school every day. → She goes to school every day. — a regular action uses the Simple.
I am wanting a coffee. → I want a coffee. — want is not used in the Continuous.

el_u09.text: Unit 9 – I have got or I have
el_u09.note: Have you got a car? / Do you have a car?
el_u09.exp:
Unit 9 — have got and have in the sense of possession (ownership, family ties, illnesses, appearance).

Formula:
  • BrE: I've got / she's got; ? Have you got…?; — I haven't got…
  • AmE: I have / she has; ? Do you have…?; — I don't have…
Examples:
  • 'I've got two sisters.' / 'I have two sisters.'
  • 'She's got blue eyes.'
  • 'Have you got a pen?'
  • 'He doesn't have a car.'
In the past tense only had (without got): I had a bike as a child.
el_u09.exc: In American English have got is almost never used. In the past tense only had (no had got in this sense): I had a car in 2010. With have in the sense of actions (have breakfast) got is not added.
mistakes: Do you have got a car? → Have you got a car? or Do you have a car? — you cannot mix the two constructions.
I haven't a car. → I don't have a car. or I haven't got a car.
She has got breakfast at 8. → She has breakfast at 8. — have got = possession, not an action.
He has got a headache yesterday. → He had a headache yesterday. — in the past only had.

el_u10.text: Unit 10 – Was / were (past tense to be)
el_u10.note: I was tired. They were at home.
el_u10.exp:
Unit 10 — was / were: the past tense of the verb to be.

Formula:
  • I / he / she / it + was
  • you / we / they + were
  • — wasn't / weren't; ? Was/Were + subject…?
Examples:
  • 'I was at home yesterday.'
  • 'They were very tired.'
  • 'Was she at the party?'
  • 'We weren't ready.'
Markers: yesterday, last week, two days ago, in 2010, when I was a child.
el_u10.exc: In polite/hypothetical sentences were is kept with I/he/she: If I were you…. In all other cases use was for I/he/she/it. Negative contractions: wasn't, weren't.
mistakes: I were at home. → I was at home. — with I in the past use was.
They was tired. → They were tired. — with they use were.
Was you there? → Were you there? — with you always use were.
She weren't happy. → She wasn't happy. — with she use wasn't.

el_u100.note: If I were rich, I would travel.
el_u100.exp:
Unit 100 — Second Conditional: an unreal or unlikely condition in the present/future.

Expresses an imagined or hypothetical situation contrary to reality.

Structure: If + Past Simple, would + bare infinitive.

Important: Past Simple here is 'subjunctive' past, not real past tense.

  • If I had a million dollars, I would travel the world. (In fact I don't have the money.)
  • If I were you, I would apologise.
  • What would you do if you saw a ghost?

With the verb be — formally were for all persons: If he were here, he would help. In speech was is acceptable, but only If I were you is fixed.

Instead of would in the main clause — could, might: If I had time, I could help you.

Difference from First Conditional: First — real possibility (If it rains, I'll stay); Second — a hypothesis, unlikely (If I had wings, I would fly).
el_u100.exc: In formal style were for all persons: If I were you. In colloquial style — was for I/he/she: If I was rich. Only If I were you is firmly fixed.
mistakes: If I would have money → If I had money — would only in the main clause.
If I am rich I would travel → If I were/was rich, I would travel — Past in the condition.
If he had time, he will help → he would help — tense agreement.
If she was you (formally) → If she were you.

el_u101.note: the man who called / the book that I read
el_u101.exp:
Unit 101 — defining relative clauses with who and that/which.

A relative clause specifies which person or thing is meant. It stands immediately after the noun.

Pronoun choice:

  • who — for people: The woman who called is my aunt.
  • that — for people and things: The man that lives next door. The book that I bought.
  • which — only for things: The car which I bought is red.

Omission of the pronoun:

  • If it is the subject of the clause (a verb follows it directly), it cannot be omitted: The man who called me.
  • If it is the object (a new subject + verb follows), it can be omitted: The book (that) I read was interesting.

No repeated subject/object: you cannot say the man who he called — correct is the man who called.

Examples: I have a friend who speaks five languages. The film (which) we saw yesterday was boring.
el_u101.exc: If the relative pronoun is the object, it can be omitted: the book (that) I read. If it is the subject, it cannot be omitted: the man who called, not the man called me.
mistakes: the man which called → the man who/that called — for people.
the book who I read → the book that/which I read — for things.
the woman who she lives here → the woman who lives here — no repeated subject.
the film that I saw it → the film that I saw — no repeated object.

el_u102.note: the place where / the day when / the reason why
el_u102.exp:
Unit 102 — extension of relative clauses: where, when, why, whose.

  • where — about a place: This is the hotel where we stayed.
  • when — about a time: I remember the day when we met.
  • why — about a reason: Tell me the reason why you're late. Often omitted: the reason (why) I called.
  • whose — belonging (for both people and things): The woman whose car was stolen called the police. The book whose cover is red.

Equivalents with prepositions (formal): the house where I live = the house in which I live; the day when we met = the day on which we met.

Omission rules are the same as in Unit 101: the object is omitted, the subject is not.

Distinction: whose ≠ who's (who is / who has). The man whose bag was stolen (whose bag) vs The man who's here (who is here).

Examples: I visited the village where my grandmother was born. That's the woman whose son won the prize.
el_u102.exc: Whose is used for both people and things: the book whose cover is torn. Where can be replaced with in/at which in formal style: the house in which she lives.
mistakes: the place which I was born → the place where I was born.
the day which we met (about a time) → the day when we met.
the man who's car → the man whose car — who's = who is.
the reason because → the reason why / the reason (that).

el_u103.text: Unit 103 – Prepositions of time: at, on, in
el_u103.note: at 5pm / on Monday / in March / in 2020
el_u103.exp:
Unit 103 — prepositions of time at, on, in.

Distribution by time units:

  • at — exact time, points: at 7 o'clock, at noon, at midnight, at night, at the weekend (BrE), at Christmas, at Easter, at the moment.
  • on — days and dates: on Monday, on 5 May, on my birthday, on Christmas Day, on New Year's Eve, on a rainy day, on the morning of 1 May.
  • in — periods: months, years, centuries, seasons, parts of the day: in January, in 2024, in the 21st century, in summer, in the morning / afternoon / evening.

Important exceptions:

  • at night, but in the morning / afternoon / evening.
  • at the weekend (BrE) vs on the weekend (AmE).
  • Before this, last, next, every, all the preposition is dropped: see you next Monday, last summer, every day, all day.

Meaning of in = 'in' (future): I'll be back in an hour.

Example: We met on Friday at 6 o'clock in the evening.
el_u103.exc: No preposition before this/that/next/last/every: see you next Monday (not on next Monday). At night, but in the night (during the night).
mistakes: in Monday → on Monday — days of the week.
at March → in March — months.
on 2020 → in 2020 — years.
on next week → next week — no preposition.

el_u104.note: from 9 to 5 / until Friday / since Monday / for 3 hours
el_u104.exp:
Unit 104 — prepositions of time periods: from … to, until, since, for.

from A to/till B — marking an interval:

  • I work from 9 to 5.
  • The shop is open from Monday to Friday.

until / till — 'until a certain moment' (the action continues and ends):

  • Wait until Friday.
  • I'll be here till 6.

since — 'from a certain moment' (starting point up to now); usually with Present Perfect:

  • I've lived here since 2010.
  • She has been sick since Monday.

for — 'for, during' (duration); with any tenses:

  • I lived there for five years.
  • We've been waiting for two hours.

Key contrast since vs for: since + moment (since Monday, since 2020, since I was a child); for + period (for two days, for a long time, for ages).

Related: by + moment = 'not later than' (by Friday); before + moment = 'before' (before lunch).
el_u104.exc: Since + moment of time (since Monday), for + period (for two days). Until/till are interchangeable; by — by a moment (by Friday = not later than).
mistakes: since two hours → for two hours — duration.
for Monday → since Monday — starting point.
I'll wait till to Friday → until Friday / till Friday — no to.
from 9 till to 5 → from 9 to 5 / from 9 till 5.

el_u105.note: before lunch / after work / during the film
el_u105.exp:
Unit 105 — before, after, during, while linking events in time.

before / after can be both prepositions and conjunctions:

  • Preposition + noun/gerund: after dinner, before leaving.
  • Conjunction + clause: before you leave, after we had finished.
  • In time clauses — Present instead of Future: Call me before you leave (not before you will leave).

during — only a preposition, before a noun ('during something'):

  • during the film, during the holidays, during lunch.
  • I fell asleep during the lecture.

while — only a conjunction, before a clause with subject and verb:

  • while I was watching TV, while she slept.
  • He called while I was cooking.

Key contrast: during + noun; while + full clause. You cannot say during I slept → only while I slept or during my sleep.

Difference during / for: during = when exactly (during the war), for = how long (for five years).
el_u105.exc: During + noun, while + subordinate clause. Not allowed: during I was reading. Possible: during the meeting or while I was in the meeting.
mistakes: during I slept → while I slept — before a verb use while.
while the film → during the film — before a noun use during.
after to leave → after leaving / after I leave — not infinitive with to.
before I will go → before I go — no will.

el_u106.text: Unit 106 – Prepositions of place: in, at, on (lesson 1)
el_u106.note: in the room / at the door / on the table
el_u106.exp:
Unit 106 — basic prepositions of place in, at, on.

in — inside a bounded space/volume:

  • in the room, in the box, in the garden, in a car, in the water, in Paris, in the sky.
  • The keys are in my bag.

on — on a surface (on top, on the side):

  • on the table, on the wall, on the floor, on the ceiling, on the right / left, on the first floor, on a bus / train / plane.
  • There's a picture on the wall.

at — at a point, at a specific place (without emphasis on volume):

  • at the door, at the bus stop, at the traffic lights, at the top / bottom, at home, at work, at the station.
  • Meet me at the entrance.

Contrasting pairs:

  • in the corner (corner of a room) vs on the corner (street corner) vs at the corner (by the corner).
  • in the bus (inside) vs on the bus (travelling by bus).
  • in bed (asleep) vs on the bed (sitting on top).
el_u106.exc: At home, at work, at school — no article. On the bus/train, but in the car/taxi. In bed (asleep), on the bed (sitting on top).
mistakes: in the door → at the door — by the door, not inside.
on the room → in the room.
in the street corner → on the corner.
at the picture on the wall → in the picture / on the wall.

el_u107.text: Unit 107 – Prepositions of place: in, at, on (lesson 2)
el_u107.note: at work / in hospital / on holiday
el_u107.exp:
Unit 107 — fixed expressions with prepositions of place (Unit 2).

Memorised as blocks — the logic is not always literal.

With at:

  • at home, at work, at school, at university, at the airport, at the station, at a concert / party / meeting.
  • at the top / bottom, at the end of (the street).

With in:

  • in hospital, in prison, in bed (as a state).
  • in the middle, in the front / back of (the car).
  • in a photo, in a picture, in a book, in a newspaper, in a queue / row / line.

With on:

  • on holiday, on business, on a trip / tour / cruise / journey.
  • on TV, on the radio, on the phone, on the internet, on the menu, on page 5, on a farm, on an island.

Examples: He's on holiday in Spain. I saw it on TV last night. There are three people in the picture.

Contrasting pairs: in hospital (patient, BrE) vs in the hospital (AmE / visitor); at the end (at the end of something) vs in the end (finally).
el_u107.exc: In hospital (BrE, as a patient) vs in the hospital (AmE or as a visitor). On holiday (BrE), on vacation (AmE).
mistakes: in holiday → on holiday.
at the bed (to sleep) → in bed.
in work → at work.
on the end (in conclusion) → in the end (finally) / at the end (at the end of something).

el_u108.text: Unit 108 – Prepositions of place: to, in, at (lesson 3)
el_u108.note: go to school / arrive in London / arrive at the station
el_u108.exp:
Unit 108 — prepositions of direction and arrival: to, in, at.

to — direction of movement (to where):

  • go to school / work / London, travel to Japan, drive to the airport, send a letter to John.

arrive — a special case, without to:

  • arrive in + city/country/large area: arrive in London, arrive in Russia.
  • arrive at + building/specific place/event: arrive at the station, arrive at the hotel, arrive at work, arrive at the party.

Important exceptions without a preposition (home — adverb):

  • go home, come home, arrive home, get home, leave home.
  • Also with here, there: come here, go there.

Examples:

  • We arrived in Paris on Monday and arrived at our hotel in the afternoon.
  • She went home after work.
  • I'm going to the supermarket.

The verb get to = 'to get to': How do you get to work? (And here too get home is without to.)
el_u108.exc: Arrive in + city/country, arrive at + place/building. Go home — no preposition (home as an adverb). Not allowed: to home.
mistakes: arrive to London → arrive in London.
arrive to the station → arrive at the station.
go to home → go home.
come in Paris → come to Paris — direction.

el_u109.text: Unit 109 – Under, behind, opposite and others
el_u109.note: under the table / behind the door / opposite the bank
el_u109.exp:
Unit 109 — prepositions of relative position.

  • under (under) / over, above (over): The cat is under the table. The lamp hangs above the desk.
  • behind (behind) / in front of (in front of): A garden behind the house. He parked in front of the shop.
  • opposite (opposite): The bank is opposite the post office.
  • next to / beside / by (next to): She sat next to me.
  • between (between two) / among (among many): between you and me, among the trees.

Subtle differences:

  • above — higher (not necessarily directly over); over — directly above, across: a plane flew over the city.
  • below ↔ above; under ↔ over.
  • beside (next to) ≠ besides (besides, in addition).

Compound prepositions are written in full: in front of (not in front), next to, on top of. Opposite is used without to: opposite the bank.

Examples: There's a mirror above the sink. The cat is sleeping between the two cushions.
el_u109.exc: Between — between two; among — among many. Above (higher, not necessarily directly over) vs over (directly above / across). Beside (next to) ≠ besides (besides).
mistakes: between the crowd → among the crowd — many people.
in front the house → in front of the house — compound preposition.
opposite to the bank → opposite the bank — no to.
next the shop → next to the shop.

el_u11.note: I worked. She went. They saw.
el_u11.exp:
Unit 11 — Past Simple for finished actions in the past with a specified or implied time.

Formula:
  • Regular: V + -ed (worked, played, lived)
  • Irregular: special V2 forms (go→went, see→saw, buy→bought, have→had)
Markers: yesterday, last Monday, two hours ago, in 1999, when I was young.

Examples:
  • 'I worked late yesterday.'
  • 'She went to Paris last summer.'
  • 'We saw a great film.'
  • 'He bought a new phone.'
Spelling: stop→stopped, study→studied, like→liked.
el_u11.exc: Spelling of -ed: stop→stopped (doubling), study→studied (y→i), like→liked (drop e). Irregular verbs must be learned: go→went, see→saw, buy→bought, have→had.
mistakes: She goed home. → She went home. — go is an irregular verb.
I buyed a book. → I bought a book. — buy is irregular.
He stoped the car. → He stopped the car. — the final consonant is doubled.
They studyed hard. → They studied hard. — after a consonant y→i.

el_u110.text: Unit 110 – Up, over, through and prepositions of direction
el_u110.note: go through the tunnel / walk up the stairs
el_u110.exp:
Unit 110 — prepositions of direction of movement.

They describe a trajectory:

  • up (up) / down (down): walk up the hill, run down the stairs.
  • into (into) / out of (out of): go into the room, get out of the car.
  • onto (onto) / off (off): jump onto the table, fall off the horse.
  • across (across a surface): walk across the street / the bridge.
  • through (through a volume): drive through the tunnel, walk through the forest.
  • along (along): walk along the river.
  • past (past): walk past the shop.
  • round / around (around): fly around the world.
  • over (over, jumping / above): jump over the fence.
  • towards (in the direction of): She walked towards me.

Subtle difference:

  • across — across a flat surface (street, bridge, field).
  • through — through a three-dimensional space or inside (forest, tunnel, crowd).

Contrast in / into: in — position (she is in the room), into — movement (she went into the room).
el_u110.exc: Across — from one side of a flat surface to another, through — through a volume (forest, tunnel). Over/above differ: jump over (jump across), hang above (hang over).
mistakes: through the street → across the street — a street is crossed with across.
across the tunnel → through the tunnel.
go in the room (movement) → go into the room.
get out the car → get out of the car.

el_u111.text: Unit 111 - On, at, by, with, about
el_u111.note: by car / with a pen / about the problem / on TV
el_u111.exp:
Unit 111 — polysemous prepositions on, at, by, with, about.

by:

  • Means of transport (no article): by car, by bus, by train, by plane, by taxi. But: on foot, not by foot.
  • Agent of the passive voice: written by Tolstoy.
  • Manner: by mistake, by accident, by chance, pay by credit card.

with — instrument, accompaniment:

  • write with a pen, cut with a knife, see with one's eyes.
  • Accompaniment: come with me, coffee with milk.

about — topic:

  • talk / think / read / know / worry about: She's thinking about her future.

on — channel / state:

  • on TV, on the radio, on the phone, on the internet, on holiday, on business, on a diet, on purpose.

Contrasting pairs: write with a pen (instrument) vs write by hand (manner); a book about history (topic) vs a book by a historian (author).

Examples: She paid for the taxi by credit card. He hit the ball with a bat. I heard it on the radio.
el_u111.exc: By bus/car — no article; but in my car, on the bus — with an article. On foot (on foot), not by foot. With/by: written by hand vs written with a pen.
mistakes: by the car → by car / in the car.
with bus → by bus.
by foot → on foot.
talk on the problem → talk about the problem.

el_u112.note: good at / interested in / afraid of
el_u112.exp:
Unit 112 — fixed 'adjective + preposition' collocations.

They must be memorised together with the preposition. After the preposition comes a noun or a gerund (-ing).

Frequent collocations:

  • good / bad at: She is good at maths. He is bad at lying.
  • interested in: I'm interested in history.
  • afraid / scared of: afraid of spiders, afraid of flying.
  • sorry for / about: sorry for being late, sorry about the mistake.
  • different from: My life is different from yours.
  • similar to: Your coat is similar to mine.
  • married / engaged to: She is married to an actor.
  • proud / ashamed of: proud of her children.
  • tired of: tired of waiting.
  • bored with / by: bored with the lesson.
  • responsible for: responsible for the report.
  • full of: full of water.
  • famous for: famous for its cheese.

Examples: I'm tired of listening to him. She's really good at playing the piano.
el_u112.exc: Different from (BrE/standard), different than (AmE), different to (BrE colloquial). Afraid of + noun/gerund: afraid of spiders, afraid of flying.
mistakes: good in math → good at math.
interested on music → interested in music.
afraid from dogs → afraid of dogs.
married with her → married to her.

el_u113.note: wait for / listen to / look at / look for
el_u113.exp:
Unit 113 — fixed 'verb + preposition' collocations (prepositional verbs).
