The preposition is inseparable from the verb and determines the meaning. After the preposition comes a noun or a gerund.

Key collocations:

  • listen to: listen to music / to the teacher. (But hear is without a preposition: I heard a noise.)
  • wait for: wait for the bus / for you.
  • look at (to look at), look for (to search for), look after (to take care of), look forward to (to look forward to).
  • talk / speak to / with + somebody; talk about (about something).
  • think of / about: What do you think of my idea?
  • depend on: It depends on the weather.
  • belong to: This pen belongs to me.
  • apply for (a job).
  • believe in, happen to, pay for, ask for, laugh at.

In questions the preposition usually stays at the end: Who are you waiting for? What are you talking about?

Contrasting pairs: look at (a glance) / look for (a search) / look after (care) / look up (in a dictionary).
el_u113.exc: Listen to always with to, but hear — no preposition: I heard the noise. Look for (to search) ≠ look at (to look at) ≠ look after (to take care of).
mistakes: listen music → listen to music.
wait you → wait for you.
look the picture → look at the picture.
depend of → depend on.

el_u114.note: turn on/off, put on/take off, get up
el_u114.exp:
Unit 114 — introduction to phrasal verbs: verb + short particle changes the base meaning.

Basic examples:

  • turn on / off — to switch on/off: Turn on the TV.
  • put on / take off — to put on/take off: Put on your coat.
  • get up — to get up: I get up at 7.
  • sit down, stand up, lie down — sit down, stand up, lie down.
  • wake up — to wake up.
  • give up — to quit (a habit): She gave up smoking.
  • look up — to look up in a dictionary.
  • write down — to write down.

Separable vs inseparable:

  • With separable phrasal verbs, a noun may stand either between the verb and the particle or after it: turn the TV off = turn off the TV.
  • A pronoun (it, them, him) always goes between: turn it on, not turn on it; take them off.

Examples: Can you turn the light off? → Turn it off. I took off my shoes = I took my shoes off.
el_u114.exc: With a pronoun object it is placed between the verb and the particle: turn it on, not turn on it. With a noun — either order is possible.
mistakes: turn on it → turn it on — pronoun in the middle.
I put on my coat on → I put on my coat / I put my coat on.
get up from bed (for 'to wake up') → get up without a preposition.
Take off them → Take them off.

el_u115.note: break down, carry on, find out, look forward to
el_u115.exp:
Unit 115 — continuation of the topic: more complex and idiomatic phrasal verbs.

Frequent items:

  • break down — to break down (of machinery): My car broke down.
  • carry on (with) — to continue: Carry on working.
  • find out — to find out, discover: I found out the truth.
  • give up — to give up, surrender: Don't give up!
  • go on — to continue / to happen: What's going on?
  • look forward to — to look forward to. After to — gerund: I'm looking forward to seeing you.
  • run out of — to run out of: We ran out of milk.
  • set off / set out — to set off: We set off early.
  • get on with — to get on with: She gets on well with her boss.
  • take off — to take off (of a plane).
  • turn up / turn down — to turn up/down the volume or to show up/decline.

Intransitive (no object): break down, set off, go on. Transitive often with an obligatory preposition: run out of sugar, look forward to the weekend. Examples: We set off at dawn and broke down on the motorway. I look forward to meeting you soon.
el_u115.exc: Look forward to — after to always a gerund, not an infinitive: looking forward to seeing you. Run out of — obligatory of before the object: We ran out of milk.
mistakes: I look forward to see you → I look forward to seeing you — gerund.
find out the truth out → find out the truth / find the truth out.
The car broke (about a breakdown) → The car broke down.

el_u12.text: Unit 12 - Past Simple: Question and Negation
el_u12.note: Did you work? I didn't go.
el_u12.exp:
Unit 12 — questions and negatives in the Past Simple with the auxiliary did.

Formula:
  • ? Did + subject + V1 …?
  • — subject + didn't + V1 …
  • Wh-question: Wh- + did + subject + V1?
After did / didn't the verb is in the bare infinitive, not V2.

Examples:
  • 'Did you see him?'
  • 'She didn't come to the party.'
  • 'Where did they go?'
  • 'What did he say?'
With the verb to be no auxiliary is needed: Were you there?
el_u12.exc: With the verb to be the auxiliary did is not needed: Were you tired? (not Did you be tired?). With who/what as the subject: Who called? — no did.
mistakes: Did she went home? → Did she go home? — after did use the bare infinitive.
I didn't saw him. → I didn't see him. — after didn't use the bare infinitive.
Where you went? → Where did you go? — did is needed.
Did he was there? → Was he there? — with to be did is not used.

el_u13.note: I was reading when she called.
el_u13.exp:
Unit 13 — Past Continuous for long actions at a specific moment or period in the past.

Formula: was/were + V-ing.

Examples:
  • 'At 8 p.m. yesterday I was watching TV.'
  • 'They were having dinner when I arrived.'
  • 'What were you doing at 10?'
  • 'She wasn't listening.'
Often used as the background for another action in the Past Simple: a longer action (Continuous) is interrupted by a short one (Simple). Stative verbs are usually in the Past Simple.
el_u13.exc: Stative verbs (know, like, want) are not used in the Past Continuous. The -ing spelling rules are the same: lie→lying, run→running, write→writing.
mistakes: I was knowing the answer. → I knew the answer. — stative verbs use the Simple.
She were reading. → She was reading. — with she use was.
They was playing football. → They were playing football. — with they use were.
What you were doing? → What were you doing? — inversion is required.

el_u14.text: Unit 14 - Past Simple vs Past Continuous
el_u14.note: She was cooking when he arrived.
el_u14.exp:
Unit 14 — Past Simple vs Past Continuous.

Past Simple — a short completed action: He called me.
Past Continuous — an action in progress: I was cooking.

Conjunctions:
  • when + Past Simple — the moment of interruption
  • while + Past Continuous — a parallel or background long action
Examples:
  • 'I was cooking when he called.'
  • 'While she was reading, the baby was sleeping.'
  • 'When he arrived, we started dinner.'
Two Simples in a row = sequential actions; Continuous + Simple = interruption.
el_u14.exc: If there are two short sequential actions in the past, the Past Simple is used in both parts: When he arrived, she made coffee (= after he arrived). Compare: When he arrived, she was making coffee (= she was already making it).
mistakes: While I cooked, he called. → While I was cooking, he called. — while + a long action.
When the phone rang, I watched TV. → When the phone rang, I was watching TV. — background action uses the Continuous.
I was falling asleep at 10 p.m. → I fell asleep at 10 p.m. — an instantaneous action uses the Simple.
She was knowing the truth. → She knew the truth. — a stative verb is not in the Continuous.

el_u15.note: I have done. She has gone.
el_u15.exp:
Unit 15 — Present Perfect: connection of a past action with the present moment (result, experience, change).

Formula: have/has + V3 (past participle).

Examples:
  • 'I have lost my keys.' (and still haven't found them)
  • 'She has broken her arm.' (and it still hurts)
  • 'We have finished the project.'
  • 'He has gone home.' (he isn't here)
V3: go→gone, see→seen, do→done, write→written, make→made. The focus is on the current result, not exactly when it happened.
el_u15.exc: With yesterday, last week, ago, in 2010 the Present Perfect is not used — only the Past Simple. In American English the Past Simple is often used where British English prefers the Present Perfect: Did you eat? / Have you eaten?
mistakes: I have seen him yesterday. → I saw him yesterday. — with yesterday use the Past Simple.
She has went home. → She has gone home. — the third form of go is gone.
They have finish the work. → They have finished the work. — you need the V3/-ed form.
He have lost his keys. → He has lost his keys. — with he use has.

el_u16.text: Unit 16 – Present Perfect: just, already, yet
el_u16.note: I've just eaten. Have you finished yet?
el_u16.exp:
Unit 16 — the adverbs just, already, yet with the Present Perfect.

  • just — just now (affirmative): I've just seen him.
  • already — already, sooner than expected (affirmative): She has already left.
  • yet — yet (in negatives) / already (in questions), at the end of the sentence: Have you finished yet? / I haven't finished yet.
Examples:
  • 'They've just arrived.'
  • 'I've already eaten.'
  • 'Has the postman come yet?'
just/already go between have and V3; yet goes at the end.
el_u16.exc: Yet goes at the end of the sentence; just/already go between the auxiliary and the main verb. In American English just/already are acceptable with the Past Simple: I just ate.
mistakes: I have yet finished. → I haven't finished yet. — yet is for negatives/questions.
She already has left. → She has already left. — already goes between has and V3.
Have you finished already? (as a neutral question) → Have you finished yet? — in questions yet is usual.
I have just ate. → I have just eaten. — you need the V3 form.

el_u17.text: Unit 17 – Present Perfect: Have you ever...?
el_u17.note: Have you ever been to Paris?
el_u17.exp:
Unit 17 — Present Perfect with ever / never to talk about life experience.

Formula: Have/Has + subject + ever + V3…?

Examples:
  • 'Have you ever been to Japan?'
  • 'I have never tried sushi.'
  • 'She has never flown on a plane.'
  • 'It's the best film I've ever seen.'
been vs gone: He has been to Rome (went and came back) vs He has gone to Rome (left and is still there). Never is the only negation needed — a second not is not required.
el_u17.exc: Have you been to…? — went and came back; Have you gone to…? — left and still there. Never is already a negative word — a second negative is not needed: I have never been (not I haven't never been).
mistakes: Have you ever went to Paris? → Have you ever been to Paris? — in the Perfect use the V3 been.
I haven't never tried it. → I have never tried it. — English uses one negation.
She has gone to Rome twice. → She has been to Rome twice. — for experience use been.
Did you ever eat sushi? → Have you ever eaten sushi? — for experience use the Present Perfect.

el_u18.note: I have been working for 3 hours.
el_u18.exp:
Unit 18 — Present Perfect Continuous for an action that started in the past and has been going on up to the present (or has just finished with a visible result).

Formula: have/has + been + V-ing.

Examples:
  • 'I have been waiting for an hour.'
  • 'She has been studying all day.'
  • 'It has been raining since morning.'
  • 'Your eyes are red — have you been crying?'
Often used with for, since, all day, how long. Stative verbs use the Present Perfect Simple: I have known him for years.
el_u18.exc: With stative verbs (know, love, have) the Present Perfect Simple is used, not the Continuous: I have known him for years (not I have been knowing).
mistakes: I am working here for 3 years. → I have been working here for 3 years.
She has been knowing him since 2010. → She has known him since 2010. — stative verb.
How long you have been waiting? → How long have you been waiting? — inversion required.
He has been write a book. → He has been writing a book. — you need the -ing form.

el_u19.note: for 3 hours, since Monday, 2 years ago
el_u19.exp:
Unit 19 — since, for, ago for expressing time.

  • for + length of time: for two hours, for a week, for years
  • since + starting point: since Monday, since 2010, since I was a child
  • ago after a length of time — with the Past Simple: two days ago
Examples:
  • 'I have lived here for 10 years.'
  • 'She has worked here since January.'
  • 'He arrived two hours ago.'
For/since — with the Present Perfect (Continuous); ago — only with the Past Simple, not with the Perfect.
el_u19.exc: Ago is used ONLY with the Past Simple and goes after the length of time: two years ago. Since can introduce a whole clause: since I was a child.
mistakes: I have lived here since 5 years. → I have lived here for 5 years. — a period → for.
She has worked here for 2010. → She has worked here since 2010. — a starting point → since.
I saw him since two days ago. → I saw him two days ago. — ago without a preposition.
He has arrived two hours ago. → He arrived two hours ago. — with ago use the Past Simple.

el_u20.text: Unit 20 – Present Perfect vs Past Simple
el_u20.note: I've seen it. / I saw it yesterday.
el_u20.exp:
Unit 20 — comparison of Present Perfect and Past Simple.

Present Perfect — connection to the present, no specific time: I have seen that film.
Past Simple — completed action with a specified time: I saw it yesterday.

Examples:
  • 'I have been to London.' (experience) vs 'I went to London in 2018.' (fact)
  • 'She has just called.' (connection) vs 'She called an hour ago.' (past)
  • 'Have you finished?' (relating to now) vs 'Did you finish on time?'
With the markers yesterday, last week, in 2020, ago, when — only the Past Simple. With today, this week, this morning (period not yet over) — the Present Perfect.
el_u20.exc: With a specific past time (yesterday, last year, in 1990, when I was young) only the Past Simple is used. With today, this week, this morning (if the period is still going) — the Present Perfect.
mistakes: I have seen this film last week. → I saw this film last week.
She went to Paris. She loves it. (if she returned recently and the connection matters) → She has been to Paris.
Did you finish your work yet? → Have you finished your work yet? (BrE) — with yet use the Perfect.
When have you arrived? → When did you arrive? — with when about a specific moment use the Past Simple.

el_u21.note: The door is opened. The car was stolen.
el_u21.exp:
Unit 21 — the passive voice in the Present and Past Simple, when the action is more important than the doer.

Formula:
  • Present: am/is/are + V3
  • Past: was/were + V3
  • The doer is introduced with by.
Examples:
  • 'English is spoken all over the world.'
  • 'The letter was written by Anna.'
  • 'My car was stolen last night.'
  • 'These cakes are made every morning.'
Intransitive verbs (happen, arrive, come, die) cannot be used in the passive.
el_u21.exc: Intransitive verbs (happen, come, arrive, die) are not used in the passive. Some verbs form the passive unusually: born → be born (I was born in 1990).
mistakes: The book was wrote by him. → The book was written by him. — you need the V3 form.
The accident was happened yesterday. → The accident happened yesterday. — happen is not used in the passive.
I am born in Moscow. → I was born in Moscow. — born is in the past.
English speaks in many countries. → English is spoken in many countries.

el_u22.text: Unit 22 - Passive voice in different tenses
el_u22.note: is made / was made / will be made
el_u22.exp:
Unit 22 — the passive voice in various tenses and with modal verbs.

Formulas:
  • Present Cont.: is/are being + V3
  • Present Perfect: has/have been + V3
  • Past Cont.: was/were being + V3
  • Future: will be + V3
  • Modal: can/must/should + be + V3
Examples:
  • 'The house is being built.'
  • 'The letter has been sent.'
  • 'It will be finished tomorrow.'
  • 'This must be done today.'
Principle: only the be part changes, while V3 stays the same.
el_u22.exc: Continuous Passive: is being done / was being done. Perfect Passive: has been done / had been done. Modals: can be done, must be done, should be done.
mistakes: The house is build now. → The house is being built now. — Continuous Passive.
The letter has wrote. → The letter has been written. — Perfect Passive: has been + V3.
It must done today. → It must be done today. — after a modal use be + V3.
The car will repaired. → The car will be repaired. — in the future: will be + V3.

el_u23.text: Unit 23 – Auxiliary verbs BE, DO, HAVE
el_u23.note: am doing / do you / have you
el_u23.exp:
Unit 23 — three key auxiliary verbs: be, do, have.

  • be — forms the Continuous (is working) and the Passive (is made)
  • do/does/did — questions and negatives in the Simple (Do you know? I don't know)
  • have/has/had — Perfect tenses (has done, had seen)
Examples:
  • 'She is reading.' — Continuous.
  • 'Do you like tea?' — Simple question.
  • 'They have arrived.' — Perfect.
  • 'The door was opened.' — Passive.
The choice of auxiliary depends on the tense/construction. In affirmative Simple do is usually not needed (except for emphasis: I do like it!).
el_u23.exc: In the Present/Past Simple the auxiliary do/did appears only in questions and negatives; it is absent in affirmative sentences: I work (not I do work, except for emphasis).
mistakes: Does she can swim? → Can she swim? — with a modal do is not needed.
I do work here every day. (as neutral) → I work here every day.
Have you going? → Are you going? — the Continuous requires be.
Do you been there? → Have you been there? — the Perfect requires have.

el_u24.exp:
Unit 24 — regular and irregular verbs.

Regular: add -ed for V2 and V3 (work-worked-worked).
Irregular: have special V1/V2/V3 forms that must be memorised.

Common irregular:
  • go – went – gone
  • see – saw – seen
  • take – took – taken
  • write – wrote – written
  • buy – bought – bought
  • have – had – had
Examples:
  • 'I worked yesterday.' (reg.)
  • 'She has taken my book.' (irreg.)
V2 is used in the Past Simple; V3 — in Perfect tenses and the Passive.
el_u24.exc: Some verbs have both forms: learn→learned/learnt, dream→dreamed/dreamt. The forms of read are spelled the same but pronounced differently: [riːd] → [red] → [red].
mistakes: I goed to school. → I went to school. — go is irregular.
She has eated dinner. → She has eaten dinner. — V3 of eat is eaten.
They taked my bag. → They took my bag. — V2 of take is took.
He has writed a letter. → He has written a letter. — V3 is written.

el_u25.text: Unit 25 – Present Continuous for the future
el_u25.exp:
Unit 25 — Present Continuous for arranged events in the future (arrangements with a specific time/place).

Formula: am/is/are + V-ing + a future marker.

Examples:
  • 'I am meeting John tomorrow.'
  • 'She is flying to Rome next week.'
  • 'We are having dinner with them on Saturday.'
  • 'What are you doing this evening?'
Contrast: for timetables (trains, lessons) — Present Simple (The train leaves at 7); for spontaneous decisions — will; for intentions — going to.
el_u25.exc: For scheduled events (trains, films) the Present Simple is used: The train leaves at 6. The Present Continuous is for personal plans with a definite arrangement.
mistakes: I meet him tomorrow. → I'm meeting him tomorrow. — a personal plan.
The train is leaving at 8 every day. → The train leaves at 8 every day. — a timetable.
I will meet her tomorrow (when there is already an arrangement) → I'm meeting her tomorrow.
She is flying to Paris. (without a future marker) → She is flying to Paris next week.

el_u26.note: I'm going to study. She's going to rain.
el_u26.exp:
Unit 26 — the construction be going to for intentions and predictions based on visible evidence.

Formula: am/is/are + going to + V1.

Examples:
  • 'I'm going to learn Spanish.'
  • 'They are going to move to Berlin.'
  • 'Look at those clouds — it's going to rain!'
  • 'She is going to have a baby.'
Contrast with will: going to — a decision made in advance; will — a spontaneous one. Often going to go is simplified to going to + place: I'm going to the shop.
el_u26.exc: Going to go is often replaced by going to: I'm going to the shop (instead of going to go to). In speech going to is pronounced gonna (informal).
mistakes: I going to study. → I'm going to study. — the auxiliary am/is/are is needed.
She is going to studies. → She is going to study. — after to use the bare infinitive.
Look! It will rain. (clouds are visible) → Look! It's going to rain. — based on visible evidence.
He is going to go to London. → He is going to London. — we avoid the repetition.

el_u27.note: I'll help you. It will be sunny.
el_u27.exp:
Unit 27 — Future Simple with will for spontaneous decisions, offers, promises, predictions and facts about the future.

Formula: will + V1 (without to). Negative: won't.

Examples:
  • 'I'll help you with the bags.' (a decision right now)
  • 'I promise I'll call.'
  • 'It will be sunny tomorrow.'
  • 'She won't be late.'
In time/condition clauses after when, if, before, after, until will is not used: When I arrive, I'll call you.
el_u27.exc: Will is not used in clauses of time and condition (when, if, before, after): When I arrive, I will call (not When I will arrive). Short negative form: won't.
mistakes: When I will see him, I'll tell him. → When I see him, I'll tell him.
I will to help you. → I will help you. — after will use the bare infinitive.
She willn't come. → She won't come. — the short form is won't.
Will you helping me? → Will you help me? — after will use the bare infinitive.

el_u28.note: Will you help? Shall I open the window?
el_u28.exp:
Unit 28 — the modals will and shall.

Will — future, promises, requests: Will you help me?
Shall — in modern English mainly for offers with I/we: Shall I…? / Shall we…?

Examples:
  • 'Shall I open the window?'
  • 'Shall we go for a walk?'
  • 'Will you pass the salt, please?'
  • 'I shall never forget this day.' (formal)
After modals — the bare infinitive. Shall with he/she/they is almost never used in modern English.
el_u28.exc: Shall I…? / Shall we…? — only for offers with I/we. In affirmative sentences shall is outdated and sounds formal. The question Will you…? can be a request or a question about the future.
mistakes: Shall he come? → Will he come? — shall is not used with he/she/they.
I shall to help. → I shall help. — after a modal use the bare infinitive.
Shall I to open the window? → Shall I open the window?
Will I help you? (as an offer) → Shall I help you? — an offer from oneself.

el_u29.note: It might rain. She may be at home.
el_u29.exp:
Unit 29 — may and might for expressing possibility (probably/maybe).

Formula: may/might + V1. Might usually expresses less certainty, but in modern speech they are often interchangeable.

Examples:
  • 'It may rain later.'
  • 'She might be at home.'
  • 'I might go to the party.'
  • 'They may not come.'
May also expresses formal permission: May I come in? Negative: may not / might not (no contraction mayn't).
el_u29.exc: May can also mean permission (formally): May I come in? Might is almost never used in this sense. After both — the bare infinitive.
mistakes: She may to come. → She may come. — without to.
He mights know. → He might know. — modals do not take -s.
It may rains tomorrow. → It may rain tomorrow. — after may use the bare infinitive.
Do you may help me? → May I help you? — without do.

el_u30.note: I can swim. Could you help me?
el_u30.exp:
Unit 30 — can and could for abilities, possibility, permission and requests.

Can — present ability/permission: I can swim. Can I sit here?
Could — past ability and polite requests: I could read at 5. Could you help me?

Examples:
  • 'She can speak three languages.'
  • 'Can you help me?'
  • 'When I was young, I could run fast.'
  • 'Could you repeat, please?'
Can has no future form — use will be able to instead. After a modal — the bare infinitive.
el_u30.exc: Can has no future form — will be able to is used. Past could describes general ability; for a specific successful situation — was/were able to or managed to.
mistakes: She cans swim. → She can swim. — modals take no -s.
I can to drive. → I can drive. — without to.
He will can help. → He will be able to help. — can has no future.
Do you can help? → Can you help? — without do.

el_u31.note: You must stop. You needn't worry.
el_u31.exp:
Unit 31 — must, mustn't, needn't.

  • must — strong necessity/obligation: You must stop at the red light.
  • mustn't — prohibition: You mustn't smoke here.
  • needn't (= don't need to / don't have to) — lack of necessity: You needn't come early.
Examples:
  • 'I must finish this report today.'
  • 'Children mustn't play with matches.'
  • 'You needn't worry about it.'
After must/mustn't/needn't — the bare infinitive. Must expresses a confident conclusion: She must be tired.
el_u31.exc: Mustn't = prohibition (not allowed); don't have to / needn't = not necessary (you don't have to). Must has no past form — had to is used.
mistakes: You mustn't come — it's not necessary. → You don't have to come.
She musts go. → She must go. — modals take no -s.
I must to leave. → I must leave. — without to.
He musted wait. → He had to wait. — the past is had to.

el_u32.exp:
Unit 32 — should for advice, recommendations and opinions (ought to / it's a good idea).

Formula: should + V1. Negative: shouldn't. Question: Should I…?

Examples:
  • 'You should see a doctor.'
  • 'He shouldn't drive so fast.'
  • 'Should I call her?'
  • 'We should leave now.'
Should is weaker than must: it's advice, not a command. Often used with I think: I think you should apologize. After should — the bare infinitive.
el_u32.exc: The negation shouldn't = ought not to. For strong advice — ought to (synonym). Should have + V3 — regret about the past (should have done).
mistakes: You should to see a doctor. → You should see a doctor. — without to.
He shoulds study more. → He should study more.
Do I should go? → Should I go? — without do.
She should went there. → She should have gone there. — regret about the past.

el_u33.note: I have to work. She has to leave.
el_u33.exp:
Unit 33 — have to / has to for expressing obligation or necessity coming from outside (rules, circumstances).

Formulas:
  • Present: have to / has to + V1
  • Past: had to + V1
  • ? / — : using do/does/did: Do you have to…? / I don't have to…
Examples:
  • 'I have to work on Saturday.'
  • 'She has to wear a uniform.'
  • 'Did you have to pay?'
  • 'I don't have to get up early tomorrow.'
Don't have to = not necessary (unlike mustn't = not allowed).
el_u33.exc: Unlike must, have to has all forms: had to, will have to, have had to. Questions and negatives are formed with do/does/did: Do you have to…?
mistakes: She have to work. → She has to work. — in the third person use has.
I haven't to go. → I don't have to go. — negative with don't.
Have you to leave? → Do you have to leave? — question with do.
He must to work yesterday. → He had to work yesterday. — in the past use had to.

el_u34.note: Would you like tea? I'd like to go.
el_u34.exp:
Unit 34 — would for polite requests, offers, hypothetical situations and past habits.

Main meanings:
  • Polite request: Would you pass me the salt?
  • Offer/wish: I would like a coffee.
  • Hypothesis: I would travel more if I had money.
  • Past habit: When I was a child, I would play in the park.
Examples:
  • 'Would you like some tea?'
  • 'I'd love to come.'
  • 'He would always help his friends.'
Short form: 'd. After would — the bare infinitive.
el_u34.exc: Would you like…? — an offer (would you like something?); Do you like…? — a general question about tastes. After would like use the infinitive with to: I'd like to go.
mistakes: Do you would like tea? → Would you like tea? — without do.
I would like going. → I would like to go. — after would like use the infinitive.
Would you like some tea? (asking whether you like it in general) → Do you like tea?
She would likes coffee. → She would like coffee. — after a modal no -s.

el_u35.note: Open the door! Don't be late!
el_u35.exp:
Unit 35 — the imperative for commands, requests, instructions and advice.

Formula:
  • Affirmative: V1 + … (Sit down!)
  • Negative: Don't + V1 (Don't worry!)
  • With let's — suggestion of joint action: Let's go!
Examples:
  • 'Open the window, please.'
  • 'Don't be late.'
  • 'Let's have lunch together.'
  • 'Turn left at the corner.'
The imperative is addressed to you, but the pronoun is omitted. To soften, add please.
el_u35.exc: Let's = suggestion of joint action; negative — Let's not go (not Don't let's go). In polite requests we add please, but it is not obligatory.
mistakes: You sit down! → Sit down! — the subject is omitted in the imperative.
No smoke here! → Don't smoke here! — negative with don't.
Let's to go. → Let's go. — without to.
Don't be to late. → Don't be late. — without to.

el_u36.note: I used to live there. She used to smoke.
el_u36.exp:
Unit 36 — used to for describing habitual actions and states that existed in the past but no longer occur.

Formula:
  • Affirmative: used to + V1
  • Negative: didn't use to + V1
  • Question: Did + subject + use to + V1?
Examples:
  • 'I used to smoke, but I quit.'
  • 'She used to live in London.'
  • 'Did you use to play football?'
  • 'He didn't use to like coffee.'
It only refers to the past; there is no modern equivalent use to — for present habits use the Present Simple with usually.
el_u36.exc: In negatives and questions used to turns into use to: didn't use to, did you use to…? Do not confuse with be used to + -ing (be accustomed) and get used to.
mistakes: I used to living there. → I used to live there. — after used to use the bare infinitive.
She didn't used to smoke. → She didn't use to smoke. — after did use use.
I am used to live here. (in the sense of 'used to live here before') → I used to live here.
He use to play football. → He used to play football. — in the affirmative use used.

el_u37.note: There is a book. There are two cats.
el_u37.exp:
Unit 37 — the construction there is / there are to indicate the existence or presence of something.

Formula:
  • there is + singular: There is a book on the table.
  • there are + plural: There are two chairs.
  • Negative: there isn't / there aren't (any)
  • Question: Is there…? / Are there…?
Examples:
  • 'There is a cat in the garden.'
  • 'There are many people here.'
  • 'Is there a bank near here?'
  • 'There isn't any milk.'
Agreement with the first noun after the construction: There is a book and two pens.
el_u37.exc: The verb agrees with the nearest noun: There is a book and two pens / There are two pens and a book. In contractions: there's (not there're — rare).
mistakes: Is a book on the table. → There is a book on the table. — there is needed.
There are a cat here. → There is a cat here. — with singular use is.
It is a problem (in the sense of 'there exists') → There is a problem.
There is many people. → There are many people. — with plural use are.

el_u38.text: Unit 38 – There was / there will be / there has been
el_u38.note: There was a problem. Will there be...?
el_u38.exp:
Unit 38 — there in other tenses: there was/were, there will be, there has/have been.

Forms:
  • Past: there was / there were
  • Future: there will be
  • Perfect: there has been / there have been
  • With modals: there might be, there must be
Examples:
  • 'There was a party yesterday.'
  • 'There were many guests.'
  • 'There will be a meeting tomorrow.'
  • 'There has been an accident.'
Agreement with the noun: was/has been + singular; were/have been + plural.
el_u38.exc: There will be (not there will have). After there modal verbs can follow: there must be, there might be, there should be.
mistakes: There will a party. → There will be a party. — be is needed.
There was many people. → There were many people. — with plural use were.
It was a problem yesterday (it existed) → There was a problem yesterday.
There have been a mistake. → There has been a mistake. — with singular use has.

el_u39.exp:
Unit 39 — the formal subject it for time, weather, distance, temperature and in impersonal constructions.

Use:
  • Time: It is 7 o'clock.
  • Date/day: It is Monday.
  • Weather: It is raining.
  • Distance: It is 5 km to the station.
  • Evaluation: It is difficult to learn Chinese.
Examples:
  • 'It is cold today.'
  • 'It takes an hour to get there.'
  • 'It is nice to see you.'
Contrast with there: there is = exists; it is = this/the situation.
el_u39.exc: It is used for weather, time, distance, but is NOT replaced by a real subject: It is raining, not The weather is raining.
mistakes: Is cold today. → It is cold today. — the formal it is needed.
Is 5 o'clock. → It is 5 o'clock.
The weather is raining. → It is raining.
How long it takes? → How long does it take? — does is needed.

el_u40.text: Unit 40 - Short Answers with Auxiliary Verbs
el_u40.note: Yes, I do. / No, she isn't. / So do I.
el_u40.exp:
Unit 40 — short answers and replies with auxiliary verbs (to avoid repeating the full verb).

Principle: in a short answer the auxiliary/modal verb matching the tense of the question is repeated.

Examples:
  • 'Do you like tea?' — 'Yes, I do. / No, I don't.'
  • 'Is she coming?' — 'Yes, she is. / No, she isn't.'
  • 'Can you swim?' — 'Yes, I can.'
  • 'Have you seen it?' — 'Yes, I have.'
The same principle in replies: I live in Moscow. — Do you? / Oh, really?; She can drive. — Can she? This allows you to show interest or surprise without full repetition.
el_u40.exc: So do I = agreement with an affirmative statement; Neither do I / Nor do I = with a negative. Inversion is required: So + auxiliary + subject.
mistakes: Yes, I like. → Yes, I do. — the auxiliary is repeated.
Me too. (after 'I don't like it') → Me neither. or Neither do I.
So I do. → So do I. — inversion.
Yes, she's. → Yes, she is. — no contraction in a short answer.

el_u41.note: It's nice, isn't it? You didn't go, did you?
el_u41.exp:
Unit 41 — tag questions — a short 'tail' for clarifying, agreeing or getting confirmation.

Rule: after an affirmative sentence — a negative tag; after a negative — an affirmative one. The same auxiliary is used.

Examples:
  • 'You are a teacher, aren't you?'
  • 'She doesn't smoke, does she?'
  • 'They have arrived, haven't they?'
  • 'You can swim, can't you?'
With I am the tag is aren't I? With imperative sentences the tag is will you? / won't you?
el_u41.exc: With I am → aren't I?; with Let's → shall we?; with the imperative → will you? / won't you?. After nobody/no one/nothing the tag is positive: Nobody came, did they?
mistakes: She is a doctor, is she? → She is a doctor, isn't she? — after an affirmative a negative.
You don't like it, do you like it? → You don't like it, do you?
I'm late, amn't I? → I'm late, aren't I?
Let's go, will we? → Let's go, shall we?

el_u42.text: Unit 42 – Too, either, so, neither, nor
el_u42.note: I'm tired. So am I. / Me too.
el_u42.exp:
Unit 42 — too, either, so, neither/nor for expressing agreement or similarity ('also / also not').

Rules:
  • Affirmative + too / so: I am tired. — Me too. / So am I.
  • Negative + either / neither: I don't like it. — Me neither. / Neither do I.
  • Order: So/Neither + auxiliary + subject.
Examples:
  • 'I can swim.' — 'So can I.'
  • 'She isn't hungry.' — 'Neither am I.'
  • 'He likes jazz, and I do too.'
  • 'They haven't finished. — Nor have we.'
The auxiliary in a so/neither answer must match the original.
el_u42.exc: So/Neither require inversion; too/either — direct word order. Either — with a negative, too — with an affirmative.
mistakes: I like tea. So I do. → I like tea. So do I.
I'm tired too. (reply to 'I'm tired') — correct; but I'm tired either. → I'm tired too.
I don't know. Me too. → Me neither. / Neither do I.
Neither do I like it. → I don't like it either. or Neither do I.

el_u43.note: I'm not sure. They haven't arrived.
el_u43.exp:
Unit 43 — negative sentences and the particle not.

Main ways:
  • With to be: am/is/are + not (isn't, aren't)
  • With modals: can't, won't, mustn't, shouldn't
  • With Simple: don't / doesn't / didn't + V1
  • With Perfect: haven't / hasn't / hadn't + V3
Examples:
  • 'She isn't at home.'
  • 'I don't like coffee.'
  • 'We haven't seen him.'
  • 'He won't come.'
English has one negative per sentence: with never, nobody, nothing a second not is not used.
el_u43.exc: Double negation is not allowed in English: I don't know anything (not I don't know nothing). After nobody/nothing/never the verb is affirmative.
mistakes: I don't know nothing. → I don't know anything.
She no likes coffee. → She doesn't like coffee.
He isn't never late. → He is never late.
They haven't no money. → They don't have any money. / They have no money.

el_u44.note: Why did she leave? Where are you going?
el_u44.exp:
Unit 44 — word order in a question: the auxiliary verb goes before the subject (inversion).

Formula:
  • General: Aux + S + V + …?
  • Wh-question: Wh- + aux + S + V + …?
In the Simple the auxiliary is do/does/did; in the Continuous — be; in the Perfect — have; with modals — the modal itself.

Examples:
  • 'Where do you live?'
  • 'What is she doing?'
  • 'Have they arrived?'
  • 'Can he swim?'
In a statement the order is: S + V; in a question — inversion using the auxiliary.
el_u44.exc: If the question is about the subject (who/what = subject), no auxiliary is used: Who lives here?
mistakes: Why she left? → Why did she leave?
Where you are going? → Where are you going?
What she does? → What does she do?
When you will come? → When will you come?

el_u45.exp:
Unit 45 — a subject question with who / what. When the question word replaces the subject, the word order stays direct (as in a statement) and the auxiliary do/did is not needed.

Formula: Who/What + V (+ …)?

Examples:
  • 'Who lives here?'
  • 'What happened?'
  • 'Who called you?'
  • 'What is in the box?'
Compare: Who did you call? (object — did is needed) vs Who called you? (subject — no did). The verb is usually in the third person singular.
el_u45.exc: When who/what/which is the subject, the verb stands in the third person singular form in the Present Simple: Who likes tea? (not Who do like tea?).
mistakes: Who did break the window? → Who broke the window? — without did.
What did happen? → What happened?
Who do live here? → Who lives here?
What does make you happy? → What makes you happy?

el_u46.note: Who did you talk to? What is it for?
el_u46.exp:
Unit 46 — questions with prepositions. In English the preposition usually stays at the end of the question (it is not moved to the front).

Formula: Wh- + aux + S + V … + preposition?

Examples:
  • 'Who are you waiting for?'
  • 'What is she talking about?'
  • 'Where do you come from?'
  • 'Who did you go with?'
The formal variant (With whom did you go?) sounds bookish. The spoken norm is a preposition at the end. The preposition depends on the verb: listen to, wait for, look at, talk about.
el_u46.exc: The preposition usually stays at the end of the question: Who are you talking to? The formal variant with the preposition in front: To whom are you talking? — almost never used in speech.
mistakes: With who did you go? → Who did you go with?
At what are you looking? (colloquial) → What are you looking at?
For what is it? → What is it for?
About what are you thinking? → What are you thinking about?

el_u47.text: Unit 47 – Questions with what, which, how
el_u47.note: What color? Which one? How often?
el_u47.exp:
Unit 47 — the question words what, which, how.

  • what — open choice: What is your name?
  • which — choice from a limited set: Which colour do you prefer, red or blue?
  • how — way, state; with adjectives/adverbs: how old, how far, how often
Examples:
  • 'What time is it?'
  • 'Which bus goes to the centre?'
  • 'How are you?'
  • 'How old is your brother?'
What kind of…? — about type; How many / How much — about quantity (countable/uncountable).
el_u47.exc: What — open choice; Which — from a limited number. How many — with countables, How much — with uncountables and for price.
mistakes: How many water do you drink? → How much water do you drink?
How much books do you have? → How many books do you have?
What of these shirts do you like? (out of two) → Which of these shirts do you like?
How long time does it take? → How long does it take?

el_u48.text: Unit 48 – How long does it take?
el_u48.note: It takes 30 minutes to get there.
el_u48.exp:
Unit 48 — the construction How long does it take…? for asking about the duration of an action.

Formula: How long does it take (+ someone) + to + V1?

Examples:
  • 'How long does it take to get to the airport?'
  • 'It takes 20 minutes by bus.'
  • 'How long did it take you to learn English?'
  • 'It took me three years.'
In the past — How long did it take…?; in the future — How long will it take…? Note: it is the formal subject.
el_u48.exc: Note the order: It takes + (object) + period + to + infinitive: It takes me 20 minutes to get there. In the past — It took…
mistakes: How long it takes? → How long does it take?
It takes 20 minutes for go there. → It takes 20 minutes to go there.
It take me an hour. → It takes me an hour.
How long did it took? → How long did it take?

el_u49.note: Do you know where she lives?
el_u49.exp:
Unit 49 — indirect (embedded) questions and clauses: after expressions such as Do you know…, Can you tell me…, I wonder… the word order becomes direct (as in a statement) and the auxiliary do/did disappears.

Formula: Do you know + wh- + S + V …?

Examples:
  • 'Do you know where she lives?'
  • 'Can you tell me what time it is?'
  • 'I wonder why he is late.'
  • 'I don't know if/whether she will come.'
For yes/no questions use if / whether.
el_u49.exc: In an indirect question use direct word order (no inversion) and no do/does/did. For yes/no questions use if/whether.
mistakes: Do you know where does she live? → Do you know where she lives?
Tell me what is it. → Tell me what it is.
I don't know is he coming. → I don't know if he is coming.
Can you tell me where did he go? → Can you tell me where he went?

el_u50.exp:
Unit 50 — Reported Speech: conveying someone else's words. When moving from direct to reported speech the verb tense usually shifts one step back (backshift), and pronouns/time references change.

Tense shift:
  • Present Simple → Past Simple
  • Present Cont. → Past Cont.
  • Present Perfect → Past Perfect
  • Past Simple → Past Perfect
  • will → would; can → could
Examples:
  • 'I am tired,' she said. → She said (that) she was tired.
  • 'I will call you.' → He said he would call me.
  • 'We have finished.' → They said they had finished.
Changes: today→that day, tomorrow→the next day, here→there, this→that.
el_u50.exc: General facts do not 'shift': He said the Earth is round. The modals would, could, might, should do not change. Must → had to (for obligation).
mistakes: She said she is tired. → She said she was tired.
He said he will call. → He said he would call.
They said me that… → They told me that… / They said (that)…
She said that today is Monday. (when retelling later) → She said that that day was Monday.

el_u51.note: I want to go. I enjoy swimming.
el_u51.exp:
Unit 51 — choosing between the infinitive (to + V) and the gerund/-ing form (V-ing) after other verbs.

After these verbs usually V-ing: enjoy, finish, stop, mind, suggest, avoid, practise, keep.
After these — to + V: want, need, decide, hope, promise, learn, plan, offer.

Examples:
  • 'I enjoy reading.'
  • 'She wants to leave.'
  • 'They decided to move.'
  • 'He finished writing.'
After prepositions always use V-ing: good at swimming, instead of working.
el_u51.exc: After stop the meaning changes: stop to smoke (stop in order to smoke) vs stop smoking (quit smoking). After remember/forget/try — similarly.
mistakes: I enjoy to swim. → I enjoy swimming.
She wants going home. → She wants to go home.
He decided going. → He decided to go.
I finished to work. → I finished working.

el_u52.note: He agreed to help. She loves reading.
el_u52.exp:
Unit 52 — verbs that allow both forms (to + V or V-ing), sometimes with a change of meaning.

No difference: like, love, hate, start, begin, continue, prefer (I like swimming / I like to swim).
With a difference:
  • remember to do — remember to do (in the future) vs remember doing — remember doing (in the past)
  • stop to do — stop in order to do vs stop doing — quit doing
  • try to do — attempt vs try doing — try a way
Examples:
  • 'I remembered to lock the door.'
  • 'I remember meeting him.'
  • 'He stopped smoking.'
el_u52.exc: After prepositions ALWAYS use the gerund: good at swimming, afraid of flying, instead of going. An infinitive after a preposition is impossible.
mistakes: She is good at to sing. → She is good at singing.
I'm interested in to learn French. → I'm interested in learning French.
He told me go. → He told me to go.
Thanks for to help. → Thanks for helping.

el_u53.text: Unit 53 - Infinitive after make and let
el_u53.note: She made me cry. He let me go.
el_u53.exp:
Unit 53 — the bare infinitive (without to) after the verbs make and let.

Formulas:
  • make somebody do — force someone to do
  • let somebody do — allow/permit someone to do
Examples:
  • 'She made me wait.'
  • 'My parents don't let me stay out late.'
  • 'Let me help you.'
  • 'The teacher made us work hard.'
In the passive make takes to: I was made to wait. Let has no passive form — allowed to is used: We were allowed to leave.
el_u53.exc: After make/let/hear/see/feel/watch in the active — the bare infinitive. But in the passive with make to appears: I was made to work.
mistakes: She made me to cry. → She made me cry.
He let me to go. → He let me go.
I saw him to leave. → I saw him leave.
They made me working late. → They made me work late.

el_u54.note: I came here to learn. She works to earn money.
el_u54.exp:
Unit 54 — the infinitive of purpose: to + V1 answers the question 'why?', 'what for?'.

Formula: … + to + V1 (+ …). More formal variants: in order to, so as to.

Examples:
  • 'I went to the shop to buy milk.'
  • 'She is learning English to get a better job.'
  • 'He called me to say goodbye.'
  • 'We stopped to rest.'
Negative purpose: in order not to / so as not to (I left early so as not to be late.). For + V-ing expresses the function of an object: a knife for cutting bread.
el_u54.exc: Purpose is expressed by the infinitive with to or by in order to / so as to. Negative purpose: so as not to / in order not to (not for not to).
mistakes: I came here for learn English. → I came here to learn English.
She works for earning money. → She works to earn money.
He went to shop for to buy bread. → He went to the shop to buy bread.
I whispered for not to wake him. → I whispered so as not to wake him.

el_u55.text: Unit 55 – Verb go: go on, go for, go doing
el_u55.note: go shopping, go for a walk, go on holiday
el_u55.exp:
Unit 55 — the verb go in fixed expressions and combinations.

Common patterns:
  • go + V-ing — for types of activity: go shopping, go swimming, go skiing, go fishing
  • go for + noun: go for a walk, go for a drink, go for a swim
  • go to + place: go to school, go to work, go to the cinema
  • go on — continue: Go on talking.
Examples:
  • 'Let's go swimming.'
  • 'We went for a walk in the park.'
  • 'She goes to university by bus.'
  • 'Go on — I'm listening.'
el_u55.exc: After go in the sense of an activity — a gerund without an article: go swimming. After go for an article is usual: go for a walk, go for a coffee.
mistakes: I go to swimming every week. → I go swimming every week.
Let's go for walk. → Let's go for a walk.
She went on a holiday. (BrE) → She went on holiday.
We go to shop. (about shopping) → We go shopping.

el_u56.note: get tired, get married, get to work
el_u56.exp:
Unit 56 — the multi-purpose verb get.

Main meanings:
  • Receive: I got a letter.
  • Become (+ adj.): It's getting dark. She got angry.
  • Arrive: How do you get to work?
  • Fetch/buy: I'll get some bread.
  • Phrasal: get up, get on, get off, get in, get out, get back
Examples:
  • 'I got a present.'
  • 'He is getting old.'
  • 'We got home at 10.'
  • 'Get off the bus here.'
Get + V3 often = passive in speech: He got hurt.
el_u56.exc: Get + adjective = become (change). Get to + place = arrive at. Before home, here, there no preposition is needed: get home.
mistakes: I get to home at 7. → I get home at 7.
She got tiredly. → She got tired. — after get use an adjective.
He is getting to angry. → He is getting angry.
I got the bus. (meaning 'boarded') — correct, but in the sense of 'reached': I got to the station.

el_u57.text: Unit 57 - Difference between do and make
el_u57.exp:
Unit 57 — the difference between do and make.

Do — for actions, duties, unspecified activity: do homework, do the washing, do a job, do exercise, do your best.
Make — for creating, producing, a result: make breakfast, make a cake, make a mistake, make a decision, make noise.

Examples:
  • 'I do my homework every evening.'
  • 'She is making a cake.'
  • 'He made a mistake.'
  • 'Don't make noise.'
Fixed collocations need to be memorised: do a favour, make a phone call, do business, make friends.
el_u57.exc: Do — actions in general (work, duties); make — creating something new. Memorise: do a favour, do one's best, make a phone call, make a decision.
mistakes: I must make my homework. → I must do my homework.
She did a mistake. → She made a mistake.
Can you do me a photo? → Can you take me a photo? / take a photo of me.
He did a decision. → He made a decision.

el_u58.text: Unit 58 – The verb have in fixed expressions
el_u58.note: have breakfast, have a look, have fun
el_u58.exp:
Unit 58 — have in fixed expressions: a verb of action (not possession), where have replaces a whole range of actions.

Common expressions: have breakfast/lunch/dinner, have a shower/bath, have a rest/break, have a holiday, have a swim, have a drink, have a good time, have a look, have a dream, have a party, have a baby.

Examples:
  • 'I usually have breakfast at 8.'
  • 'She had a shower.'
  • 'Let's have a break.'
  • 'We had a great time in Italy.'
In these expressions have behaves like an ordinary main verb: questions/negatives with do/does/did.
el_u58.exc: In such expressions have is a main verb, so questions and negatives are formed with do/does/did: Do you have breakfast? (not Have you breakfast?). Have got is not used with these expressions.
mistakes: I have got breakfast at 8. → I have breakfast at 8.
Have you lunch? → Do you have lunch?
She is having brown hair. → She has brown hair. — possession, not action.
Let's make a party. → Let's have a party.

el_u59.exp:
Unit 59 — personal pronouns: subject and object.

Subject (as subject): I, you, he, she, it, we, you, they.
Object (as object): me, you, him, her, it, us, you, them.

Examples:
  • 'I love her.'
  • 'She knows me.'
  • 'They invited us.'
  • 'Give it to him.'
Object pronouns are used after verbs and prepositions: with me, for her, to them. In short answers/replies the object form is often used: Who's there? — Me!
el_u59.exc: After prepositions always use the object form: for me, with him, to us. After than, as in speech also the object form: taller than me.
mistakes: She saw I. → She saw me. — after a verb use the object form.
Give it to I. → Give it to me.
Him is my brother. → He is my brother. — subject.
Me and her went. → She and I went. — the subject takes the nominative.

el_u60.note: my book / the book is mine
el_u60.exp:
Unit 60 — possessive adjectives and pronouns: my/mine, your/yours, his/his, her/hers, its/—, our/ours, their/theirs.

Difference:
  • A possessive adjective goes before the noun: my book.
  • A possessive pronoun replaces the phrase: This book is mine.
Examples:
  • 'This is my car. That car is yours.'
  • 'Her dress is beautiful. Hers is blue.'
  • 'Our house is big; theirs is small.'
  • 'Is this pen yours or his?'
Possessive case of nouns: Mary's book, the children's toys.
el_u60.exc: Its (possessive, 'its') is written WITHOUT an apostrophe; it's = it is/has. Before a noun use the adjective (my); without a noun use the pronoun (mine).
mistakes: This book is my. → This book is mine.
Mine car is new. → My car is new. — before a noun use my.
The dog hurt it's leg. → The dog hurt its leg. — possessive has no apostrophe.
A friend of me. → A friend of mine.

el_u61.exp:
Unit 61 — summary table of all personal pronoun forms in English.

In English a pronoun has five forms: nominative (subject), objective (object), possessive adjective (before a noun), possessive pronoun (without a noun), and reflexive.

  • I / me / my / mine / myself
  • he / him / his / his / himself; she / her / her / hers / herself
  • we / us / our / ours / ourselves; they / them / their / theirs / themselves
  • you / you / your / yours / yourself(ves); it / it / its / — / itself

Examples: I gave him my pen; his is blue; he hurt himself. The choice of form depends on the role in the sentence: subject → I, after a verb/preposition → me, before a noun → my, in place of a noun → mine, reflexive reference to the subject → myself.
el_u61.exc: The form his is the same for both the possessive adjective and the possessive pronoun: his book / This book is his. It has no reflexive form itselves — only itself.
mistakes: This is mine book → This is my book / This book is mine — mine without a noun.
Me and John went → John and I went — the subject needs the nominative, and 'I' is mentioned last.
Give it to I → Give it to me — only the objective case after a preposition.

el_u62.exp:
Unit 62 — practical consolidation of all pronoun case forms in connected speech.

Case rule: after a preposition and as a direct/indirect object — always the objective form (me, him, her, us, them), even when there are two pronouns joined by and.

  • She invited John and me.
  • Between you and me, it's a secret.
  • It's me at the door. (colloquial norm; formally It is I).
  • He is taller than I am / than me.

After than/as the objective form is used in speech (taller than me), and the nominative with a verb in formal style (than I am). In emphatic It is/was … who constructions the subject-pronoun takes the nominative: It was she who called.
el_u62.exc: In colloquial speech It's me / It's him is acceptable, though grammatically 'correct' is It is I. After than/as in speech — taller than me; in formal style — taller than I (am).
mistakes: between you and I → between you and me — only the objective case after the preposition between.
Her and me are friends → She and I are friends — a subject requires the nominative.
Us students work hard → We students work hard — the pronoun agrees with its role in the sentence.

el_u63.exp:
Unit 63 — reflexive pronouns -self/-selves, used when the action is directed at the doer.

Forms: myself, yourself, himself, herself, itself, ourselves, yourselves, themselves. Used when subject and object are the same person, and also for emphasis ('self').

Structures:

  • Reflexive meaning: She cut herself with a knife.
  • Emphasis: I did it myself.
  • After a preposition: He lives by himself. (on his own).
  • Help yourself to some cake!

Signal verbs often used with reflexives: hurt, cut, enjoy, blame, teach, introduce, look at. The set phrase by myself/yourself… = 'on one's own, alone'. Compare with each other: They looked at themselves (in the mirror — each at himself) vs They looked at each other.
el_u63.exc: You has two forms: yourself (sing.) and yourselves (pl.). After prepositions of place the reflexive is usually NOT used: She closed the door behind her (not herself).
mistakes: I wash myself every morning → I wash every morning — with wash, shave, dress a reflexive is usually not needed.
Themself → themselves — the plural form always ends in -selves.
She and me did it ourself → She and I did it ourselves — for 'we' use ourselves.
Meet me at my self house → Meet me at my house — myself does not replace my.

el_u64.note: Tom's car. The girl's name. My parents' house.
el_u64.exp:
Unit 64 — possessive case of nouns with apostrophe 's.

Marker of belonging for people and living beings. Structure: owner + 's + thing owned.

  • Singular: Tom's car, the girl's book, my brother's friend.
  • Plural in -s: apostrophe only — my parents' house, the students' exams.
  • Irregular plural: 's as usual — children's toys, women's rights, men's clothes.
  • Places (ellipsis): at the doctor's, to the hairdresser's, at Tom's — at the doctor's, to the hairdresser's, at Tom's place.

For inanimate objects the of construction is preferred: the door of the car, the roof of the house. However, for organisations, countries, and time periods both options are acceptable: the company's policy, today's news, a week's holiday. Double possessive — a friend of mine / a friend of Tom's.
el_u64.exc: Double possessive: a friend of mine (not a mine friend). With inanimates of is more common: the roof of the house, though for organisations — the company's policy.
mistakes: the car of Tom → Tom's car — with people 's is preferred.
the childrens' toys → the children's toys — children is already plural, apostrophe before s.
its' color → its color — its is never written with an apostrophe.
James car → James's car / James' car — the apostrophe is required.

el_u65.note: a book, an apple, a university, an hour
el_u65.exp:
Unit 65 — the indefinite article a/an before a countable noun in the singular.

Meanings: 'one of many', first mention, classification, profession, generalisation of a class.

The choice of form is determined by the sound, not the letter:

  • a — before a consonant sound: a book, a car, a university [juː], a European, a one-way street [w].
  • an — before a vowel sound: an apple, an hour (silent h), an honest man, an MP [em-piː].

Typical cases:

  • Professions: She is a teacher.
  • First mention: I saw a dog in the park.
  • Generalisation: A cat is a mammal.
  • In exclamations: What a surprise!

Not used with uncountables (water, music) or with plurals — instead use some or zero article.
el_u65.exc: The choice is by sound, not letter: a university, a European, a one-way street (sounds /j/, /w/); an hour, an honest man, an MP (silent h or vowel letter sound).
mistakes: an university → a university — starts with the sound /juː/.
a hour → an hour — h is silent, the sound is a vowel.
She is doctor → She is a doctor — a singular profession needs an article.
a advice → uncountable, some advice — a is impossible.

el_u66.exp:
Unit 66 — formation of the plural of nouns.

Basic rule: add -s. Ending variants depend on the word's final letters.

  • Standard: book → books, cat → cats.
  • After -s, -ss, -sh, -ch, -x, -o: add -es — bus → buses, box → boxes, tomato → tomatoes.
  • Consonant + -y → -ies: city → cities, story → stories. Vowel + -y → just -s: boy → boys.
  • -f/-fe → -ves: leaf → leaves, knife → knives, wife → wives.

Irregular forms (to memorise): man → men, woman → women, child → children, foot → feet, tooth → teeth, mouse → mice, person → people.

Identical singular and plural forms: sheep, fish, deer, aircraft, series, species. Example: I saw three deer. Pluralia tantum nouns (plural only): trousers, jeans, scissors, glasses — with a plural verb.
el_u66.exc: Unchanging: sheep, fish, deer, aircraft, series. Plural only: trousers, jeans, scissors, glasses, clothes. Latin: analysis→analyses, crisis→crises, phenomenon→phenomena.
mistakes: childrens → children — already a plural form.
two sheeps → two sheep — the form does not change.
my trouser is → my trousers are — the word is always plural.
womans → women — vowel change.

el_u67.exp:
Unit 67 — uncountable nouns: they have no plural form and do not combine with a/an.

These include substances, abstract concepts, and collectives: water, milk, bread, money, time, music, love, advice, information, news, furniture, luggage, work, weather, traffic.

The verb is singular: The news is bad. Your advice was helpful.

To indicate a quantity/portion use of constructions:

  • a piece of advice / news / furniture.
  • a glass of water, a cup of tea, a slice of bread, a bar of chocolate.
  • some / any / much / a little / a lot of + uncountable: some milk, much time, a little sugar.

Some words can be both countable and uncountable with different meanings: a paper (newspaper) / paper (material); a coffee (cup of coffee) / coffee (coffee as a product); an experience (an event) / experience (knowledge).
el_u67.exc: Some words can be both countable and uncountable with different meanings: a hair (a single hair) / hair (hair in general); a paper (newspaper) / paper (material); a coffee (cup of coffee) / coffee (coffee as a drink).
mistakes: an advice / advices → a piece of advice / some advice — uncountable.
informations → information — without -s.
a news → some news / a piece of news — though it ends in -s, this is a singular uncountable.
furnitures → furniture — a collective uncountable.

el_u68.note: some water, some advice, some music
el_u68.exp:
Unit 68 — use of some with uncountables and plurals.

Some means 'a certain amount, several' and corresponds to 'a little / some / a few' or zero in translation.

Main rules:

  • In affirmative sentences: I need some water. There are some apples on the table.
  • In polite offers and requests (expecting 'yes'): Would you like some coffee? Can I have some sugar?
  • In neutral questions and negatives use any instead of some: Is there any milk? I don't have any money.

With a singular countable some is not used — there a/an applies. In the meaning 'some, a certain' some can also stand before a singular: Some man called you. Its pronunciation in this meaning is strong /sʌm/, while for 'a little' it is weak /səm/.
el_u68.exc: Some in questions — when expecting 'yes' or when offering/requesting: Can I have some water? In 'real' questions about existence use any: Is there any water?
mistakes: I don't have some money → I don't have any money — in a negative use any.
Would you like any tea? → Would you like some tea? — an offer requires some.
some informations → some information — uncountable without -s.
Is there some milk? → Is there any milk? — a neutral question.

el_u69.note: I saw a dog. The dog was brown.
el_u69.exp:
Unit 69 — the fundamental difference between a/an and the.

a/an — indefiniteness: the listener does not know which specific object is meant; one of many, mentioned for the first time, any representative of a class.

the — definiteness: the object is known to both speakers from context, situation, shared knowledge, or has been mentioned before; unique of its kind.

  • I bought a book yesterday. The book is really interesting. (second mention → the.)
  • Close the door, please. (a specific, known door.)
  • The sun is shining. (unique object.)
  • She is a nurse. (profession — always a/an.)

Signals of the: same, only, first, last, next, right, wrong, superlative degree, restrictive relative clauses (the man who called), geographical objects with plural names (the USA, the Alps).
el_u69.exc: Before unique objects always the: the sun, the moon, the world. On first mention, even of an object unique to the speakers, a may appear: I had a strange dream.
mistakes: I saw the dog in the street for the first time → I saw a dog — first mention.
Sun is shining → The sun is shining — unique of its kind.
Close door, please → Close the door, please — a specific door known to the listener.
She is the teacher (about profession) → She is a teacher — profession: a/an.

el_u70.note: the sun, the airport, the same, play the piano
el_u70.exp:
Unit 70 — systematisation of the uses of the definite article the.

The is used when an object is unique, specified by context, or belongs to a fixed group.

  • Unique objects: the sun, the moon, the Earth, the sky, the world, the universe.
  • Specified by context: the kitchen (in our house), the manager (in this shop).
  • After same, only, first, second, next, last: the same day, the first time, the last chance.
  • With the superlative degree: the tallest building, the best student.
  • Musical instruments: play the piano / the guitar / the violin.
  • Adjectives as groups of people: the rich, the poor, the elderly, the British.
  • Some geographical features: oceans, rivers, mountain ranges, countries in the plural (the Pacific, the Nile, the Alps, the Netherlands).

Examples: The moon goes round the Earth. She plays the piano. He is the only friend I have.
el_u70.exc: No the before language names: I speak English (but the English language). Next/last week — without the relative to the moment of speech; the next day — in narratives about the past.
mistakes: play piano → play the piano — musical instruments take the.
She is best student → She is the best student — superlative with the.
on first day → on the first day — after first/next/last/same.
Moon is bright → The moon is bright — unique object.

el_u71.text: Unit 71 – The: when you need it and when you don’t
el_u71.note: go to school / go to the cinema
el_u71.exp:
Unit 71 — when the article the is omitted before familiar nouns.

Zero article is used if the place/action is taken in its typical function, not as a specific object.

  • Places by purpose: go to school / work / church / university / bed / hospital / prison. Tom goes to school every day. (He is a pupil.) Compare His mother went to the school to see him. (As a visitor — the.)
  • Meals: have breakfast / lunch / dinner — no article. But the dinner (that) we had yesterday — with a modifier.
  • Languages: speak English / French / Russian.
  • Sports and games: play football / chess / tennis.
  • Seasons and months in a general sense: in summer, in July (or in the summer).

Contrasting pairs: go to bed (to sleep) vs sit on the bed (on a specific bed); at home vs in the house (inside the house); at school (studying) vs at the school (at the school building).
el_u71.exc: The phrase in bed means 'sleeping/lying down', while in the bed means literally 'in the bed' as a piece of furniture. Similarly at school (studying) vs at the school (in the school building as a visitor).
mistakes: go to the school every day (of a pupil) → go to school — attendance by purpose.
have the breakfast → have breakfast — meals without an article.
play the football → play football — sports without an article.
learn the English → learn English — languages without an article.

el_u72.note: the radio / television / phone / the bus
el_u72.exp:
Unit 72 — fixed expressions with and without the in everyday speech.

Memorised as ready-made phrases — general rules are not enough.

  • With the: the cinema, the theatre, the radio, the phone, the internet, the bank, the doctor, the dentist, the post office, the station. Listen to the radio. I saw her at the cinema.
  • Without the: television / TV (to watch), bed (to sleep), home, work, transport after by — by car, by bus, by train, by plane, on foot.
  • Fixed: on holiday, on business, on the phone, at home, at work.

Examples: I watch TV in the evening, but I also listen to the radio. They travel by car, not by train. She is still on the phone.

Compare: in bed (in bed, sleeping) vs on the bed (sitting on the bed); go home (no preposition and no article) — home works as an adverb.
el_u72.exc: Go to work (no the), but go to the cinema/theatre (with the). At home — no article; but in the house. These expressions must be memorised as set phrases.
mistakes: watch the television → watch television / watch TV — no article.
listen to radio → listen to the radio — with the.
go to the bed → go to bed — fixed phrase without the.
by the bus → by bus — transport after by without an article.

el_u73.note: the UK, the Thames, France, London
el_u73.exp:
Unit 73 — rules for using the with geographical names.

Without article:

  • Continents: Europe, Asia, Africa.
  • Most countries: France, Germany, Russia, Japan.
  • States, counties, regions: Texas, Yorkshire.
  • Cities: London, Paris, Moscow.
  • Individual mountains and lakes: Mount Everest, Lake Baikal.
  • Streets, squares, parks: Oxford Street, Trafalgar Square, Central Park.

With the:

  • Countries with common names / in the plural: the UK, the USA, the Netherlands, the Philippines, the Czech Republic.
  • Oceans, seas, rivers, canals: the Atlantic, the Mediterranean, the Thames, the Suez Canal.
  • Mountain ranges and island groups: the Alps, the Himalayas, the Bahamas.
  • Deserts: the Sahara, the Gobi.
  • Hotels, museums, theatres, cinemas: the Hilton, the British Museum, the Odeon.

Examples: We flew from London to the USA across the Atlantic. We climbed Mount Fuji but not the Alps.
el_u73.exc: Without the: individual lakes (Lake Baikal), individual mountains (Mount Everest), streets (Oxford Street). With the: deserts (the Sahara), island groups (the Bahamas), hotels (the Hilton).
mistakes: the France → France — a singular country without the.
Alps → the Alps — mountain ranges with the.
United States → the United States — country with a common name.
the London → London — cities without an article.

el_u74.note: this book / these books / that car / those cars
el_u74.exp:
Unit 74 — demonstrative pronouns this, these, that, those.

They point to an object by distance (physical or emotional) and by number.

  • this (sing.) / these (pl.) — close to the speaker, 'this / these': This book is mine. These shoes are new.
  • that (sing.) / those (pl.) — far away, 'that / those': That car over there is fast. Those days were happy.

Functions:

  • As a modifier before a noun: this pen, those people.
  • Independently: What is this? Who are those?
  • On the phone (BrE): Hello, this is Anna. Is that Tom? (AmE: Is this Tom?)
  • Reference to what has been said: That's a good idea.

Emotional tone: that may express distancing or disapproval — I don't like that music. In the pair this and that = 'this and that'.
el_u74.exc: In phone conversations: BrE Is that Tom? — This is Anna. In AmE more often Is this Tom? That may express emotional distancing: I don't like that idea.
mistakes: this books → these books — this only with singular.
that childs → those children — plural form.
This is my friends → These are my friends — number agreement.
I like these music → I like this music — music is uncountable.

el_u75.note: Which one? I want the blue one.
el_u75.exp:
Unit 75 — substitute pronoun one / ones for countable nouns.

Replaces a previously mentioned noun to avoid repetition.

  • one — for singular: I need a pen. Have you got one?
  • ones — for plural: These apples are bad. The red ones are better.

Typical combinations:

  • After an adjective: the blue one, a big one, a new one.
  • After this/that/which: Which one do you want? I'll take this one.
  • After the with a modifier: the one on the table, the ones I bought yesterday.

Examples: My car is old. I want a new one. These cups are dirty; give me clean ones.

Not used with uncountables: you cannot say I need water. Give me one. → Give me some. After my/his/her/your without an adjective — no one: That's mine, not my one.
el_u75.exc: One/ones is not used with uncountables: you cannot say I need some water. Give me one. → Give me some. After my, his, her without a noun — no one: That's mine, not my one (though it occurs in speech).
mistakes: I want a red one shoes → I want red ones — with a plural the form is ones.
This bread is better than that one → than that — uncountable, one is unnecessary.
the blue ones shirt → the blue one — one replaces the noun.
Which ones you want? → Which ones do you want? — an auxiliary is needed.

el_u76.exp:
Unit 76 — distribution of some and any in sentences.

Rule: some — in affirmatives and polite offers; any — in negatives, questions, and after if.

  • Affirmative: There is some milk in the fridge.
  • Negative: There isn't any milk.
  • Question: Is there any milk?
  • Offer/request (expecting 'yes'): Would you like some tea? Can I have some water?

Derivatives follow the same principle:

  • someone / somebody, something, somewhere — in affirmatives.
  • anyone / anybody, anything, anywhere — in negatives and questions.

Special case: any in an affirmative = 'any' (no restriction on choice):

  • You can take any book you like.
  • Anyone can do this.

After if usually any: If you have any questions, ask.
el_u76.exc: Any in an affirmative = 'any (whatever)': Take any book you like. In conditional clauses with if usually any: If you have any questions, ask.
mistakes: I have any money → I have some money — in an affirmative use some.
There isn't some bread → There isn't any bread — in a negative use any.
somewhere to go? No, nowhere → in a question use anywhere: Anywhere to go?
I don't know something → I don't know anything — after a negative any-.

el_u77.note: There is no milk. None of them came.
el_u77.exp:
Unit 77 — negative quantifiers no, none and the not … any construction.

Three ways to express 'no / not any':

  • no + noun (emphatic): There is no time. I have no idea.
  • not … any + noun (neutral): There isn't any time. I don't have any idea.
  • none — without a noun, as a replacement: How much money do you have? — None.

Key rule: in English there is only one negative per clause. If the verb is already negative, only any (not no/none) may follow.

  • I don't have any friends here. = I have no friends here.
  • There weren't any people in the shop. = There were no people in the shop.

None of + determiner + noun: None of my friends came. None of the milk was fresh. After none of + plural the verb may be singular (formally) or plural (colloquially).
el_u77.exc: None of + plural noun may take the verb in either singular (formal) or plural (colloquial): None of us knows / know.
mistakes: I don't know nothing → I don't know anything / I know nothing — one negative.
There is none milk → There is no milk — before a noun use no.
No of them came → None of them came — before of use none.
He hasn't no money → He has no money / He hasn't any money.

el_u78.note: Nobody came. I didn't see anybody.
el_u78.exp:
Unit 78 — negative pronouns nobody / no one, nothing, nowhere and their relation to not anybody etc.

Equivalent forms:

  • nobody / no one = not anybody / not anyone
  • nothing = not anything
  • nowhere = not anywhere

One-negative rule: after nobody/nothing/nowhere the verb is affirmative.

  • Nobody knows the answer.
  • Nothing happened yesterday.
  • There is nowhere to go.
  • Cf.: I didn't see anybody. = I saw nobody.

After nobody/no one/nothing the verb is singular, but the referring pronoun is usually they/their/them (gender-neutral): Nobody brought their umbrella. Did anyone forget their ticket?

In tag questions after negative meaning — affirmative tag: Nobody came, did they?
el_u78.exc: Nobody/no one/nothing agree with a singular verb, but the referring pronoun is often they/their: Nobody brought their umbrella.
mistakes: Nobody don't like it → Nobody likes it — no second negative.
I didn't see nothing → I didn't see anything / I saw nothing.
Anyone is here (as a negative) → No one is here — in a statement about no one — nobody/no one.
Nothing happened, didn't it? → Nothing happened, did it? — the tag is affirmative.

el_u79.note: Is somebody there? Anybody can do this.
el_u79.exp:
Unit 79 — the full system of indefinite pronouns with suffixes -body / -one / -thing / -where.

Form matrix:

  • People: somebody / someone, anybody / anyone, nobody / no one, everybody / everyone.
  • Things: something, anything, nothing, everything.
  • Places: somewhere, anywhere, nowhere, everywhere.

-body and -one are interchangeable with the same meaning. The choice among some-/any-/no- follows the general rule: affirmative — some-, negative/question — any-, negative without the particle not — no-.

Examples:

  • Someone is knocking at the door.
  • Is there anything to eat?
  • There's nothing in the box.
  • Everyone was happy at the party.

After these words the verb is singular: Everybody is here. The meaning 'any' of any- in an affirmative: Anyone can learn English.
el_u79.exc: After somebody/anybody/nobody the verb is singular, but the referring pronoun is often they: Someone left their bag. In AmE: somebody and someone are interchangeable.
mistakes: Somebody are waiting → Somebody is waiting — singular.
I need nothing anywhere → I don't need anything anywhere — don't mix the systems.
Is there somebody? (a real question) → Is there anybody? — the standard for a question.
Nobody didn't come → Nobody came — one negative.

el_u80.exp:
Unit 80 — the difference between every and all.

Every means 'each' with emphasis on separate units and is used with a singular noun and singular verb:

  • Every student has a book.
  • Every day I drink coffee.
  • Every + derivatives: everyone / everybody, everything, everywhere — also with a singular verb: Everyone is ready.

All means 'all / every' as a collective whole, used with plurals or uncountables:

  • All students must come.
  • I worked all day.
  • All the water was dirty.

Contrast: Every day (every day, 365 times) ≠ all day (all day long, from morning till evening). Time expressions: every year / month / Monday and all year / month / Monday. Derivatives with every- are always written as one word and act as singular pronouns.
el_u80.exc: All day/all night/all week — no article (= 'all day long'). Every day ≠ everyday (adj. 'routine, daily'): an everyday routine.
mistakes: every students → every student — after every singular.
Everyone are happy → Everyone is happy — singular verb.
all the day I worked → all day I worked — set phrase without the.
I go there every days → every day — singular.

el_u81.text: Unit 81 - All, most, some, any, no
el_u81.note: All cats / Most people / Some water / No idea
el_u81.exp:
Unit 81 — quantifiers all, most, some, any, no with nouns and with of.

Two registers of use:

  • Without of — general statements about a class: All birds can fly. Most children like sweets. Some people are lucky. I have no money.
  • With of — before a pronoun or a noun with a determiner (the, my, this): All of us agree. Most of the students passed. Some of my friends live abroad. None of them came.

The form no before of is impossible — instead use none: none of the books, none of us.

All before the/my/this allows of to be dropped: all (of) my friends, all (of) the time. With a personal pronoun of is required: all of us, not all we.

Examples: Some people like tea; some of my friends drink it every day. Most animals sleep at night, but most of my cats sleep all day.
el_u81.exc: All can stand before the/my/this without of: all (of) my friends. With pronouns of is required: all of us, not all we.
mistakes: All of people → All people / All of the people — of requires a determiner.
Most of students → Most students / Most of the students.
All we → All of us — with a pronoun of is required.
No of them → None of them — before of the form is none.

el_u82.note: Both sisters / Either day / Neither option
el_u82.exp:
Unit 82 — paired quantifiers both, either, neither for two objects.

Meanings:

  • both — both / the one and the other (positive).
  • either — any one of the two / one or the other.
  • neither — neither one nor the other (negative without not).

Forms:

  • Before a noun: both sisters, either day, neither answer.
  • With of + determiner: both of the girls, either of us, neither of them.
  • Correlative conjunctions: both … and, either … or, neither … nor.

Verb agreement:

  • After both — always plural: Both are correct.
  • After either/neither — usually singular (formally): Neither of the answers is right. In speech plural is acceptable.

Examples: Both of my parents are teachers. You can come on Monday or Tuesday — either day is fine. Neither book was interesting. She speaks neither French nor German.
el_u82.exc: Neither + singular verb (formally): Neither of them is right. In speech plural is allowed. After both the verb is only plural: Both are correct.
mistakes: Both of sisters → Both sisters / Both of the sisters.
Neither answers is right → Neither answer is right — after neither singular.
I don't like neither → I don't like either — no double negative.
Both my parents is → Both my parents are — plural.

el_u83.text: Unit 83 – Much, many, a lot of
