el_u83.note: much water / many friends / a lot of work
el_u83.exp:
Unit 83 — quantity expressions much, many, a lot of / lots of.

The choice depends on the countability of the noun and the type of sentence.

  • many + countable plural: many books, many people, many problems.
  • much + uncountable: much time, much money, much information.
  • a lot of / lots of + both types: a lot of friends, a lot of water.

Stylistic rule:

  • In affirmatives a lot of is preferred: I have a lot of work. (much work — formal.)
  • In negatives and questions — much/many: Do you have many friends? I don't have much money.

After how, so, too, as — always much/many, not a lot of: How much is it? There are too many cars. I have so much work!

Without a noun: He works a lot. Thanks a lot. Examples: How many languages do you speak? — I speak three. How much time do you need? — Not much.
el_u83.exc: In affirmatives much/many sound formal: He has a lot of money — neutral, He has much money — unusual. But too much/so much/as much are normal in an affirmative.
mistakes: much books → many books — countable.
many water → much water / a lot of water.
I have much friends → I have a lot of friends / many friends — in an affirmative.
How many money? → How much money? — uncountable.

el_u84.note: few friends / little time / a few things
el_u84.exp:
Unit 84 — the quantitative contrast of (a) few and (a) little.

The form depends on countability, the meaning — on the presence of the article a.

  • few + countable plural = 'few, hardly any' (negative assessment): He has few friends and feels lonely.
  • a few + countable plural = 'several, some' (positive): He has a few good friends.
  • little + uncountable = 'little, not enough': We have little time — hurry!
  • a little + uncountable = 'a bit, enough': We have a little time — we can rest.

Signal combinations:

  • very few / very little intensify 'little': Very few people came.
  • quite a few / quite a lot of = 'quite a lot' (for countables).
  • only a few / only a little = 'just a few / a little'.

Examples: Can you lend me a little money? I understood little of what she said.
el_u84.exc: Quite a few / quite a little = 'quite a lot'. Only a few / only a little = 'just a few'. Incorrect to say a few water — with uncountables only a little.
mistakes: a few water → a little water — uncountable.
little friends (meaning 'small') → small friends — little here = 'few'.
I have few money → I have little money.
There are a little chairs → a few chairs — countable.

el_u85.note: a beautiful day / the sky is blue
el_u85.exp:
Unit 85 — adjectives: describing nouns.

An adjective names a quality and does not change for gender, number, or case: a big house, big houses, big cars.

Two positions:

  • Attributive — before a noun: a beautiful garden, an interesting book.
  • Predicative — after linking verbs be, seem, look, feel, taste, smell, become, get: The garden is beautiful. She looks tired. This tastes good.

Order of multiple adjectives: Opinion → Size → Age → Shape → Colour → Origin → Material → Purpose: a lovely small old round wooden dining table.

-ed / -ing pairs:

  • -ed — feeling: I am bored / tired / interested / excited.
  • -ing — source of the feeling: The film is boring. The news is exciting.

Examples: I'm interested in this interesting topic. She was surprised by the surprising result.
el_u85.exc: Adjectives in -ed describe a feeling (I'm bored), in -ing — the source (The book is boring). Order: Opinion-Size-Age-Shape-Colour-Origin-Material (a lovely small old round wooden table).
mistakes: a house beautiful → a beautiful house — adjective before the noun.
I'm boring of this film → I'm bored with this film — feeling via -ed.
happys children → happy children — adjectives do not change.
a red big car → a big red car — size first, then colour.

el_u86.exp:
Unit 86 — adverbs of manner: answering the question 'how?'

Formation: adjective + -ly.

  • slow → slowly, quick → quickly, careful → carefully, beautiful → beautifully.
  • Ending in -y: y → ily — happy → happily, easy → easily.
  • Ending in -le: le → ly — simple → simply.

Special forms (adjective = adverb):

  • fast, hard, late, early, high, low, near, far, daily, weekly.
  • He runs fast. She works hard. We arrived late.

Exception: good → well. She plays the piano well.

Meaning changes in pairs:

  • hard (diligently) vs hardly (barely): He works hard ≠ He hardly works.
  • late (late) vs lately (recently).
  • near (close) vs nearly (almost).

An adverb describes a verb (drive carefully), an adjective (incredibly beautiful), or another adverb (very quickly).
el_u86.exc: Hardly ≠ hard: He works hard (diligently) vs He hardly works (barely works). Late/lately: arrive late vs lately (recently). Friendly, lovely, silly — adjectives, not adverbs.
mistakes: She speaks very good → She speaks very well — an adverb after the verb.
He drives careful → He drives carefully.
She sings beautiful → She sings beautifully.
He runs quick → He runs quickly (or fast).

el_u87.exp:
Unit 87 — comparative degree of adjectives.

Formation depends on the number of syllables.

  • 1 syllable: + -er — old → older, cheap → cheaper, fast → faster.
  • 1 syllable ending in cons.+vowel+cons.: doubling — big → bigger, hot → hotter, thin → thinner.
  • Ending in -y (2 syllables): y → ier — happy → happier, busy → busier, easy → easier.
  • 2+ syllables: more + adjective — more expensive, more interesting, more beautiful.

Irregular forms:

  • good → better, bad → worse, far → farther / further, little → less, much/many → more.

Some two-syllable adjectives allow both forms: clever → cleverer / more clever, quiet → quieter / more quiet, simple → simpler / more simple.

Examples: My new car is faster than my old one. This book is more interesting than that one. Today is hotter than yesterday.
el_u87.exc: Two-syllable words ending in -y take -ier: happy→happier, busy→busier. Some two-syllable adjectives allow both variants: clever→cleverer / more clever; quiet→quieter / more quiet.
mistakes: more big → bigger — one-syllable adjective.
more better → better — double comparison is not allowed.
more happy → happier — two-syllable in -y.
expensiver → more expensive — long adjective.

el_u88.note: She is taller than me. It's better than I thought.
el_u88.exp:
Unit 88 — comparison with the conjunction than.

Structure: A + comparative + than + B.

  • Tom is taller than Jim.
  • This book is more interesting than that one.

Pronoun form after than:

  • Colloquial norm — objective case: taller than me / him / her / them.
  • Formal norm — nominative with a verb: taller than I am / than he is.

Intensifiers before the comparative:

  • 'Significantly': much, far, a lot, way — much taller, far more expensive, a lot better.
  • 'A tiny bit': a bit, a little, slightly — a bit older, slightly cheaper.
  • very + comparative is not allowed (very taller is impossible).

Comparison of numbers: more than 100 people, less than an hour. Parallel increase: The older he gets, the wiser he becomes. It's getting colder and colder.
el_u88.exc: In comparisons with intensification: much/a lot/far + comparative (much taller); a bit/slightly + comparative (a bit older). Not allowed: very taller.
mistakes: taller that me → taller than me — the conjunction is than, not that.
very taller → much taller — intensification is not via very.
more better than → better than — without more.
She is tall than him → She is taller than him — the comparative form is needed.

el_u89.note: I'm not as tall as you.
el_u89.exp:
Unit 89 — the (not) as … as construction for comparing equality.

Statement of equality — as + adjective/adverb + as:

  • Tom is as tall as Jim.
  • She runs as fast as her brother.

Denial of equality — not as … as (= softer than than):

  • This hotel isn't as expensive as the other one. = The other hotel is more expensive.
  • Today isn't as cold as yesterday.

Constructions with quantity:

  • as much + uncount. + as: I have as much money as you.
  • as many + count. pl. + as: She has as many books as me.

Related expressions:

  • the same as (the same as): My bag is the same as yours. (not the same like).
  • different from (different from): Her answer is different from mine.
  • Colloquial equivalent of not as … as — not so … as.
el_u89.exc: The same as (not the same like): My bag is the same as yours. Possible: as much/many as: I have as many books as you.
mistakes: as tall than → as tall as — the as…as construction.
the same like → the same as.
She is so tall as me → She is as tall as me — in an affirmative use as…as.
not as taller as → not as tall as — the base form of the adjective.

el_u90.note: the oldest, the most expensive, the best
el_u90.exp:
Unit 90 — the superlative degree: the extreme of a quality.

Forms parallel the comparative:

  • 1 syllable: the + adj. + -est — the oldest, the cheapest, the biggest.
  • Ending in -y: the + adj. + -iest — the happiest, the easiest.
  • 2+ syllables: the most + adj. — the most expensive, the most beautiful.

Irregular: good → the best, bad → the worst, far → the farthest / furthest, little → the least, much/many → the most.

The article the is required (sometimes a possessive: my best friend).

Prepositions after the superlative:

  • in with a place/group: the tallest building in the world, the best student in the class.
  • of before an enumeration: the youngest of the three brothers.

Often followed by Present Perfect: It's the best film I've ever seen.

Examples: Mount Everest is the highest mountain on Earth. She's the most intelligent person I know.
el_u90.exc: After the superlative degree usually in (place/group): the best in the world, but of before people/things: the youngest of the three. Afterwards — Present Perfect: the best film I've ever seen.
mistakes: the most tall → the tallest — one-syllable with -est.
best student → the best student — the article is needed.
the most best → the best — no most.
the interestingest → the most interesting — long via most.

el_u91.note: good enough / enough money / old enough to drive
el_u91.exp:
Unit 91 — the word enough.

Position depends on part of speech:

  • After an adjective/adverb: fast enough, old enough, warm enough, well enough.
  • Before a noun: enough money, enough time, enough people.

Construction enough + to-infinitive:

  • She is old enough to drive.
  • Is it warm enough to swim?
  • I don't have enough money to buy it.

Alternative construction with for + noun: This box is big enough for all my books. With an infinitive subject: The coffee is hot enough for me to drink.

Negative: not … enough = 'not enough': He isn't tall enough to reach the shelf.

Independently: Have you got enough? — Yes, thanks, that's enough.
el_u91.exc: Enough stands before a noun but after an adjective/adverb: enough money, warm enough. Sometimes enough stands after a noun (rare, archaic): time enough.
mistakes: enough old → old enough — after an adjective.
money enough → enough money — before a noun.
old enough for drive → old enough to drive — to-infinitive.
She isn't tall enough for reach it → tall enough to reach it.

el_u92.note: too tired to work / too much / too many
el_u92.exp:
Unit 92 — the word too (excessively).

Always carries a negative tone — the quality 'prevents' something from being done.

Structure too + adj./adv. (+ to-infinitive):

  • It's too cold to swim.
  • She's too young to drive.
  • He speaks too quickly for me to understand.

With nouns — too much / too many:

  • too much + uncount.: too much sugar, too much noise, too much work.
  • too many + count. pl.: too many people, too many mistakes.

Contrast too vs very:

  • very — neutral: The coffee is very hot (but I drink it).
  • too — excessively: The coffee is too hot (to drink) — I cannot drink it.

Not to be confused with the homonym too = 'also' (at the end of a phrase): I'm tired too.

With for + person: These shoes are too small for me.
el_u92.exc: Too ≠ very: very cold (neutral) vs too cold (excessively, preventing action). Too in the meaning 'also' goes at the end: I'm tired too.
mistakes: too much books → too many books — countable.
too many water → too much water.
She is too beautiful (as a compliment) → She is very beautiful — too carries a negative tone.
too tired for work → too tired to work — to-infinitive.

el_u93.text: Unit 93 - Word Order in a Sentence
el_u93.note: She always works hard at the office.
el_u93.exp:
Unit 93 — basic word order in an English sentence.

Standard scheme: Subject → Verb → Object → Manner → Place → Time (SVOMPT).

  • She (S) reads (V) books (O) carefully (M) in the library (P) every evening (T).

Key rule: never place an adverbial between a verb and its direct object. You cannot say She reads carefully books — correct is She reads books carefully.

Order of adverbials: as a rule, place first, then time: I met him at the station yesterday. However, short time adverbials may start the sentence: Yesterday I met him at the station.

Examples:

  • I drink coffee every morning at home.
  • He speaks English very well.
  • We went to the cinema last Saturday.

A long object may 'shift' an adverbial closer to the verb: She opened carefully the box she had been given.
el_u93.exc: Adverbs of manner are usually at the end: She speaks English well. If the object is long, the adverb may come before it: He opened carefully the box he had found.
mistakes: I like very much chocolate → I like chocolate very much — the adverb follows the object.
She reads every day books → She reads books every day.
He speaks very well English → He speaks English very well.
I saw yesterday him → I saw him yesterday.

el_u94.text: Unit 94 – Place of adverbs of frequency
el_u94.exp:
Unit 94 — position of adverbs of frequency in a sentence.

Adverbs in decreasing frequency: always (100%) → usually → normally → often → sometimes → occasionally → seldom → rarely → hardly ever → never (0%).

Position rules:

  • Before the main verb: I always get up at 7. She often visits her parents.
  • After the verb be: He is always late. They are usually busy.
  • After the first auxiliary, before the main verb: I have never been to Paris. She doesn't usually drink coffee.

Only one negative: He never smokes, not He doesn't never smoke.

Flexible-position adverbs — sometimes, usually, often, occasionally — may stand at the beginning or end of the sentence for emphasis: Sometimes I work late. / I work late sometimes.

Never, rarely, seldom, hardly ever — only in the middle; fronting them triggers inversion (formal): Never have I seen such a thing.
el_u94.exc: Sometimes, usually, often may stand at the beginning or end of a sentence: Sometimes I work late. Never, rarely, seldom — only in the middle (or initially with inversion, which is formal).
mistakes: I go always to school → I always go to school — before the main verb.
She always is late → She is always late — after be.
He doesn't never smoke → He never smokes — one negative.
Always I wake up early → I always wake up early.

el_u95.note: I'm still waiting. She's already left.
el_u95.exp:
Unit 95 — the adverbs still, yet, already and their position.

still (still, until now) — the situation continues longer than expected:

  • Position: before the main verb, after be/auxiliary: I still live here. She is still waiting. He still hasn't called.

yet (yet? / not yet) — in questions and negatives, at the end of the phrase:

  • Have you finished yet? I haven't finished yet.

already (already) — in affirmatives, earlier than expected; in the middle:

  • She has already left.
  • I've already seen that film.

Contrasting nuances:

  • still + negative expresses irritation: He still hasn't answered. (but he should have).
  • not yet — neutral 'not yet': Not yet, but soon.
  • already in a question = surprise: Have you finished already?

With tenses: yet / already more often with Present Perfect (BrE); AmE allows Past Simple.
el_u95.exc: Still in negatives expresses dissatisfaction with a delay: She still hasn't called (but she should have). Not yet — neutral 'not yet'. Already in questions = surprise: Have you finished already?
mistakes: I haven't finished already → I haven't finished yet — in a negative use yet.
She already is here → She is already here — after be.
Did you see him yet? (BrE) → Have you seen him yet? — yet with Perfect.
He still not came → He still hasn't come / He hasn't come yet.

el_u96.text: Unit 96 – Give, lend, pass, send, show + two additions
el_u96.note: Give me the book. / Give the book to me.
el_u96.exp:
Unit 96 — two objects after verbs of giving and transferring.

The verbs give, send, lend, pass, show, tell, teach, offer, bring, buy, make, write take a direct (what?) and indirect (to whom?) object.

Two possible orders:

  • Without preposition: verb + indirect (to whom) + direct (what). Give me the book. She told us the story.
  • With preposition to / for: verb + direct + to/for + indirect. Give the book to me. She told the story to us.

The choice depends on focus:

  • With a pronoun direct object usually only the prepositional form: Give it to me (not Give me it).
  • A long or new object goes at the end: She gave her friend a beautiful handmade scarf.

The preposition for is used with buy, make, cook, get, find: I bought a present for my mother. / I bought my mother a present.

Exceptions: explain, describe, suggest, mention, introduce require only the prepositional construction: Explain the rule to me (not Explain me the rule).
el_u96.exc: With the verbs explain and describe the double-object without to is impossible: explain something to somebody (not explain me something).
mistakes: Give me it → Give it to me — with a pronoun direct object the preposition is required.
Explain me the rule → Explain the rule to me.
She showed to me the photo → She showed me the photo / showed the photo to me.
He said me the news → He told me the news — say does not take a double object.

el_u97.text: Unit 97 - And, but, or, so, because
el_u97.note: I was tired, so I went to bed.
el_u97.exp:
Unit 97 — the main coordinating and subordinating conjunctions: and, but, or, so, because.

  • and — addition: I got up and had breakfast.
  • but — contrast: I'm tired but happy.
  • or — alternative: Tea or coffee?
  • so — result: It was late, so I went home.
  • because — reason: I went home because it was late.

The logic of so / because is opposite — the order of cause and effect is mirrored:

  • Cause + so + effect.
  • Effect + because + cause.

Punctuation: before and, but, or, so in compound sentences a comma is usually placed. Before because no comma if it is in the middle; a comma is placed if the subordinate clause is initial: Because it was raining, we stayed in.

Related connectors: although / though (concession): Although it rained, we had fun.
el_u97.exc: So and because are opposites: It rained, so I stayed home = I stayed home because it rained. Although/though — concession: Although it rained, we went out.
mistakes: Because it was late, so I left → choose one: Because it was late, I left / It was late, so I left.
I was tired, because I slept → I was tired, so I slept — result, not cause.
But however → choose one: but or however.
I like tea and I don't like coffee (contrast) → I like tea but I don't like coffee.

el_u98.text: Unit 98 - When and null conditional type (if)
el_u98.note: When it rains, I stay home. If you heat ice, it melts.
el_u98.exp:
Unit 98 — the conjunction when and Zero Conditional (general truths).

Main rule: in time and condition clauses the future tense is not used, even if the meaning is future — instead of will, Present Simple is used.

  • When I arrive, I'll call you. (not when I will arrive)
  • If it rains, we'll stay home.

Zero Conditional — always-true situations (laws of nature, habits, general facts):

Structure: If / When + Present Simple, Present Simple.

  • If you heat water to 100°C, it boils.
  • When the sun sets, it gets dark.
  • If I'm tired, I go to bed early.

In Zero Conditional if and when are almost interchangeable: both mean 'always when'.

Difference from First Conditional: Zero — a general truth (If I eat fish, I feel sick); First — a specific future event (If I eat fish tonight, I'll feel sick).
el_u98.exc: When about the future = present tense: When I see him, I'll tell him. In Zero Conditional if and when are almost interchangeable for general truths.
mistakes: When I will arrive, I'll call → When I arrive, I'll call — no will after when.
If water will boil → If water boils — in Zero Conditional both tenses are Present.
When I'll be ready → When I'm ready.
If it rains I will staying home → I'll stay home — infinitive after will.

el_u99.note: If it rains, I'll stay home.
el_u99.exp:
Unit 99 — First Conditional: a real condition in the future.

Used for probable or possible future events: 'if X happens, Y will happen'.

Structure:

  • If + Present Simple, will + bare infinitive.
  • The clauses can be swapped: with the subordinate clause first, use a comma.

Examples:

  • If it rains tomorrow, I'll stay at home.
  • She'll be angry if you're late.
  • If you study hard, you'll pass the exam.

Instead of will in the main clause you may have:

  • A modal: If you're tired, you can rest.
  • An imperative: If you see John, tell him to call me.
  • be going to: If he doesn't hurry, he's going to miss the train.

In the if-clause there is never will (except polite requests: If you will wait a moment…). Synonyms of if: unless (unless), provided that, as long as.
el_u99.exc: Instead of will in the main clause there may be an imperative or a modal: If you see him, tell him / you should tell him. No will after if, except requests: If you will wait a moment…
mistakes: If it will rain, I stay → If it rains, I'll stay — will only in the main clause.
If I will have time → If I have time.
If he comes, I will to help → I will help — no to after will.
I'll go if it won't rain → if it doesn't rain — Present after if.

int_u01.note: I am doing / She is working right now
int_u01.exp:
Unit 1 — Present Continuous describes an action happening at the moment of speaking, a temporary situation, or a pre-arranged future plan.

Formula: (+) S + am/is/are + V-ing; (−) am/is/are + not; (?) Am/Is/Are + S + V-ing?

Examples:
  • I'm writing an email now. — I'm writing an email now.
  • She is staying with friends this week. — She is staying with friends this week (temporarily).
  • We're flying to Rome on Friday. — We're flying to Rome on Friday (plan).
  • The climate is changing rapidly. — The climate is changing rapidly (ongoing process).
Signal words: now, at the moment, currently, today, this week, Look!, Listen!
Contrast: with Present Simple — permanent vs. temporary (He works in a bank vs. He's working from home this month).
int_u01.exc:
Stative verbs (know, want, believe, need, like, love, hate) are not used in Continuous.
Exceptions: have meaning 'eat/spend time' (I'm having lunch), see = 'meet' (I'm seeing her tonight).
mistakes: I am knowing the answer. → I know the answer. — stative verbs are not used in Continuous.
She works on a project now. → She is working on a project now. — now/at this moment requires Continuous.
I'm wanting some coffee. → I want some coffee. — want is a stative verb.

int_u02.note: I work / She works / general truths
int_u02.exp:
Unit 2 — Present Simple expresses permanent truths, regular actions, schedules, and habits.

Formula: (+) S + V(-s/-es for 3rd person sg.); (−) do/does + not + V; (?) Do/Does + S + V?

Examples:
  • Water boils at 100°C. — Water boils at 100°C (fact).
  • She teaches English at a university. — She teaches English (permanently).
  • I usually have coffee for breakfast. — I usually have coffee for breakfast (habit).
  • The museum opens at 10. — The museum opens at 10 (schedule).
Signal words: always, usually, often, sometimes, rarely, never, every day, twice a week.
Spelling: 3rd person sg. — -es after -o, -s, -x, -ch, -sh; -y → -ies (study → studies).
int_u02.exc:
Schedules and programs — Present Simple, even about the future: The train leaves at 8.
In 3rd person sg., -s/-es is mandatory: she works, he goes, it does.
mistakes: She work in a bank. → She works in a bank. — the -s was forgotten in 3rd person.
Do she like tea? → Does she like tea? — 3rd person needs does, and the verb without -s.
The Earth is revolving around the Sun. → The Earth revolves around the Sun. — permanent truths require Simple.

int_u03.text: Unit 3 – Present Simple or Present Continuous
int_u03.note: I work here. / I'm working from home this week.
int_u03.exp:
Unit 3 — choosing between Present Simple and Present Continuous: permanent vs. temporary.

Rule: Present Simple — for a general/permanent state of affairs (work, place of residence, habits). Present Continuous — for what is happening now or around now (temporary changes, ongoing processes).

Examples:
  • Tom lives in London, but he's living in Berlin at the moment. — Tom lives in London, but he's temporarily in Berlin now.
  • Prices are rising very fast. — Prices are rising fast (current process).
  • Water freezes at 0°C. — Water freezes at 0°C (law of nature).
Special case — emotions: always/constantly + Continuous expresses irritation: He's always losing his keys! — He's always losing his keys!
Key question: 'Is this a general truth or happening right now/temporarily?'
int_u03.exc:
Stative verbs are always Simple, even if the situation is 'now': I know it now.
Always + Continuous = irritation: He's always complaining!
mistakes: She lives with her parents this month. → She is living with her parents this month. — temporary situation.
I'm usually going to work by bus. → I usually go to work by bus. — regular action — Simple.
What you are doing? → What are you doing? — in the question, inversion are you.

int_u04.text: Unit 4 - Stative verbs: verbs not in Continuous
int_u04.note: I know / I understand / I want (not: I am knowing)
int_u04.exp:
Unit 4 — stative verbs are usually not used in Continuous.

Groups of stative verbs:
  • Thinking/opinion: know, believe, think (=consider), understand, remember, suppose, mean
  • Feelings: like, love, hate, prefer, want, need, wish
  • Perception: see, hear, smell, taste, seem, appear
  • Possession/relations: have (=own), own, belong, contain, consist, depend
Examples:
  • I know what you mean. — I understand what you mean.
  • This soup tastes great. — This soup tastes great.
Double meanings: think — opinion (Simple) vs. process of thinking (Continuous); have — own (Simple) vs. 'having/taking' (having lunch/a shower — Continuous); see — see (Simple) vs. meet (I'm seeing John tonight).
int_u04.exc:
Think, have, see change meaning: I'm thinking (considering) vs. I think (believe). I'm having a bath (taking) vs. I have a car (own).
Feel and look about well-being/appearance — allow both forms: I feel / I'm feeling tired.
mistakes: I am understanding now. → I understand now. — understand is a stative verb.
Are you believing me? → Do you believe me? — believe is not used in Continuous.
This dress is belonging to her. → This dress belongs to her. — belong is stative.

int_u05.note: I worked / Did you go? / I didn't see
int_u05.exp:
Unit 5 — Past Simple denotes a completed action at a specific moment in the past.

Formula: (+) regular: V+ed, irregular: V2; (−) did not (didn't) + V1; (?) Did + S + V1?

Examples:
  • We met in 2015. — We met in 2015.
  • She didn't call yesterday. — She didn't call yesterday.
  • Did you enjoy the film? — Did you enjoy the film?
  • Columbus discovered America in 1492. — Columbus discovered America in 1492.
Signal words: yesterday, last week/year, in 2010, ago, when I was young, then.
Spelling of -ed: studied, stopped, liked (doubling/softening). Important: after did, the verb is always in V1 form without -ed.
int_u05.exc:
Irregular verbs (go/went, see/saw, take/took) must be memorized.
In question/negative with did, the main verb is in V1 form: Did you go? (not Did you went?).
mistakes: I goed home. → I went home. — go is an irregular verb.
Did you saw the film? → Did you see the film? — after did — infinitive without to.
She didn't knew. → She didn't know. — in negatives didn't + V1.

int_u06.note: I was reading when you called.
int_u06.exp:
Unit 6 — Past Continuous describes an ongoing action at a specific moment in the past, or a background action that was interrupted.

Formula: was/were + V-ing.

Uses:
  • Ongoing at a moment: At 8 p.m. I was having dinner. — At 8 p.m. I was having dinner.
  • Interrupted event: I was reading when the lights went out. — I was reading when the lights went out.
  • Parallel processes: While he was cooking, she was setting the table. — While he was cooking, she was setting the table.
  • Story background: The sun was shining and birds were singing.
Contrast with Past Simple: Past Simple — brief completed event; Past Continuous — ongoing background (when/while + background, brief event — in Simple).
int_u06.exc:
Stative verbs are not used in Past Continuous: I knew (not I was knowing).
For background and interrupting event: Past Continuous + Past Simple.
mistakes: I was knowing him well. → I knew him well. — know is stative.
When she called, I cooked. → When she called, I was cooking. — ongoing background — Continuous.
I was watching TV while I did homework. → I was watching TV while I was doing homework. — two parallel processes.

int_u07.note: I have finished. She has left.
int_u07.exp:
Unit 7 — Present Perfect connects a past action with the present moment: the result matters, not exactly when it happened.

Formula: have/has + V3 (past participle).

Three meanings:
  • Present result: I've lost my keys (they are not here now).
  • Life experience: Have you ever been to Japan? — Have you ever been to Japan?
  • News/recent events: The President has resigned. — The President has resigned.
Examples:
  • She has written three books. — She has written three books (up to now).
  • The price has gone up. — The price has gone up.
Important: not combined with specific past time (yesterday, ago, in 1990). Compare: I've seen this film (experience) vs. I saw it last night (fact).
int_u07.exc:
With yesterday, ago, last week, in 2010 — only Past Simple.
With today, this week, this morning (if not yet finished) — Present Perfect.
mistakes: I have seen him yesterday. → I saw him yesterday. — with specific past — Past Simple.
She has went home. → She has gone home. — V3 is needed (gone).
I finished my homework (result matters). → I have finished my homework. — result now.

int_u08.exp:
Unit 8 — Present Perfect with typical adverbs: just, already, yet, ever, never, recently, lately.

Meanings and position:
  • just (just now) — between have and V3: She has just left. — She has just left.
  • already (already, earlier than expected) — between have and V3: I've already eaten. — I've already eaten.
  • yet (still/already) — at the end of negatives/questions: Has he arrived yet?
  • ever/never (ever/never): Have you ever tried sushi? / I've never smoked.
  • recently/lately: I haven't seen him lately.
BrE vs. AmE: in British — Present Perfect; in American with just/already/yet often Past Simple: Did you eat yet?
int_u08.exc:
Yet — at the end in questions/negatives. Already — between have and V3 (or at the end for surprise). Just — between have and V3.
In American English with just/already/yet often Past Simple: Did you eat yet?
mistakes: I have yet finished. → I haven't finished yet. — yet in negatives/questions.
Have you ever been to Paris? Yes, I have been there last year. → Yes, I went there last year. — specific time → Past Simple.
She has already went. → She has already gone. — V3 is needed.

int_u09.exp:
Unit 9 — Present Perfect Continuous emphasizes the duration of an action that started in the past and continues now (or has just finished with a visible result).

Formula: have/has + been + V-ing.

Meanings:
  • Process up to now: She has been studying for three hours. — She has been studying for three hours.
  • Visible result: You look tired — have you been running? — You look tired — have you been running?
  • Recently finished: It's been raining (the ground is wet).
Signals: for, since, all day/morning, how long, recently, lately.
Important: stative verbs (know, love, have=own) are not used in Continuous — use Present Perfect: I've known him for years.
int_u09.exc: Stative verbs are not used in Continuous — we use Present Perfect: I've known her for years (not I've been knowing).
mistakes: I am working here for 5 years. → I have been working here for 5 years. — with for/since Perfect is needed.
I've been knowing him since 2010. → I've known him since 2010. — know is stative.
How long you have been waiting? → How long have you been waiting? — inversion is needed.

int_u10.text: Unit 10 – Present Perfect vs Present Perfect Continuous
int_u10.exp:
Unit 10 — choosing between Present Perfect and Present Perfect Continuous: result vs. process.

Present Perfect (have+V3) — how much done / what result, action completed: I've read 50 pages. — I've read 50 pages.
Present Perfect Continuous (have been+V-ing) — how long the process has been going, emphasis on duration: I've been reading all morning. — I've been reading all morning.

Contrast examples:
  • She's painted the kitchen (done) vs. She's been painting the kitchen (process, may not be finished).
  • I've written three emails (quantity) vs. I've been writing emails (being busy with the process).
With verbs live, work, teach, study: both forms are often interchangeable without difference in meaning: I've lived / I've been living here for 5 years.
With quantity (how many, three times) — only Perfect.
int_u10.exc: With live, work, study, teach Perfect and Perfect Continuous are often equivalent: I've lived / I've been living here for 5 years.
mistakes: I've been reading three books this month. → I've read three books this month. — countable result — Perfect.
She's painted the wall all morning. → She's been painting the wall all morning. — process without completion.
How many times have you been visiting Rome? → How many times have you visited Rome? — number of times — Perfect.

int_u100.exp:
Unit 100 — formation and use of adverbs in -ly.

Basic rule: adjective + -ly → adverb: quick→quickly, careful→carefully. Spelling changes: y→ily (happy→happily), -le→-ly (simple→simply).
Exceptions (identical forms): fast, hard, early, late, high, low, straight — adjective = adverb.
  • She drives fast. It's a fast car.
  • He works hard. It's a hard job.
False friends:
  • hardly = barely (not 'hard');
  • lately = recently (not 'late');
  • highly = very (not 'high');
  • nearly = almost.
Good → well: She sings well, not good. But: I feel good/well — both are acceptable.
Adjectives in -ly (NOT adverbs!): friendly, lovely, lonely, silly, ugly. The adverb is expressed descriptively: in a friendly way.

The position of a manner adverb is usually after the verb/object.
int_u100.exc: Adjectives in -ly: friendly, lovely, lonely, silly — not adverbs! The adverb is formed descriptively: in a friendly way.
mistakes: She sings good. → She sings well. — well is an adverb to good.
He works hardly. → He works hard. — hardly = barely.
She spoke friendly. → spoke in a friendly way — friendly is an adjective, not an adverb.

int_u101.text: Unit 101 – Adjectives and adverbs: well, fast, late, hard
int_u101.note: It's a fast car. She drives fast.
int_u101.exp:
Unit 101 — pairs with identical and different forms of adjective and adverb.

Identical forms (adj. = adv.): fast, hard, late, early, high, low, near, deep, straight.
  • It's a fast train. The train goes fast.
  • She arrived late. It was a late arrival.
Pairs with different meanings:
  • hard (hard, diligently) vs. hardly (barely): I work hard. I can hardly hear you.
  • late (late) vs. lately (recently): She arrived late. I haven't seen him lately.
  • high (high) vs. highly (very): The bird flew high. She is highly qualified.
  • near (near) vs. nearly (almost): Come near. I nearly fell.
  • free (free of charge) vs. freely (freely): Children travel free. Speak freely.

Context decides the meaning: hardly has a negative tone, close to almost not.
mistakes: I haven't seen him late. → I haven't seen him lately. — 'recently'.
I can hard hear you. → I can hardly hear you. — 'barely' — hardly.
He arrived lately. (= late) → He arrived late. — 'late' — late.

int_u102.note: so cold / such cold weather / so many
int_u102.exp:
Unit 102 — intensifiers so and such.

So — before an adjective or adverb (without a noun):
  • It's so cold.
  • She speaks so quickly.
  • so much / so many: I have so much work.
Such — before (article and) a noun, possibly with an adjective:
  • such a + adj + sing. count.: such a cold day, such a nice person;
  • such + adj + pl./uncount.: such nice people, such good weather.
Result constructions:
  • so + adj/adv + that: It was so cold that we left.
  • such a + adj + noun + that: It was such a cold day that we left.
Comparison: It was so cold = It was such cold weather.

Remember: before a noun — such; before a 'bare' adjective — so.
int_u102.exc: Such a — with singular countables: such a cold day. Without a with plurals and uncountables: such good ideas, such good food.
mistakes: It was so a cold day. → It was such a cold day. — with a noun such.
Such cold (without a noun) → so cold — before an adjective alone — so.
Such a good weather → such good weather — weather is uncountable, without a.

int_u103.note: too tired to work / not warm enough
int_u103.exp:
Unit 103 — enough and too.

Enough:
  • after an adjective/adverb: warm enough, fast enough;
  • before a noun: enough money, enough time;
  • construction: adj/noun + enough + to-inf: She's old enough to drive. We have enough money to buy it.
Too:
  • before an adjective/adverb: too tired, too quickly;
  • construction: too + adj/adv + to-inf: He's too young to vote. It's too late to call.;
  • too + adj + for somebody: It's too difficult for me.
Contrast of meanings:
  • enough — positive sufficiency: everything is fine;
  • too — excess that prevents doing something: negative tone.
Very vs. too: very cold — very cold (a fact); too cold — too cold (preventing an action).

Don't confuse: too ≠ very; enough money, but old enough (different positions).
int_u103.exc: Enough after an adjective, but before a noun: warm enough, enough money.
mistakes: She is enough old to drive. → old enough — enough after an adjective.
I'm too much tired. → too tired — too without much before an adjective.
He is too young for vote. → too young to vote — the too + adj + to-inf construction.

int_u104.note: quite good / rather difficult / fairly easy
int_u104.exp:
Unit 104 — adverbs of moderate degree: quite, rather, pretty, fairly.

Quite (fairly) — neutrally-positive: quite good, quite nice. With some adjectives = completely: quite right, quite wrong, quite sure, quite impossible.
Rather — stronger than quite, often with a negative tone: rather difficult, rather cold. With positive adjectives means 'pleasantly surprised': It was rather good!
Pretty — colloquial = quite: pretty good, pretty difficult.
Fairly — weaker than quite, neutral: fairly easy, fairly good.
Position before a noun:
  • quite/rather stand BEFORE the article: quite a good film, rather a nice day;
  • fairly/pretty — after: a fairly good film, a pretty nice day.
Intensity scale: fairly < quite/pretty < rather < very.

Rather often implies a contrast with expectation.
int_u104.exc: Quite + some absolute adjectives = 'completely': quite right, quite impossible, quite sure.
mistakes: A quite good film → quite a good film — quite stands before the article.
Rather a good result or a rather good result — both variants occur, but a rather good result is better.
He's quite right? — in this sense quite = 'completely' and does not combine with the qualifier 'fairly'.

int_u105.exp:
Unit 105 — comparative degree of adjectives and adverbs.

One-syllable: +er: cheap→cheaper, old→older, fast→faster.
Two-syllable in -y: y→ier: happy→happier, easy→easier, busy→busier.
Long (2+ syllables): more +: more expensive, more difficult, more interesting.
Exceptions (irregular):
  • good → better;
  • bad → worse;
  • far → farther/further;
  • little → less;
  • much/many → more;
  • old → older / elder (only of relatives).
Doubling of the consonant (one syllable, cons.+vowel+cons.): big→bigger, hot→hotter, thin→thinner.
Older vs. elder: my elder brother (in the family), but in comparison — only older: He is older than me.

You cannot combine: more better → better.
int_u105.exc: Elder/eldest — only for family members (my elder brother). With others — older.
mistakes: More big → bigger — for short adjectives — -er.
More better → better — better is already the comparative form.
He is elder than me. → older than me — in comparison older is used.

int_u106.note: much better / a bit warmer / any better?
int_u106.exp:
Unit 106 — modification of the comparative degree.

Intensifiers (significantly more/less):
  • much, far, a lot, considerably + comparative: much better, far more expensive, a lot cheaper.
Softeners (a little):
  • a bit, a little, slightly: a bit warmer, slightly more difficult.
Any / no in questions and negatives:
  • Is it any better?
  • It's no better.
Progression: comparative and comparative = more and more: getting better and better, more and more expensive.
The … the … : The more you practise, the better you get. The sooner, the better.
Ban on very: very is NOT used with comparative: not very better → much better.

One form at a time: more cheaper → cheaper.
int_u106.exc: You cannot use very + comparative. Much, far, a lot, a bit, slightly are used.
mistakes: Very better → much better / far better — the comparative is strengthened by much.
It's a bit more cheaper. → a bit cheaper — only one comparative form.
Is it more better? → Is it any better? — in questions — any.

int_u107.note: as fast as / faster than / not as good as
int_u107.exp:
Unit 107 — all comparison constructions.

Equality: as + adj/adv + as:
  • She's as tall as her brother.
  • He runs as fast as me.
Inequality (softer than comparative): not as … as / not so … as:
  • It's not as cold as yesterday.
Comparative + than:
  • She runs faster than me/I.
  • This book is more interesting than that one.
After than/as: in speech — object case (than me, as her); in formal style — nominative (than I am, as she does).
Twice/three times as:
  • It's twice as expensive as before.
The same as: My car is the same colour as yours.

You cannot mix: not as tall than, not more cheap than.
int_u107.exc: After than/as in formal style — nominative case: taller than I (am). In speech — taller than me.
mistakes: She's taller as me. → taller than me — after the comparative — than.
He's as tall than her. → as tall as her — equality — as…as.
It's more cheap than the other. → cheaper than — for short ones — -er.

int_u108.note: the oldest / the most interesting / the best
int_u108.exp:
Unit 108 — superlative degree.

Forms:
  • short: +est: oldest, biggest, happiest;
  • long: the most +: the most beautiful;
  • irregular: best, worst, furthest, least, most, eldest.
Always with the: the oldest, the most interesting — the definite article is obligatory.
Prepositions after the superlative:
  • in + place/group: the tallest in the class, the best player in the team;
  • of + time/totality: the hottest day of the year, the best of all.
With Present Perfect — a characteristic pairing:
  • It's the best film I've ever seen.
  • She's the nicest person I've ever met.
Alternatives: by far the best, easily the best — even stronger.

You cannot say the most best — only the best.
int_u108.exc: After the superlative — in + place/group, not of: the tallest in the class, not of the class.
mistakes: She is most beautiful. → the most beautiful — with the superlative the is obligatory.
The best player of the team → the best player in the team — with a group — in.
It's the most best film. → the best film — one superlative form.

int_u109.text: Unit 109 - Word Order: Place and Time (Lesson 1)
int_u109.note: She works in London every day.
int_u109.exp:
Unit 109 — the order of adverbials at the end of a sentence.

General rule: verb → object → manner → place → time.
  • She sang the song beautifully at the concert last night.
  • He drove carefully to the airport yesterday.
Place before time:
  • correct: I went to the cinema last night.
  • incorrect: I went last night to the cinema.
Several place adverbials — from smaller to larger:
  • I live at 12 Oxford Street in London in England.
Several time adverbials — from specific to general:
  • She called at 5pm yesterday.
  • We'll meet at 7 on Monday next week.
Movement to the beginning for emphasis (stylistic):
  • Yesterday, I went to the cinema.

You cannot place an adverb between the verb and the object: She sang beautifully the song.
int_u109.exc: Time adverbs can move to the beginning for emphasis: Yesterday she went to the cinema.
mistakes: She went last night to the cinema. → She went to the cinema last night. — place before time.
I live in London at 12 Oxford Street. → at 12 Oxford Street in London — from smaller to larger.
He works every day in the office. → He works in the office every day. — time at the end.

int_u11.text: Unit 11 – How long have you been...?
int_u11.note: How long have you been waiting?
int_u11.exp:
Unit 11 — the question How long…? about the duration of a situation that started in the past and continues now.

Structures:
  • How long have you + V3/been V-ing?
  • Answer: for + period or since + moment.
Examples:
  • How long have you lived in Paris? — For ten years. — How long have you lived in Paris? — Ten years.
  • How long has she been waiting? — Since 8 o'clock. — Since when has she been waiting? — Since 8 o'clock.
  • How long have you known him? (stative — Perfect, not Continuous).
Important: Russian Present ('how long have you lived?') → English Perfect. How long are you…? — is a mistake. With stative verbs — only Perfect, not Perfect Continuous. For = period of time, since = starting point.
int_u11.exc: With stative verbs — Present Perfect: How long have you known her? (not been knowing).
mistakes: How long do you live here? → How long have you lived here? — situation from past up to now.
I'm here since Monday. → I've been here since Monday. — Perfect is needed.
How long are you waiting? → How long have you been waiting? — duration up to present.

int_u110.text: Unit 110 - Word Order: Adverbs of Verbs (Lesson 2)
int_u110.note: always, often, usually - place in a sentence
int_u110.exp:
Unit 110 — the position of adverbs with verbs.

Adverbs of frequency (always, usually, often, sometimes, rarely, never, hardly ever):
  • BEFORE the main verb: I always drink coffee in the morning.
  • AFTER be: She is always late.
  • AFTER the auxiliary: I have never been to Paris. You can always ask me.
Adverbs of certainty/comment (probably, definitely, certainly, obviously, clearly) — same position:
  • She will probably come.
  • He is definitely right.
Adverbs of manner (well, quickly, carefully) — usually at the end:
  • She speaks English well.
For emphasis at the beginning: Sometimes I feel sad. Often, she arrives early.
Not between the verb and direct object: She speaks well English.

Signal: with be — after; with others — before; with Perfect/Modal — between the auxiliary and main verb.
int_u110.exc: With be a frequency adverb goes AFTER: She is always late. With other verbs — BEFORE: She always arrives late.
mistakes: I always am happy. → I am always happy. — after to be.
She eats always breakfast. → She always eats breakfast. — before the main verb.
I have been never there. → I have never been there. — after the auxiliary.

int_u111.text: Unit 111 – Still, yet, already; any more / no longer
int_u111.note: I'm still waiting. He no longer works here.
int_u111.exp:
Unit 111 — adverbs of a continuing/completed situation.

Still — the situation continues contrary to expectation of ending:
  • I'm still waiting for him.
  • She still lives with her parents.
  • Position: before the main verb, after be: He is still here.
Yet — 'so far (not)/already' in questions and negatives, often at the end:
  • Have you finished yet?
  • I haven't seen him yet.
Already — 'already', in statements, usually between parts of the verb or at the end:
  • She has already left.
Any more / any longer / no longer — cessation:
  • He doesn't work here any more / any longer.
  • He no longer works here. (more formal, no longer before the main verb)

Contrast: still — continues; any more/no longer — stopped; yet — has not happened yet; already — earlier than expected.
int_u111.exc: Still — in statements before the main verb. Yet — at the end of questions/negatives.
mistakes: I don't know still. → I still don't know. — still before the verb (even in a negative).
Have you already finished? (neutral question) → Have you finished yet? — in a question usually yet.
He doesn't work here no longer. → He no longer works here. / doesn't work here any more.

int_u112.note: Even I knew. She can't even walk.
int_u112.exp:
Unit 112 — the focusing adverb even to emphasise unexpectedness.

Basic meaning: 'even' — emphasises that something goes beyond what is expected.
Position: before the word/phrase it emphasises (or before the main verb):
  • Even a child could solve it.
  • She can't even walk.
  • He works even on Sundays.
With comparative: even = even more:
  • It's even better than I expected.
  • She's even more talented than her sister.
Negative emphasis: not even:
  • He didn't even say sorry.
Even though / even if — concessive conjunctions (before a clause):
  • Even though it was cold, we went out.
  • I'll come even if it rains.

You cannot say: Even it rained → Even though it rained.
int_u112.exc: Even is not used at the beginning of a sentence without changing word order. The comparative is strengthened: even better, even more.
mistakes: Even it was cold, we went out. → Even though it was cold… — before a clause — even though.
It's more better. → It's even better. — to strengthen the comparative — even.
He not even said sorry. → He didn't even say sorry. — even after the auxiliary.

int_u113.text: Unit 113 – Although, though, even though, in spite of, despite
int_u113.note: Although she was tired, she worked.
int_u113.exp:
Unit 113 — ways of expressing concession (contrast, 'in spite of').

Conjunctions (before a subordinate clause):
  • although, though, even though — synonyms; even though is stronger;
  • Although it was cold, she went out.
  • Even though he was tired, he kept working.
  • though often at the end of a sentence as an adverb: It was cold. I went out, though.
Prepositions (before a noun, pronoun or -ing):
  • in spite of, despite — the same meaning;
  • Despite the rain, we continued.
  • In spite of being tired, she worked.
  • Despite the fact that it was cold, we went out. (the fact that adds a clause)
Key prohibitions:
  • despite of — without of;
  • after although — only a clause, not a noun.

Contrast: although + clause, despite/in spite of + noun/-ing.
int_u113.exc: Although/though — with a clause; despite/in spite of — with a noun or -ing. Not despite of.
mistakes: Despite of the rain → Despite the rain / In spite of the rain — despite without of.
Although the rain, we went out. → Although it was raining… — after although — a clause.
In spite she was tired, she worked. → In spite of being tired — in spite of + noun/-ing.

int_u114.note: Take an umbrella in case it rains.
int_u114.exp:
Unit 114 — in case (in case) — precautions taken in advance.

Meaning: in case means an action done in advance to prepare for a possible situation.
  • Take an umbrella in case it rains.
  • I'll write down the address in case I forget.
Difference from if:
  • I'll call you if I'm late. — I'll call only if I'm late (condition).
  • I'll call you in case I'm late. — I'll call in advance (just in case).
Grammar: after in case — Present Simple/Past Simple, NOT will/would, even about the future:
  • Take water in case you get thirsty.
  • I took a map in case we got lost.
Just in case — the colloquial phrase 'just in case': Take your phone just in case.

In case of + noun (formal, signs) — 'in case of something': In case of fire, break glass.
int_u114.exc: After in case — Present Simple, even about the future: I'll take an umbrella in case it rains, not will rain.
mistakes: Take money in case you will need it. → in case you need it — after in case — Present Simple.
Call me if I'm late vs. Call me in case I'm late: meaning is confused — if = condition, in case = in advance.
In case of he comes → In case he comes — without of before a clause.

int_u115.text: Unit 115 — Unless, as long as, provided, providing
int_u115.note: Unless you hurry, you'll be late.
int_u115.exp:
Unit 115 — conditional alternatives to if.

Unless = if not:
  • Unless you hurry, you'll be late. = If you don't hurry…
  • I won't go unless you come with me. = if you don't come.
Prohibition on double negation: you cannot say unless … not.
As long as / so long as — 'as long as/provided that':
  • You can borrow my car as long as you're careful.
  • I'll help so long as you tell me the truth.
Provided (that) / providing (that) — more formal, 'provided that':
  • You can stay provided that you pay rent.
  • Providing he apologises, I'll forgive him.
Grammar: after all these conjunctions — Present Simple (as in if-clauses), NOT will:
  • Unless it rains, we'll go out.
  • As long as you are ready, we can leave.

All these conjunctions introduce conditions, but with different nuances: unless — a negative condition, provided — a restricting requirement.
int_u115.exc: Unless = if not, so you cannot say unless … not: Unless you hurry, not unless you don't hurry.
mistakes: Unless you don't hurry → Unless you hurry — without double negation.
As long as you will be careful → as long as you are careful — after the conjunction Present Simple.
Provided that he will come → provided that he comes — present tense.

int_u116.note: As I was leaving, the phone rank.
int_u116.exp:
Unit 116 — the polysemous conjunction as.

Meaning 1: 'because' (= because, since):
  • As it was late, I went home.
  • As she knew French, she translated.
Meaning 2: 'when/while' — simultaneous ongoing actions:
  • As I was walking home, I saw an accident.
  • She sang as she cooked.
Just as — 'at the very moment when':
  • Just as I was leaving, the phone rang.
Choice between as / when / while:
  • as — simultaneous ongoing actions or gradual development;
  • when — for brief, instantaneous events: When I opened the door, I saw him.;
  • while — emphasises duration: While I was cooking, he called.

Other meanings of as: role (work as a teacher), comparison (as tall as) — discussed in other units.
int_u116.exc: As meaning 'while' is used with ongoing actions (often Past Continuous). For brief ones — when.
mistakes: As I opened the door, I dropped the keys. (instantaneous) → When I opened the door… — for brief ones — when.
As that it was late, I went home. → As it was late… — without that.
Because as it rained → As it rained / Because it rained — no need to duplicate the cause.

int_u117.text: Unit 117 – Like and as: such as
int_u117.note: She looks like her mother. She works as a teacher.
int_u117.exp:
Unit 117 — like vs. as in comparison and indicating a role.

Like (preposition) + noun/pronoun/-ing — 'similar to, like':
  • She sings like a bird.
  • He's like his father.
  • It tastes like chicken.
As (conjunction) + clause — 'as, in the way that':
  • Do as I say, not as I do.
  • She works hard, as her mother did.
As (preposition) + noun — actual role or function:
  • She works as a teacher.
  • He used the book as a pillow.
Key contrast:
  • As a teacher, I understand students. — I am a teacher.
  • Like a teacher, he spoke clearly. — Similar to a teacher, but not a teacher.

Colloquial: like is sometimes used as a conjunction (Do like I told you), but this is informal; in written speech — as.
int_u117.exc: As denotes a real role: She works as a teacher (she is a teacher). Like a teacher = similar to a teacher, but is not one.
mistakes: She works like a nurse. (she actually is a nurse) → works as a nurse — actual role — as.
Do like I say. → Do as I say. — before a clause — as.
He's like his father's. → He's like his father. — after like the noun is in the common case.

int_u118.text: Unit 118 – Like / as if / as though: as if
int_u118.note: He looks as if he's seen a ghost.
int_u118.exp:
Unit 118 — impressions and comparisons: like, as if, as though.

As if / as though — conjunctions 'as if' (equivalent in meaning, interchangeable):
  • He looks as if he's seen a ghost.
  • She acts as though she owns the place.
Like — a colloquial variant, often in American English: It looks like it's going to rain. In formal speech — as if.
Real situations (probably true) — present/Perfect:
  • It looks as if it's raining.
  • She looks as if she has seen something scary.
Unreal / imagined — past (as in Subjunctive):
  • He talks as if he knew everything. — But he doesn't know.
  • She walks as if she were a queen. (formally were, colloquially was)

Difference: as if he is rich (possibly true) vs. as if he were rich (unreal, actually not).
int_u118.exc: After as if/as though in an unreal situation — Past (as in Subjunctive): He acts as if he knew — but he doesn't know.
mistakes: He looks as if he was a ghost. (real appearance) → as if he has seen a ghost — real situation — present/Perfect.
She speaks as if she know everything. → as if she knew everything — unreal — Past.
Like he's tired (formally) → as if he's tired — in written speech — as if/as though.

int_u119.note: for three days / during the film / while I was there
int_u119.exp:
Unit 119 — for, during, while for denoting time.

For + period of time — how long the action lasted:
  • I waited for two hours.
  • She's lived here for five years.
During + noun — when (in what period) the action took place:
  • I fell asleep during the film.
  • We met during the conference.
While + clause — conjunction 'while':
  • I fell asleep while I was watching the film.
  • She called while I was cooking.
Key differences:
  • for answers 'how long?': for 2 hours;
  • during answers 'when?' + noun: during lunch;
  • while — same meaning as during, but before a clause.

You cannot say: during three days (duration) → for three days; while the film (before a noun) → during the film.
int_u119.exc: During denotes 'when' (in what period), for — 'how long'. They are easy to confuse: for 2 hours vs. during the meeting.
mistakes: I was there during three days. → for three days — duration — for.
During I was watching TV → While I was watching TV — before a clause — while.
While the film → during the film — before a noun — during.

int_u12.text: Unit 12 – Present Perfect with since and for
int_u12.note: I've known her for years. Since Monday.
int_u12.exp:
Unit 12 — prepositions for and since with Present Perfect and Perfect Continuous.

for + duration period: for two hours, for a week, for ages, for a long time.
since + starting moment: since Monday, since 2010, since breakfast, since we met. After since a whole clause may follow: since I was a child.

Examples:
  • We've been friends for 20 years. — We've been friends for 20 years.
  • I haven't seen her since June. — I haven't seen her since June.
  • He has worked here since he graduated. — He has worked here since he graduated.
ago vs. since: ago — only with Past Simple, counted from now: two years ago. Cannot say since two years — need for two years.
In informal speech for can be omitted: I've been here (for) ten years.
int_u12.exc:
Ago is used only with Past Simple: two years ago (not since two years ago).
For can be omitted: I've lived here (for) ten years.
mistakes: I've known her since 10 years. → I've known her for 10 years. — period — for.
I've worked here for 2010. → I've worked here since 2010. — moment — since.
She lives here since March. → She has lived here since March. — with since — Perfect.

int_u120.text: Unit 120 - By, until, by the time
int_u120.note: I'll finish by Friday. By the time she arrived…
int_u120.exp:
Unit 120 — by, until, by the time for denoting an end point.

Until (till) — a continuous action up to a moment:
  • I waited until 9 pm.
  • She slept until noon.
  • We can't leave until he arrives.
By — 'no later than'; the action will be completed by this deadline (not necessarily ongoing):
  • I'll finish the report by Friday.
  • Be home by 10.
By the time + clause — 'by the time when':
  • By the time she arrived, I had already left. — Past Perfect in the main clause.
  • By the time you read this, I'll be in Paris. — Future in the main.
Contrast until vs. by:
  • I'll wait until 9. (continuous waiting)
  • I'll call you by 9. (a call by that moment)

You cannot say: I'll finish until Friday → by Friday; I'll wait by 9 → until 9.
int_u120.exc: By the time + Past Simple in the clause, and in the main — Past Perfect: By the time she arrived, I had left.
mistakes: I'll wait by 9pm. (continuous waiting) → I'll wait until 9pm. — continuous — until.
I'll finish until Friday. → I'll finish by Friday. — deadline — by.
By the time she arrived, I left. → I had already left — Past Perfect is needed.

int_u121.text: Unit 121 – Prepositions of time: at, on, in
int_u121.note: at 5 / on Monday / in July / in 2020
int_u121.exp:
Unit 121 — prepositions of time at / on / in: systematic overview.

At — point in time: clock times (at 7.30), meals (at lunch), holiday periods (at Easter), at night, at the weekend (BrE). On — days and dates: on Friday, on 3 March, on Christmas Day, on my birthday. On Friday morning — when a day is specified, on wins. In — larger periods: months, years, centuries, seasons (in May, in 1999, in summer, in the 20th century), and parts of the day: in the morning/afternoon/evening.

Examples: I'll see you on Monday evening. She was born in 1990. The lesson starts at 9.

No preposition before this, last, next, every, tomorrow, yesterday: See you next week. Compare pairs: in time (in advance) ↔ on time (punctual) — see Unit 122.
int_u121.exc:
No preposition: this/last/next/every — see you next Monday, I saw her last week.

at night, but in the night (a specific night).

at Christmas (period), on Christmas Day (the day).
mistakes: in Monday → on Monday — days of the week take on.
on the morning → in the morning — parts of the day take in.
at next week → next week — next/last/this take no preposition.
in 9 o'clock → at 9 o'clock — exact time takes at.

int_u122.text: Unit 122 - On time and in time; at the end and in the end
int_u122.note: on time (on time) / in time (in time)
int_u122.exp:
Unit 122 — two pairs of idiomatic expressions that are often confused.

On time — exactly on schedule, punctual: The train left on time. In time (for sth / to do sth) — early enough to make it: We got to the airport just in time to catch our flight.

At the end (of sth) — at the end of a specific object/period: at the end of the film, at the end of May. In the end = finally, eventually: We couldn't decide where to go. In the end we stayed home.

Mnemonic: on time = precision (a dash on a timetable), in time = a buffer (inside a window of opportunity). At the end = a point on a timeline, in the end = the outcome/resolution.
int_u122.exc:
in the end = finally; at the end = at the end of something specific.

on time has no comparative form — more on time is impossible.
mistakes: in time for the meeting (= punctual) → on time — "punctual" = on time.
at the end, I agreed → in the end, I agreed — "eventually" = in the end.
in the end of the film → at the end of the film — end of something specific — at.

int_u123.text: Unit 123 – Prepositions of place: in, at, on (lesson 1)
int_u123.note: in the room / at the bus stop / on the table
int_u123.exp:
Unit 123 — in / at / on as prepositions of place, basic meanings.

In — inside a three-dimensional/enclosed space: in the kitchen, in a car, in Paris, in the water, in the sky. At — at a point, a place seen as a node: at the door, at the bus stop, at the traffic lights, at the top/bottom. On — on a surface or line: on the wall, on the floor, on the ceiling, on the coast, on page 10.

Examples: There's somebody at the door. The picture is on the wall. She lives in a small village.

Subtle differences: in the corner of the room (inside the room, internal corner) ↔ at the corner of the street (external street corner). in the front row ↔ at the front of the building (by the facade). The preposition chosen defines the perception of space — volumetric, point-like, or flat.
int_u123.exc:
in bed / in hospital / in prison — no article when referring to function.

at the door (by the door), but on the door (a sign on the door).
mistakes: on the car → in the car — inside a car — in.
in the bus stop → at the bus stop — point/meeting place — at.
at the picture → in the picture — inside an image — in.

int_u124.text: Unit 124 – Prepositions of place: in, at, on (lesson 2)
int_u124.note: in hospital / at home / on TV / on the bus
int_u124.exp:
Unit 124 — set expressions with prepositions of place, second round.

In + large territories and "institutions without article": in bed, in hospital (BrE), in prison, in the country, in the mountains, in the world, in the middle. At + typical places/events: at home, at work, at school, at university, at the station/airport, at a concert, at a party, at Sue's (house). On: on the ground floor, on the second floor, on the way, on a farm, on an island, on the left/right, on the coast, on the river.

Examples: He's been in hospital since Monday. I met him at a party. We live on the fifth floor.

BrE vs AmE: in hospital (BrE, of a patient) ↔ in the hospital (AmE). Media: on TV, on the radio, but in the newspaper. These collocations are best learned as blocks.
int_u124.exc:
BrE: in hospital; AmE: in the hospital.

on TV / on the radio, but in the newspaper.

at home / at work / at school without article.
mistakes: in home → at home — fixed expression.
in TV → on TV — a programme is on TV.
on the bus as passenger ok, but in the bus is rarer — on the bus for public transport.

int_u125.text: Unit 125 – Prepositions of place: in, at, on (lesson 3)
int_u125.note: in the front / at the front / on the front
int_u125.exp:
Unit 125 — third round: in / at / on before the words front, back, top, bottom, side, end, middle, beginning.

The meaning is set by the "place + preposition" pair: in the front/back — inside the object (in the front of the car — in the front part of the interior); at the front/back — outside by the facade/rear (at the back of the queue); on the front/back — on the surface (on the back of the envelope).

Examples: I sat at the back of the class. Write your name on the back of the photo. The driver sits in the front (inside the car).

At the top/bottom of — at the top/bottom (at the top of the hill). On top of — on top, above (on top of the box). At the end of — at the end (of a spatial/temporal object). These pairs require understanding physical geometry.
int_u125.exc: in the front/back (of a car/room) — inside; at the front/back (of a building/queue) — at the front part; on the front/back (of a page) — on the surface.
mistakes: on the front of the class → at the front of the class — at the front — at.
at the front of the envelope → on the front of the envelope — on the surface — on.
at the back of the car → in the back of the car — inside a car — in.

int_u126.text: Unit 126 – Prepositions of direction: to, at, in, into
int_u126.note: go to / arrive at / look at / come in
int_u126.exp:
Unit 126 — prepositions of direction and motion: to, into, at, in, on, onto.

To specifies the goal of motion: go to work, drive to London, return to the office. Into — motion into an enclosed space: walk into the room, get into the car, pour milk into the cup. At after verbs of directed action: look at, point at, throw at, shout at, laugh at, aim at — target as a bullseye.

Difference in vs into: She was swimming in the lake (where, process) ↔ She jumped into the lake (where to, motion inward).

Examples: I'm going to bed. He ran into the house. Don't throw stones at the dog.

No preposition: go/come/get home. Arrive: arrive at + building/event, arrive in + city/country. Enter — transitive, without into: enter the building.
int_u126.exc:
No preposition: go home, get home.

arrive at (a place), but arrive in (a city/country).
mistakes: go to home → go home — home without to.
arrive to London → arrive in London — arrive in + city.
enter into the room → enter the room — enter without preposition.

int_u127.text: Unit 127 – On, in, at in different situations
int_u127.note: on a bus / in a taxi / at a party
int_u127.exp:
Unit 127 — choosing in, on, at in situations where rules overlap.

Transport: on a bus/train/plane/ship/bike (large, one "boards" them — treated as a platform), but in a car/taxi (you sit inside the cabin). Events: at a party/meeting/concert/wedding/match/conference — a gathering point for people. Island/coastal locations: in Sicily (a large island/territory), on a small island (a point on the map). Addresses: at 25 Baker Street (number), on/in Baker Street (street), in London (city).

Examples: I saw her on the train. There were 200 people at the concert. He grew up on a farm.

Rule within the layer: medium/public transport ⇒ on; intimate ⇒ in. Event ⇒ at. These choices are learned by analogy rather than by a logical rule.
int_u127.exc:
on a bus/train/plane/ship/bike, but in a car/taxi.

at a party / at a concert / at a meeting.
mistakes: in the bus → on the bus — public transport — on.
on a taxi → in a taxi — taxi — in.
in the party → at the party — event — at.

int_u128.note: by car / by chance / by the time / by himself
int_u128.exp:
Unit 128 — the multifunctional preposition by.

Meanings: (1) means of transport without article: by car, by bus, by train, by plane, by sea, by air; but on foot. (2) Agent of the passive: Hamlet was written by Shakespeare. (3) Means/instrument of action: pay by credit card, contact me by email, by mistake, by chance, by accident. (4) Location next to: Sit by me. (5) Deadline: I'll be back by 10 — no later than 10. (6) Alone: by myself/yourself = alone.

Examples: I'll send the file by email. She lives by the sea. Finish the project by Friday.

By vs until: by = at or before a moment (one-off completion), until = up until (duration): Wait until 6 ↔ Finish by 6. With vs by: instrument (cut with a knife) vs means/agent (killed by a soldier).
int_u128.exc:
No article: by car/bus/train/plane, but on foot.

by myself = alone; by himself (reflexive with by).
mistakes: by a car → by car — means of transport without article.
by foot → on foot — on foot = on foot.
until the time → by the time — "by the time" = by the time.

int_u129.note: a reason for / a lack of / difficulty in
int_u129.exp:
Unit 129 — noun + preposition: set collocations.

For: a reason for, a need for, a demand for, a request for. Of: a cause of, a photo of, an example of, an advantage of, a lack of. In: an increase/rise/fall/drop/decrease in, a change in, difficulty in, interest in, belief in. To: damage to, an answer to, a solution to, a reply to, an attitude to/towards, invitation to. With: a relationship/connection with, contact with. Between: difference between.

Examples: There's no reason for worry. The cause of the fire is unknown. There's been a rise in prices. What's the solution to this problem?

Tricky pairs: cause of ↔ reason for (both = cause). increase in (not of) when describing the parameter that grows. After a preposition comes a gerund: a reason for leaving early.
int_u129.exc:
cause of, but reason for.

an increase/rise/fall in (not of).

damage to, solution to.
mistakes: reason of → reason for — reason for something — for.
cause for → cause of — cause — of.
solution of → solution to — solution — to.
increase of prices → increase in prices — increase — in.

int_u13.text: Unit 13 – Present Perfect vs Past Simple (Part 1)
int_u13.note: Have you seen it? / Did you see it yesterday?
int_u13.exp:
Unit 13 — main contrast Present Perfect vs. Past Simple (part 1).

Present Perfect — connection with the present, without specifying the exact time: I've finished the report (it is ready now).
Past Simple — completed action at a specific past moment: I finished it at 3 p.m.

Decisive markers:
  • Perfect: ever, never, just, already, yet, so far, recently, this week (current period).
  • Past Simple: yesterday, ago, last week/month, in 2010, when.
Examples:
  • Have you seen the news? — Have you seen the news? vs. Did you see the news this morning? — period is closed.
  • I've been to Rome (experience) vs. I went to Rome in May (fact).
When-questions — always Past Simple: When did you arrive?
int_u13.exc:
In American English Past Simple often replaces Present Perfect: Did you eat? = Have you eaten?
With when-questions — only Past Simple: When did you arrive?
mistakes: When have you arrived? → When did you arrive? — with when about the past — Past Simple.
I have lost my keys yesterday. → I lost my keys yesterday. — yesterday → Past Simple.
Did you ever visit Japan? → Have you ever visited Japan? — lifetime experience — Perfect.

int_u130.note: good at / afraid of / different from
int_u130.exp:
Unit 130 — adjective + preposition, part 1.

Of (emotions and characteristics): afraid of, frightened of, scared of, terrified of; ashamed of, aware of, conscious of; capable of, fond of, full of, jealous of, proud of, short of, tired of, typical of. At (abilities/reactions): good/bad/brilliant/hopeless/clever/terrible at; surprised/amazed/shocked at/by. From: different from, absent from, safe from. For: famous for, responsible for, ready for, suitable for, sorry for.

Examples: She's afraid of spiders. He's very good at chess. This region is famous for its wines.

Nuances: sorry for sb (to pity) ↔ sorry about sth (to apologise for). good at + noun/-ing, but good to sb = kind to someone, good for sb/sth = beneficial. different from — neutral variant; to/than — conversational.
int_u130.exc:
different from (BrE preferred), different to/than also occur.

good at + noun/-ing, but good to sb (kind to).
mistakes: afraid from → afraid of — of.
good in math → good at math — at.
different of → different from — from.
famous of → famous for — for.

int_u131.note: interested in / keen on / satisfied with
int_u131.exp:
Unit 131 — adjective + preposition, part 2.

With: angry/annoyed/furious/pleased/happy/satisfied/bored/delighted/disappointed with. About: angry/annoyed/worried/upset/excited/happy/nervous about. In: interested in, involved in, successful in, experienced in, rich in, weak in. On: keen on, dependent on, based on. To: married to, engaged to, similar to, close to, related to, kind/nice/rude/polite to, opposed to, addicted to. By: surprised/shocked/impressed/amazed/astonished by/at.

Examples: I'm not interested in politics. She's married to a doctor. He was very kind to me.

Contrasts: angry with sb (at a person) ↔ angry about sth (about a situation). tired of = fed up with ↔ tired from = tired from (effort). interested in (long-standing interest) ↔ interesting to sb (interesting to someone).
int_u131.exc:
interested in (ongoing interest), but interesting to sb.

tired of (fed up) vs. tired from (worn out by).
mistakes: interested on music → interested in music — in.
satisfied of → satisfied with — with.
keen in → keen on — on.
proud for → proud of — of.

int_u132.text: Unit 132 – Prepositions at and to after verbs
int_u132.note: look at / look to / aim at / throw at
int_u132.exp:
Unit 132 — verb + at vs + to: directed action vs communication.

At — directing a gaze/aggression/target: look at, stare at, glance at, glare at, point at, laugh at, smile at, wave at, shout at, throw at, aim at, shoot at, fire at. To — transferring information/addressing: speak to, talk to, listen to, say sth to, explain to, describe to, mention to, suggest to, apologise to, write to, reply to.

Examples: Don't shout at me! (aggression). She shouted to me across the street (so I could hear). He threw the ball to me (to me, to catch) ↔ threw a stone at the window (at the window, to hit it).

Pairs with meaning shift: shout at = scold, shout to = call loudly. throw at (intending to hit) vs throw to (to pass). laugh at (to mock) — always at. smile at — the smile's recipient. After listen, to is required: listen to music.
int_u132.exc:
shout at (aggressively) vs. shout to (so as to be heard).

throw at (intending to hit) vs. throw to (to pass).
mistakes: look to the picture → look at the picture — at.
point to me angrily → point at me — at when directing at someone.
laugh of him → laugh at him — at.

int_u133.text: Unit 133 – Prepositions about, for, of, after
int_u133.note: worry about / wait for / dream of / look after
int_u133.exp:
Unit 133 — verbs with about, for, of, after.

About (topic of thought/talk): talk/speak/read/write/hear/know/think/dream/worry/complain/argue about. For (goal/expectation): ask for, apply for, wait for, search for, look for, care for, pay for, hope for, long for, vote for. Of: consist of, smell of, taste of, think of, hear of, dream of, die of. After: look after (take care of), take after (resemble a relative), ask after (inquire about someone's health), run after (chase).

Examples: I'm looking for my keys. Who looks after the children? She takes after her mother. The soup smells of garlic.

The three looks: look at (direct gaze at), look for (search), look after (take care). Confusing these three is a typical error point. wait for — the preposition is required, even with a pronoun: wait for me.
int_u133.exc:
wait for sb, but wait on = serve.

look after (take care of) ≠ look for (search) ≠ look at (gaze at).
mistakes: worry for → worry about — about.
wait sb → wait for sb — for is required.
dream for → dream of/about — of/about.
look for the kids (= take care) → look after the kids — take care — after.

int_u134.text: Unit 134 – Prepositions about and of: hear, think, dream
int_u134.note: hear about / think about / remind of
int_u134.exp:
Unit 134 — verbs that change meaning with about vs of.

Think about (consider, focus mentally) ↔ think of (opinion; come to mind; intend): I'm thinking about what to do; What do you think of my idea? I can't think of the name. Hear about (hear news/information) ↔ hear of (know of the existence of): Have you heard of Murphy's book? Dream about (see in a dream) ↔ dream of (to dream of/imagine).

Remind: two patterns. Remind sb of sb/sth = bring to mind (be similar): She reminds me of my sister. Remind sb about/to do sth = remind (prompt): Remind me to call him.

Complain: complain to sb about sth — to whom and about what. Warn: warn sb about/of sth — both forms occur. Preposition choice often distinguishes "topic" (about) and "content/nature" (of).
int_u134.exc:
think of (opinion / recall) vs. think about (consider).

remind of (reminds of someone) vs. remind about (remind of a matter).
mistakes: hear from news → hear about the news — about.
dream for you → dream of/about you — of/about.
remind me to her → remind me of her — similarity — of.

int_u135.text: Unit 135 – Prepositions of, for, from, on
int_u135.note: approve of / suffer from / depend on
int_u135.exp:
Unit 135 — verbs + of, for, from, on.

Of: accuse sb of, approve of, consist of, dispose of, remind sb of, suspect sb of. For: apologise for, blame sb for, forgive sb for, pay for, punish sb for, thank sb for, charge sb for, pray for, long for.
