From: borrow from, suffer from, recover from, protect/save/prevent/stop sb from, differ from, escape from, translate from. On: depend on, rely on, count on, concentrate on, insist on, spend (money) on, live on, congratulate sb on.

Examples: I depend on you. She apologised for being late. He suffers from asthma. They accused him of stealing.

"Prevention" pattern: prevent/stop/protect/save sb from doing sth — from something. Spend on + object/cause, spend (time) -ing without preposition. It depends as a standalone reply — no on, but it depends on you — with preposition.
int_u135.exc:
depend on, but it depends (no on) in a short reply.

suffer from (an illness) vs. suffer (because) of (a cause).
mistakes: approve for → approve of — of.
suffer of flu → suffer from flu — from.
depend of → depend on — on.
consist from → consist of — of.

int_u136.text: Unit 136 – Prepositions in, into, with, to, on
int_u136.note: believe in / crash into / succeed in
int_u136.exp:
Unit 136 — completing the series: verbs + in, into, with, to.

In: believe in, specialise in, succeed in, result in, invest in. Into (motion/transformation): crash/bump/run into, divide into, translate into, break into (a house). With: agree/disagree with, argue with, discuss sth with sb, collide with, deal with, provide sb with, share sth with sb, compare X with Y. To: belong to, happen to, listen to, refer to, react to, object to, prefer X to Y.

Examples: Do you believe in ghosts? The car crashed into a tree. I prefer tea to coffee. This book belongs to Tom.

Don't confuse: believe sb (believe a person) ≠ believe in sb/sth (believe in the existence/worth of). happen to sb — happens to, not with. After discuss there is no about: discuss the plan, but talk about the plan. Prepositions shape the verb's meaning — a shift in preposition shifts the meaning.
int_u136.exc:
succeed in + -ing, but success in (noun) also with in.

happen to sb (happen to), but happen with is rarer.
mistakes: believe on God → believe in God — in.
crash in the tree → crash into the tree — into.
succeed on → succeed in — in.

int_u137.note: get up / turn on / look after - principles
int_u137.exp:
Unit 137 — phrasal verbs: general structure.

Form: verb + particle (up, down, in, out, on, off, away, back). Three classes:
  • Intransitive — no object: My car broke down. She grew up in Leeds.
  • Transitive separable — a noun object goes before or after the particle; a pronoun only in between: Turn on the radio = Turn the radio on; Turn it on (NOT turn on it).
  • Transitive inseparable (prepositional verbs) — the object always follows the particle: look after the children = look after them (NOT look them after).
Meaning is often idiomatic: give up = abandon, not "give upward".

Examples: Please put your phone away. I ran into an old friend.

Heuristic: simple motion particles are more often separable (put on, take off), combinations with a "full" preposition are more often inseparable (look after, get over, run into). Dictionary labels (sep/insep) are a reliable source.
int_u137.exc:
Some phrasal verbs are separable: turn it on (not turn on it if it is a pronoun).

Inseparable: look after him (not look him after).
mistakes: turn on it → turn it on — pronoun between verb and particle.
look him after → look after him — look after is inseparable.
get on it (the bus) → get on it ok, but get it on changes meaning.

int_u138.text: Unit 138 – Phrasal verbs with in and out
int_u138.note: fill in / give out / find out / break in
int_u138.exp:
Unit 138 — phrasal verbs with in and out.

In: get in (enter/arrive), come in (enter), drop in (drop by), join in (join an activity), fill in (fill out a form), break in (break in), check in (register at a hotel/airport). Out: find out (discover), work out (figure out/turn out/exercise), figure out (work out), sort out (sort through a mess), point out (indicate), try out (try), run out (of) (run out), eat out (eat outside the home), go out (go out/go out of use).

Examples: I've just found out she's moving. Please fill in this form = fill this form in = fill it in. We've run out of milk.

Semantic logic: out often = outward + completion/fullness (sell out, tire out, burn out). In = inward/filling (fill in, hand in). Break in (intrude) ≠ break into + object (break into a building).
int_u138.exc:
find out (discover) with a pronoun object: find it out is rare — usually find out the truth.

break in = break in; break into + object.
mistakes: fill the form in (OK) / fill in it → fill it in — pronoun inside.
break in the house → break into the house — with an object — into.
find out it → find it out.

int_u139.note: carry out / sort out / turn out / wear out
int_u139.exp:
Unit 139 — continued: phrasal verbs with out.

Meaning "to completion/fully": carry out (perform), sort out (resolve), work out (calculate/turn out), cross out (cross out), leave out (omit), cut out (cut out/stop), check out (check out of a hotel). Meaning "to appear": turn out (turn out to be), come out (come out — of a book/the sun), break out (break out — of war/fire), stand out (stand out), make out (make out a faint signal).

Examples: The plan didn't work out. It turned out (that) he was right. His new novel came out last week. We must carry out the order.

Constructions: turn out to be + adj/noun or turn out that + clause. run out is intransitive (time ran out), but with an object it needs a preposition: run out of time. check out (check out) ≠ check in (check in).
int_u139.exc:
turn out (to turn out) is followed by to be / that: It turned out to be wrong.

run out of (not just run out, if there is an object): We ran out of milk.
mistakes: run out milk → run out of milk — of.
carry on the plan out → carry out the plan — wrong split.
turn out that to be → turn out to be.

int_u14.text: Unit 14 – Present Perfect vs Past Simple (Part 2)
int_u14.note: It's the first time I've done this.
int_u14.exp:
Unit 14 — subtle cases of Present Perfect vs. Past Simple (part 2).

Present Perfect — required:
  • After It's the first/second/best/worst time…: It's the first time I've eaten sushi. — This is my first time eating sushi.
  • After This is the best… I've ever…: This is the best book I've ever read.
  • When the period is not yet over: I've drunk three coffees today (the day is not over).
Period still open vs. closed:
  • I haven't seen her this morning (morning is still going) vs. I didn't see her this morning (morning is already over).
News vs. details: headline — Perfect, details — Past Simple: A plane has crashed. It took off at 6 a.m. and…
Key: is the situation relevant now or is it a closed past fact?
int_u14.exc: After It's the first/second/best time… — Present Perfect: It's the best book I've ever read.
mistakes: It's the first time I see this. → It's the first time I've seen this. — after It's the first time — Perfect.
This is the best coffee I ever drank. → This is the best coffee I've ever drunk. — Perfect is needed.
Have you eaten? — Yes, I have eaten an hour ago. → Yes, I ate an hour ago. — ago — Past Simple.

int_u140.text: Unit 140 – Phrasal verbs with on and off (lesson 1)
int_u140.note: put on / take off / set off / call off
int_u140.exp:
Unit 140 — phrasal verbs with on and off, first round.

On (switching on/continuation/putting on): turn/switch on (turn on), put on (put on/gain weight), go on (continue/happen), carry on (continue), hold on (wait), get on (with) (continue/get along). Off (switching off/separation/leaving): turn/switch off, take off (take off; take off — of a plane), set off (set out), put off (postpone; repel), call off (cancel), see sb off (see off).

Examples: The meeting has been called off. What's going on here? We set off at dawn. Put off the decision until Monday.

Particle semantics: on = contact/continuation, off = break/removal. put off has two meanings — postpone an event and emotionally repel. take off: clothing (remove) and a plane (take off) — one metaphor of separation.
int_u140.exc:
put on clothes = put on; take off clothes = take off.

call off = cancel (= cancel); set off = set out.
mistakes: put on it → put it on — pronoun inside.
cancel off → call off / cancel — don't combine.
set off to the trip → set off on the trip — on.

int_u141.text: Unit 141 – Phrasal verbs with on and off (lesson 2)
int_u141.note: fall off / get on / switch off / run off
int_u141.exp:
Unit 141 — on/off, second round.

Get on — to board a bus/train/plane; get off — to get off. For a car/taxi: get in/into, get out of. Get on with sb — to get along; get on with sth — to continue doing. Fall off — to fall off; falling sales: Sales have fallen off. Go off has several meanings: to go off (The alarm went off), to go bad (The milk has gone off), to leave (She went off to Paris), to go off (I've gone off coffee). Show off — to show off. Pay off — to pay off / settle a debt. Doze off, nod off — to doze off.

Examples: My hard work paid off. He's always showing off his new car. I get on well with my neighbours.

Subtlety: switch off ≈ turn off for electrical devices — interchangeable. get on and get off in one bundle with public transport — a mnemonic pair.
int_u141.exc:
get on (bus/train), but get in (car/taxi).

switch off / turn off — synonyms for electrical devices.
mistakes: get in the bus → get on the bus — on.
get on a taxi → get in a taxi — in.
switch off it → switch it off.

int_u142.text: Unit 142 – Phrasal verbs with up and down
int_u142.note: grow up / break down / slow down / set up
int_u142.exp:
Unit 142 — phrasal verbs with up and down: metaphors of height.

Up usually = growth/intensification/completion: grow up (of a person), speed up (accelerate), save up (save money), clean up (tidy thoroughly), wash up (do the dishes BrE), set up (establish/set up), cheer up (cheer up), eat up (finish eating), use up (use completely). Down = reduction/stopping: break down (break down/collapse emotionally), slow down (slow down), calm down (calm down), turn down (turn down/reject), cut down (on) (cut down on), let sb down (let down), write down (write down).

Examples: He's trying to cut down on sugar. Don't let me down. Our car broke down on the motorway.

Contrasting pairs: break down (a machine/person breaks down) ≠ break up (a relationship ends; school breaks up for holidays). turn up (turn up the volume/appear) ↔ turn down (turn down/reject).
int_u142.exc:
break down (the car broke down) ≠ break up (the relationship ended).

grow up (a person grows up) ≠ grow (a plant grows).
mistakes: I grow up in Moscow (now) → I grew up in Moscow — usually in past simple.
slow → slow down — for urging to slow — down.
set up on business → set up a business — no on.

int_u143.note: pick up / give up / make up / keep up
int_u143.exp:
Unit 143 — phrasal verbs with up, continued: the key layer.

Pick up — to pick up; to collect someone; to pick up (an illness/language); to pick up (of wind). Give up — to give up (+ -ing/noun): give up smoking. Make up — to make up a story; to make up (with sb); to make up (a whole); to put on makeup. Keep up (with) — to keep up with. End up — to end up (+ -ing/adjective/place): We ended up staying home. Put up (with) — to put up with, to tolerate. Take up — to take up a hobby; to take up space/time. Use up — to use up entirely.

Examples: I can't put up with this noise. She took up painting last year. Can you pick me up at the station?

Combinability: give up takes a gerund, not an infinitive. put up with — three-part (inseparable): I can't put up with it, NOT put up it with. make up is polysemous — context decides.
int_u143.exc:
make up has several meanings: invent (a story), reconcile (with sb), constitute.

give up + -ing / noun, not to-infinitive.
mistakes: give up to smoke → give up smoking — -ing form.
pick up it → pick it up.
keep up from me → keep up with me — with.

int_u144.note: turn up / show up / come up with / look up
int_u144.exp:
Unit 144 — more up: appearance, disclosure, creation.

Turn up / show up — to show up, to arrive (often unexpectedly or late). Come up — to come up (a topic, an issue); to approach. Come up with — to come up with (an idea, a solution). Look up — to look up in a dictionary/reference (separable: look it up). Set up — to found, to establish, to organise. Bring up — to raise a child; to raise a topic. Split up — to split up. Wake up — to wake up/wake someone up. Blow up — to explode/blow up; to inflate. Do up — to fasten; to do up (a house).

Examples: She didn't turn up to the party. I came up with a new idea. Can you look up the word in the dictionary?

Distinguish: come up (to arise by itself) ≠ come up with (to deliberately produce). bring up vs raise as synonyms for "raise a child"; bring up also means "to bring up in conversation". set up a business = start a business.
int_u144.exc:
come up with = think up; come up (without with) = arise.

look up (a word in a dictionary) separable: look it up.
mistakes: come up an idea → come up with an idea — with.
look up it → look it up.
show on / turn on (arrive) → show up / turn up — "to appear" = show/turn up.

int_u145.text: Unit 145 – Phrasal verbs with away and back
int_u145.note: go away / throw away / come back / look back
int_u145.exp:
Unit 145 — completing the series: away and back.

Away (removal, disposal, continuity): go away (leave), run away (run away), get away (slip away; go on holiday), throw away (discard; miss a chance), give away (give away free; reveal a secret), put away (put back in place), keep away (from) (stay away from), pass away (euph. to pass away). Back (return, response): come back, go back, get back (to return); give back, pay back, send back (to return, give back); call/ring/phone back (to call back); look back (to look back).

Examples: Don't throw those papers away. He gave away all his money to charity. I'll call you back later.

Synonyms/nuances: throw away ≈ throw out (to discard). get back (to return) ≠ get sth back (to get back). come back home — no to. put away (on a shelf) ≠ put off (postpone in time).
int_u145.exc:
get back = return; get sth back = get back.

throw away / throw out — synonyms (discard).
mistakes: come back to home → come back home — home without to.
go away from here (imperative tone) ok, but go out away — go away without extra particles.
throw away it → throw it away.

int_u15.note: She had already left when I arrived.
int_u15.exp:
Unit 15 — Past Perfect ('past in the past') shows that an action happened BEFORE another past action or moment.

Formula: had + V3 (same for all persons). Contraction: 'd.

Cases:
  • Sequence: When I arrived, the film had already started. — When I arrived, the film had already started.
  • Cause/state: She was tired because she hadn't slept. — She was tired because she hadn't slept.
  • Reported speech: He said he had seen it.
Signals: already, just, never, by the time, before, after, when.
Contrast with Past Simple: no shift to the past is needed if the sequence is already clear (I woke up and had breakfast). Past Perfect is needed when the order of events matters and is not obvious.
int_u15.exc:
Past Perfect is not needed if the sequence is already clear (especially with before, after): After I ate, I left.
Stative verbs are not used in Perfect Continuous — only in Past Perfect: I had known.
mistakes: When I came, she already left. → When I came, she had already left. — earlier past.
He said he saw the film before. → He said he had seen the film before. — in reported speech.
I had ate before he arrived. → I had eaten before he arrived. — V3 is needed (eaten).

int_u16.note: I had been waiting for an hour before she arrived.
int_u16.exp:
Unit 16 — Past Perfect Continuous denotes an ongoing process that was happening BEFORE a specific moment in the past (sometimes — with a result at that moment).

Formula: had been + V-ing.

Meanings:
  • Duration up to past moment: By 2020, she had been teaching for 15 years. — By 2020, she had been teaching for 15 years.
  • Cause of past state: His eyes were red because he had been crying. — His eyes were red because he had been crying.
  • Interrupted process: I had been waiting for an hour when the bus finally came.
Signals: for, since, how long, all day, before.
Stative verbs are not used in Continuous — we use Past Perfect: I had known him for years before he told me.
Contrast: Past Perfect — fact/result; Past Perfect Continuous — process/duration.
int_u16.exc: Stative verbs are not used — we use Past Perfect: I had known him for years.
mistakes: She was tired because she worked all day. → She was tired because she had been working all day. — ongoing action up to past moment.
He had been knowing her for years. → He had known her for years. — know is stative.
I had waiting for an hour. → I had been waiting for an hour. — the form had been + -ing is needed.

int_u17.note: Have you got a car? / Do you have a car?
int_u17.exp:
Unit 17 — have got (BrE) and have (AmE/universal) for possession.

Meanings are identical: I've got a brother = I have a brother.

Forms:
  • have got: (+) I've got / She's got; (−) I haven't got / She hasn't got; (?) Have you got…? Has she got…?
  • have: (+) I have / She has; (−) I don't have / She doesn't have; (?) Do you have…? Does she have…?
Important restriction: have got — only in the present tense. In the past — only had (without got): I had a bike when I was 10. Question: Did you have…?

Actions (not ownership) — only have without got: have breakfast/a shower/a party/a good time. Here have = 'perform/spend'.
int_u17.exc:
Have got only in the present. In the past — had (without got): I had a car then.
For actions (not possession) we use have: have lunch, have a shower (not have got).
mistakes: I've got a shower every morning. → I have a shower every morning. — action, not possession.
She had got a car last year. → She had a car last year. — have got only in Present.
Have you a pen? → Have you got a pen? / Do you have a pen? — proper form is needed.

int_u18.note: I used to smoke but I stopped.
int_u18.exp:
Unit 18 — used to + infinitive describes regular habits or states in the past that no longer exist.

Formula: (+) used to + V1; (−) didn't use to + V1; (?) Did + S + use to + V1?

Examples:
  • I used to play the guitar. — I used to play the guitar (not anymore).
  • She used to have long hair. — She used to have long hair (state).
  • Didn't you use to smoke? — Didn't you use to smoke?
  • We didn't use to have internet. — We didn't use to have internet.
Important:
  • There is no present tense form: for current habits — Present Simple (I play tennis).
  • After did — use to (without -d).
Don't confuse! used to do (past habit) vs. be used to doing (be accustomed to something, see Unit 61).
int_u18.exc:
There is no present form: used to — only about the past.
Don't confuse: be/get used to + -ing = get accustomed (I'm used to getting up early).
mistakes: I use to play tennis when I was young. → I used to play tennis… — form used to.
I am used to smoke. → I used to smoke. — for past habits.
Did you used to live there? → Did you use to live there? — after did — use to.

int_u19.text: Unit 19 – Present Continuous and Simple for the future
int_u19.note: I'm meeting her tomorrow. The train leaves at 8.
int_u19.exp:
Unit 19 — using present tenses to express the future.

Present Continuous — personal plans and arrangements: what you have already arranged/agreed with someone.
  • I'm meeting Sarah at 6. — I'm meeting Sarah at 6 (arranged).
  • We're flying to Madrid on Monday. — We're flying to Madrid on Monday (tickets booked).
Present Simple — schedules, programs, timetables (independent of the speaker): trains, films, classes.
  • The train leaves at 7:45. — The train leaves at 7:45.
  • The film starts at 8. — The film starts at 8.
  • Term begins on September 1st. — Term begins on September 1st.
Contrast with going to: Present Continuous emphasizes the arrangement, going to — intention. Often interchangeable. Stative verbs (be, know, need) — in Simple.
int_u19.exc: Stative verbs → Present Simple: I need help tomorrow (not I'm needing).
mistakes: I meet her tomorrow. → I'm meeting her tomorrow. — personal plan → Continuous.
The train is leaving at 8 every day. → The train leaves at 8 every day. — schedule → Simple.
What you doing tonight? → What are you doing tonight? — are is needed.

int_u20.note: I'm going to study harder. It's going to snow.
int_u20.exp:
Unit 20 — be going to for intentions and predictions with obvious signs.

Formula: am/is/are + going to + V1.

Two meanings:
  • Intention (decision already made): I'm going to quit my job next month. — I'm going to quit.
  • Prediction with visible evidence: Look at those clouds — it's going to rain! — Look at the clouds — it's going to rain!
Past — was/were going to (planned but didn't do): I was going to call you, but I forgot. — I was going to call you, but I forgot.

Examples:
  • She's going to have a baby. — She's expecting a baby (visibly/planned).
  • We're going to visit Spain this summer.
Style: instead of be going to go/come people often just say Present Continuous: I'm going to the cinema.
int_u20.exc: Instead of be going to go/come people often just say Present Continuous: I'm going to the party (not I'm going to go).
mistakes: Look — it going to rain! → Look — it's going to rain! — is is needed.
I going to study tonight. → I'm going to study tonight. — auxiliary am.
She's going quit her job. → She's going to quit her job. — to before the infinitive is needed.

int_u21.note: I'll help you. It will be cold tomorrow.
int_u21.exp:
Unit 21 — will/shall for spontaneous decisions, promises, requests, and predictions without obvious signs.

Formula: will/'ll + V1; negation won't; question Will + S + V1?

Meanings:
  • Spontaneous decision: The phone's ringing — I'll get it! — I'll get it!
  • Promise: I'll call you tomorrow. I promise.
  • Prediction (opinion): I think Brazil will win.
  • Offer/refusal: I'll help you / The door won't open.
Important rule: after if, when, as soon as, before, after, until — never will, use Present: When he arrives, I'll tell him.
Shall (BrE, rare in AmE) — with I/we for offers: Shall I open the window? Shall we dance?
Contrast: will = decision now; going to = decision made earlier.
int_u21.exc: After if, when, as soon as, before, after — never will: When he comes, I'll tell him (not when he will come).
mistakes: I'll call you when I will arrive. → …when I arrive. — in time clauses will is forbidden.
I think it'll rain probably. → I think it will probably rain. — adverb between will and the verb.
He'll to help us. → He'll help us. — after will — infinitive without to.

int_u22.note: Shall I open the window? Will you be there?
int_u22.exp:
Unit 22 — functions of will/shall: offering help, requests, agreement, refusal.

Shall I/we…? — offer of help or joint suggestion:
  • Shall I carry your bag? — Shall I carry your bag?
  • Shall we go to the cinema? — Shall we go to the cinema?
Will you…? — request or invitation:
  • Will you close the door, please? — Will you close the door?
  • Will you have some tea? — Will you have some tea?
Refusal/object's disobedience (won't): The car won't start. — The car won't start.

Firm promise/threat: I'll be there, don't worry. / You'll regret this.

Important: shall with you/he/she/they sounds archaic; in requests — will/would. In AmE shall is practically not used. After all these forms — infinitive without to.
int_u22.exc: Shall with I/we for offers and questions; in American English it is almost not used.
mistakes: Shall you open the window? → Will you open the window? / Shall I open it? — shall only with I/we.
I'll to help you. → I'll help you. — will + V without to.
Will I carry your bag? → Shall I carry your bag? — offer of help — shall I.

int_u23.note: I'll do it. / I'm going to do it.
int_u23.exp:
Unit 23 — key difference will vs. going to.

will — decision AT THE MOMENT of speech:
  • A: We need milk. B: OK, I'll buy some. — Decided right now.
going to — decision made BEFORE the moment of speech:
  • A: Why are you going to the shop? B: I'm going to buy milk. — Already planned.
Predictions:
  • will — opinion/feeling without evidence: I think she'll like the gift.
  • going to — there is visible evidence: She's going to have a baby (visible) / The sky is dark — it's going to rain.
Test question: 'Did I decide this just now?' — will. 'Was I already thinking about it?' — going to.
Weather forecast by announcer: will (official forecast) or going to (visible signs).
int_u23.exc: In predictions based on opinion (without evidence) — will: I think she'll win. In predictions based on visible evidence — going to: Look at the clouds — it's going to rain.
mistakes: The phone's ringing. I'm going to answer it. → I'll answer it. — spontaneous decision → will.
I've decided: I will buy a new car. → I'm going to buy a new car. — already made decision → going to.
Look at the sky! It will rain. → It's going to rain. — there is evidence → going to.

int_u24.text: Unit 24 - Future Continuous and Future Perfect
int_u24.note: I'll be working at 8. I'll have finished by then.
int_u24.exp:
Unit 24 — Future Continuous and Future Perfect.

Future Continuous (will be + V-ing) — the action will be ongoing at a specific future moment or about plans/polite questions.
  • This time tomorrow I'll be flying to Rome. — This time tomorrow I'll be flying to Rome.
  • Will you be using your car tonight? — Will you be using your car tonight? (polite inquiry).
Future Perfect (will have + V3) — the action will be completed BY a specific moment.
  • By 2030, we will have moved. — By 2030, we will have already moved.
  • She'll have finished the report by Friday.
Signals: by + time, by the time… → Perfect; at + time, this time next… → Continuous.
Future Perfect Continuous (will have been V-ing) — a rare form for duration: By June I'll have been working here for 10 years.
int_u24.exc: Future Perfect Continuous (will have been doing) is used rarely — to emphasize duration: By next month I'll have been working here for 10 years.
mistakes: At 10 tomorrow I will sleep. → At 10 tomorrow I'll be sleeping. — at a specific moment — Continuous.
By Friday I'll finish the report. → By Friday I'll have finished the report. — by a deadline — Perfect.
I will been working. → I will have been working. — the form will have been is needed.

int_u25.note: If it rains, I'll take an umbrella.
int_u25.exp:
Unit 25 — First Conditional: real/probable future conditions.

Formula: If + Present Simple, will/can/may/might + V1. The order of clauses can be changed (comma only if the if-clause is first).

Examples:
  • If it rains, we'll stay home. — If it rains, we'll stay home.
  • You'll pass the exam if you study. — You'll pass the exam if you study.
  • If you need help, call me. (imperative in the main clause).
Iron rule: after if, when, as soon as, unless, until, before, after — NEVER will.

Conjunction variants:
  • unless = if…not: Unless you hurry, you'll be late.
  • as long as / provided (that) — on condition that: I'll lend it as long as you return it.
  • in case — in case: Take an umbrella in case it rains.
int_u25.exc:
After if — never will.
Unless = if…not: Unless you hurry, you'll be late.
mistakes: If it will rain, we'll stay home. → If it rains, we'll stay home. — after if no will.
If you will call me, I answer. → If you call me, I'll answer. — will only in the main clause.
Unless you don't hurry, you'll be late. → Unless you hurry… — unless already contains negation.

int_u26.text: Unit 26 - Can, could, be able to
int_u26.note: I can swim. I was able to escape. I'll be able to come.
int_u26.exp:
Unit 26 — expressing ability: can, could, be able to.

can — ability/possibility in the present: I can speak French. Form can't/cannot.
could — general ability in the past (what you could do regularly): I could swim when I was 5.
was/were able to — a single success in a specific past situation: The door was locked but I was able to open it. — The door was locked, but I managed to open it.

be able to — the only form for:
  • Future: I'll be able to help tomorrow.
  • Perfect: I haven't been able to contact her.
  • After modals: You must be able to drive.
  • Infinitive: I want to be able to speak Spanish.
In the negative couldn't can also be used for single cases: I couldn't open the door. = I wasn't able to.
int_u26.exc:
For single past success — only was/were able to, not could: I was able to escape (not I could escape).
In the negative both forms are possible: couldn't / wasn't able to.
mistakes: Yesterday I could finally find my keys. → Yesterday I was able to find my keys. — single success.
She can to swim. → She can swim. — after a modal — without to.
I will can help you. → I will be able to help you. — after will — be able to.

int_u27.note: Could you help? I could be wrong.
int_u27.exp:
Unit 27 — additional meanings of could.

1. Polite request: Could you pass the salt, please? — Could you pass the salt, please?
2. Theoretical possibility (present/future): It could rain later. — It could rain (= might).
3. Suggestion: We could go to the beach. — We could go to the beach.
4. Could + have + V3 — possibility in the past:
  • Missed opportunity: You could have told me! — You could have told me!
  • Speculation about the past: She could have missed the train. — She could have missed the train.
Couldn't have + V3 — certainty that something couldn't have happened: He couldn't have known — he wasn't there.

Contrast with can: could — softer, more polite, less definite. Don't confuse: could-request and could-past — context decides.
int_u27.exc: Could have + V3 = could have, but didn't: You could have told me!
mistakes: Could you to help me? → Could you help me? — without to.
It could to rain. → It could rain. — after a modal — infinitive without to.
He could have told, but he didn't tell. → He could have told me but he didn't. — don't duplicate the verb.

int_u28.note: She must be tired. He can't be serious.
int_u28.exp:
Unit 28 — must and can't for logical conclusions (certainty).

must — confident positive conclusion: 'surely, must be'.
  • You must be exhausted after the flight. — You must be exhausted after the flight.
  • He must know — he was there. — He must know.
can't — confident negative conclusion: 'can't be':
  • That can't be John — he's in Paris. — That can't be John.
  • She can't be hungry — she just ate.
About the past:
  • must have + V3: She must have left early. — She must have left early.
  • can't have + V3: He can't have seen us. — He couldn't have seen us.
Important! mustn't ≠ conclusion; it's prohibition ('must not'). To negate a conclusion, use can't, not mustn't.
Contrast: must (logic, 95% certainty) vs. may/might (possibility, 50%).
int_u28.exc: For certainty about the past: must have + V3 / can't have + V3: She must have left. He can't have seen us.
mistakes: She mustn't be tired — she's smiling. → She can't be tired — she's smiling. — mustn't — prohibition, not negation of conclusion.
He must to be rich. → He must be rich. — after a modal — without to.
You must be joking yesterday. → You must have been joking yesterday. — about the past — must have.

int_u29.note: It might rain. She may not come.
int_u29.exp:
Unit 29 — may and might for expressing possibility in the present/future.

Both mean 'possibly, maybe' (≈ 40–60%). The difference is subtle: might is slightly less certain, but often interchangeable.

Formulas:
  • (+) may/might + V1
  • (−) may not / might not (mightn't) — the contraction mayn't is not used!
  • (?) rarely — usually via Do you think…?
Examples:
  • She may/might be at home. — She may be at home.
  • It may/might rain tomorrow.
  • I might go to the party, I'm not sure.
may — also formal permission: You may leave now.

Contrast with modals:
  • will — 100%, must — 95%, may/might — 50%, could — theoretical possibility.
After may/might — infinitive without to, form doesn't change by person.
int_u29.exc:
There is no contraction mayn't. We use may not / might not / mightn't.
May for permission: You may leave.
mistakes: He mays come. → He may come. — modals don't change by person.
She might to call. → She might call. — without to.
It mayn't rain. → It may not rain. — there is no mayn't contraction.

int_u30.text: Unit 30 – May and might (lesson 2): the past
int_u30.note: She might have missed the train.
int_u30.exp:
Unit 30 — may/might + have + V3 for speculation about the past.

Formula: may/might (not) + have + V3. Means 'possibly (didn't) do' (≈ 50%).

Examples:
  • She's late — she might have missed the bus. — She's late — she might have missed the bus.
  • I don't know where he is. He may have gone home. — He may have gone home.
  • They may not have received the letter yet.
Certainty scale about the past:
  • must have (sure yes): She must have forgotten.
  • may/might have (possibly): She might have forgotten.
  • can't/couldn't have (sure no): She can't have forgotten.
Special meaning of might have: reproach — 'could have…': You might have told me! — You could have told me!
int_u30.exc: Might have + V3 also expresses reproach: You might have told me! (could have told).
mistakes: She may missed the bus. → She may have missed the bus. — about the past — may have + V3.
He might have took the wrong road. → He might have taken the wrong road. — V3 is needed.
They might been late. → They might have been late. — the form have been is needed.

int_u31.note: I must go. / I have to go.
int_u31.exp:
Unit 31 — must and have to: necessity/obligation.

must — internal necessity, the speaker's personal conviction, the speaker's order:
  • I must call my mother. — I must call my mother (I think so).
  • You must see this film! — You must see this film!
have to — external requirement: rules, circumstances, other people:
  • I have to wear a uniform at work. — I have to wear a uniform (rule).
  • She has to leave early on Fridays.
Forms of have to: (−) don't have to; (?) Do you have to…?; past had to, future will have to.

Must has no past/future — use had to / will have to.
In questions, have to is more common: Do I have to go?
Important difference in the negative: mustn't = prohibition (must not); don't have to = absence of necessity (not required).
int_u31.exc:
In the past — only had to (must has no past).
Mustn't ≠ don't have to: prohibition vs. non-necessity.
mistakes: I musted work late. → I had to work late. — past — had to.
She mustn't go — it's optional. → She doesn't have to go. — mustn't = must not, not 'not required'.
Do I must go? → Do I have to go? / Must I go? — must doesn't use do.

int_u32.note: You must stop. You needn't worry.
int_u32.exp:
Unit 32 — must, mustn't, needn't / don't need to.

must — obligation/necessity: You must wear a helmet.
mustn't — prohibition, 'must not': You mustn't smoke here.
needn't / don't need to / don't have to — absence of necessity, 'not required': You needn't come early — we'll wait.

⚠️ Main difference: mustn't vs. needn't — these are opposites in meaning:
  • You mustn't tell anyone. — You must not tell anyone.
  • You needn't tell anyone. — You don't have to tell anyone (not required).
Past:
  • needn't have + V3 — didn't need to, but did (in vain): You needn't have cooked — we ate.
  • didn't need to + V1 — didn't have to (and didn't do it): I didn't need to cook — we ordered pizza.
needn't — modal, without to; need to — regular verb, with to.
int_u32.exc:
Needn't have + V3 = didn't have to (but did): You needn't have cooked — we ate.
Didn't need to = didn't have to (didn't do it).
mistakes: You mustn't worry — it's not required. → You needn't worry. — mustn't = prohibition.
She needn't to come. → She needn't come. — needn't — modal, without to.
You mustn't to smoke here. → You mustn't smoke here. — without to.

int_u33.exp:
Unit 33 — should for advice, recommendations, personal opinion.

Formula: should (not) + V1. Synonym — ought to (more formal).

Meanings:
  • Advice/recommendation: You should eat more vegetables. — You should eat more vegetables.
  • Opinion on what's right: The government should do more.
  • Asking for advice: What should I do?
should have + V3 — reproach/regret about the past ('should have, but didn't'):
  • You should have called. — You should have called.
  • I shouldn't have said that. — I shouldn't have said that.
Contrast: should — soft advice; must/have to — obligation; could — a possible option.
Common phrases: I think you should…, Do you think I should…?, You'd better (stronger advice, see Unit 35).
int_u33.exc:
Should have + V3 = should have done, but didn't (reproach/regret): You should have called.
Ought to — synonym of should, but more formal.
mistakes: You should to see a doctor. → You should see a doctor. — without to.
She should went earlier. → She should have gone earlier. — regret about the past.
You shouldn't to smoke. → You shouldn't smoke. — without to.

int_u34.note: She should be here by now.
int_u34.exp:
Unit 34 — should for expressing expectations and assumptions.

Meanings:
  • Expectation (it should be so): She should be here by now. — She should already be here.
  • Probable result: The package should arrive tomorrow. — The package should arrive tomorrow.
  • Surprise/indignation (Why should…?): Why should I pay? — Why should I pay?
should have + V3 — it was expected but may not have happened: The letter should have arrived yesterday. — The letter should have arrived yesterday (but…).

In formal constructions after It's strange/surprising/funny that…, I'm surprised/sorry that…: It's odd that he should say that. — It's odd that he should say that.

Contrast: should (expectation, 80%) vs. must (certainty, 95%) vs. might (possibility, 50%).
int_u34.exc: Should have + V3 = should have already happened: The letter should have arrived by now.
mistakes: The bus must be here any minute. → The bus should be here any minute. — expectation — should.
She should has finished. → She should have finished. — should have, not has.
The train should to arrive at 10. → The train should arrive at 10. — without to.

int_u35.text: Unit 35 – It's time and you'd better
int_u35.note: It's time to go. You'd better hurry.
int_u35.exp:
Unit 35 — It's time and had better.

It's time + to-infinitive — it's time (general): It's time to go.
It's (high/about) time + subject + Past Simple — it's high time (reproach that action is delayed): It's time you went to bed. — It's high time you went to bed. Subjunctive, Past Simple with present meaning!

had better ('d better) + V1 — had better (advice with warning about unpleasant consequences):
  • You'd better hurry or you'll miss the train. — You'd better hurry.
  • You'd better not tell her. — You'd better not tell her.
Features of had better:
  • Refers to a specific situation now/soon, not to general advice.
  • Always past form had, but the meaning is present/future.
  • Negation: 'd better not (not hadn't better).
  • Stronger than should: implies threat/problem.
int_u35.exc:
After It's time with a subject — Past Simple: It's time you went (not you go).
Had better — only present/future, not past.
mistakes: It's time you go to bed. → It's time you went to bed. — with a subject — Past Simple.
You'd better to hurry. → You'd better hurry. — without to.
You better not smoke. → You'd better not smoke. — need 'd (had).

int_u36.note: Would you like...? I'd like to go. I would...
int_u36.exp:
Unit 36 — multiple functions of would.

1. Polite offers and wishes:
  • Would you like some coffee? — Would you like some coffee?
  • I'd like to book a table. — I'd like to book a table.
2. Hypothetical/imaginary (Type 2):
  • If I had money, I would travel more.
  • What would you do? — What would you do?
3. Regular actions in the past (= used to, but only about actions):
  • As a child, I would spend hours reading. — As a child, I would read for hours.
4. Refusal in the past (won't in the past): The car wouldn't start. — The car wouldn't start.

Formulas: would + V1; would have + V3 (Type 3 and hypothetical past).
⚠️ For past STATES — only used to or Past Simple: I had a dog (not I would have). After would like — infinitive with to.
int_u36.exc:
Would like + to-infinitive (not -ing): I'd like to go.
Would for past habits — only about actions (not states): I had a bike (not I would have).
mistakes: Would you like going? → Would you like to go? — after would like — infinitive with to.
I would to travel more. → I'd like to travel more. — like is needed.
When I was a child I would have a dog. → …I had a dog. — would not for states.

int_u37.exp:
Unit 37 — requests and permission with can, could, would, may.

Request for action (will you do?):
  • Can you help me? — neutral.
  • Could you help me? — more polite.
  • Would you help me? — polite/formal.
  • Would you mind + V-ing? — the most polite: Would you mind closing the window?
Request for permission (may I?):
  • Can I…? — informal.
  • Could I…? — polite.
  • May I…? — formal: May I come in?
Answers: Sure / Of course / Certainly / No problem / I'm afraid not.
⚠️ Important with Would you mind: agreement = No, not at all / No, of course not (= 'I don't mind'). Answer Yes means 'I mind'. After Would you mind — gerund (-ing), not infinitive.
int_u37.exc:
After Would you mind — gerund: Would you mind opening the window?
Affirmative answer to Would you mind…? = No, not at all (= I don't mind).
mistakes: Would you mind to open the window? → Would you mind opening the window? — -ing is needed.
Could you to help? → Could you help? — without to.
Would you mind? — Yes, of course (wanting to agree). → No, not at all. — yes means 'I mind'.

int_u38.text: Unit 38 - Conditional Type 1 and 2 (If I do / If I did)
int_u38.note: If I see her, I'll tell her. If I were rich…
int_u38.exp:
Unit 38 — comparison of First and Second Conditional.

Type 1 (real/probable future): If + Present Simple, will + V1
  • If I see her, I'll tell her. — If I see her, I'll tell her.
Type 2 (unreal/unlikely present or future): If + Past Simple, would + V1
  • If I were rich, I would travel the world. — If I were rich, I would travel.
  • If I knew her number, I'd call her (but I don't know).
Key difference — probability:
  • Type 1: real situation, may happen.
  • Type 2: hypothesis, not true now or unlikely.
Subjunctive were for all persons in Type 2: If I were you… (if I were in your place) — standard phrase of advice. In informal speech was is acceptable. After if never will/would.
int_u38.exc:
In Type 2 instead of was, were is often used for all persons: If I were you…
After if we don't put will/would.
mistakes: If I was you, I'd study. → If I were you, I'd study. — formally — were.
If I would have time, I would help. → If I had time, I would help. — after if no would.
If he comes, I would tell him. → If he comes, I'll tell him. / If he came, I would tell him. — match types.

int_u39.text: Unit 39 – Conditional type 2 and I wish
int_u39.note: I wish I knew the answer.
int_u39.exp:
Unit 39 — I wish construction for unreal present (= Type 2).

Formula: I wish + subject + Past Simple/were — regret about the current state of affairs, wish for things to be different now.

Examples:
  • I wish I knew the answer. — I wish I knew the answer.
  • I wish I had more time. — If only I had more time.
  • I wish she were here. — I wish she were here.
  • He wishes he could speak Spanish. — He wishes he could speak Spanish.
Synonym — If only: more emotional expression of the same: If only I knew!

Important:
  • After I wish — Past Simple (not Present!) for unreal present.
  • I wish I were (formal) or I wish I was (informal).
  • Modals: can → could. Cannot say I wish I will/would (to oneself).
int_u39.exc:
I wish I were… (formal) or I wish I was… (informal).
After I wish for the present — Past Simple (not Present).
mistakes: I wish I have a car. → I wish I had a car. — for unreal present — Past Simple.
I wish I am taller. → I wish I were taller. — subjunctive were.
I wish I can fly. → I wish I could fly. — can → could.

int_u40.text: Unit 40 - Conditional Type 3 (If I had done)
int_u40.note: If I had studied, I would have passed.
int_u40.exp:
Unit 40 — Third Conditional: unreal past.

Formula: If + Past Perfect, would/could/might have + V3. We speak about what DID NOT happen in the past and about imaginary consequences.

Examples:
  • If I had studied, I would have passed. — If I had studied, I would have passed (but I didn't study and didn't pass).
  • If you hadn't helped me, I couldn't have finished.
  • She would have come if you had invited her.
Mixed Conditional: past condition + present result or vice versa:
  • If I had taken the job, I would be in Tokyo now. — If I had taken the job (past), I would be in Tokyo now (present).
  • If I were more organised, I would have finished. — If I were more organized (trait), I would have finished.
⚠️ After if — never would have. Always had + V3.
int_u40.exc: Mixed type: past condition + present result: If I had studied, I would know it now.
mistakes: If I would have known, I would have helped. → If I had known, I would have helped. — after if — had + V3.
If she had came, we would have seen her. → If she had come, we would have seen her. — V3 of come is come.
If you listened, you would pass. (about the past) → If you had listened, you would have passed. — full Type 3.

int_u41.note: I wish I could fly. I wish he hadn't done that.
int_u41.exp:
Unit 41 — all constructions with wish and if only.

1. wish + Past Simple — regret about the present: I wish I had a car. — I wish I had a car.
2. wish + Past Perfect — regret about the past: I wish I hadn't said that. — I wish I hadn't said that.
3. wish + would — irritation at another's behavior or desire for change:
  • I wish you would stop smoking. — I wish you'd stop smoking.
  • I wish it would stop raining.
4. wish + could — desire to have an ability:
  • I wish I could help. — I wish I could help.
Important rules:
  • I wish I would… — NOT ALLOWED (to oneself — only could).
  • wish + would — about irritating behavior, or about external changes.
If only — same formulas, more emotional/dramatic tone: If only I had listened!
int_u41.exc: I wish + would cannot be used about oneself: cannot say I wish I would… — use could.
mistakes: I wish I would be taller. → I wish I were taller. — would doesn't apply to oneself.
I wish I didn't say that yesterday. → I wish I hadn't said that yesterday. — about the past — Past Perfect.
If only he will stop. → If only he would stop. — would is needed.

int_u42.note: The window was broken. Cheese is made from milk.
int_u42.exp:
Unit 42 — basics of the passive voice: focus on action/object, not on the performer.

Formula: to be (in the required tense) + V3.

Main tenses:
  • Present Simple: is/are + V3: Coffee is grown in Brazil.
  • Past Simple: was/were + V3: The house was built in 1890.
  • Future Simple: will be + V3: The results will be announced tomorrow.
by-phrase is used only when the doer is important: Hamlet was written by Shakespeare. Otherwise omitted: My wallet was stolen (doesn't matter by whom).

When the passive is used:
  • The doer is unknown or unimportant.
  • The action is more important than the doer.
  • In scientific and official texts.
⚠️ Intransitive verbs (arrive, happen, go, sleep, come, exist) do NOT form the passive. Regular verbs — -ed; irregular — V3 table.
int_u42.exc:
Intransitive verbs (arrive, happen, sleep) cannot be in the passive.
V3 of irregular verbs must be known: break/broken, write/written, take/taken.
mistakes: The window broke by the kids. → The window was broken by the kids. — be + V3 is needed.
He was stole the money. → The money was stolen. — passive applies to the object.
The accident was happened yesterday. → The accident happened yesterday. — happen is intransitive.

int_u43.text: Unit 43 – Passive voice in all tenses (lesson 2)
int_u43.note: is being made / has been found / will be built
int_u43.exp:
Unit 43 — passive in all tenses and with modals.

General rule: be agrees with the tense, V3 is unchanged.

Full table:
  • Present Simple: is made
  • Present Continuous: is being made
  • Present Perfect: has been made
  • Past Simple: was made
  • Past Continuous: was being made
  • Past Perfect: had been made
  • Future: will be made
  • Future Perfect: will have been made
Modals + passive: modal + be + V3: must be done, can be fixed, should be repaired, might be lost.

Examples:
  • The road is being repaired. — The road is being repaired.
  • The letter has been sent. — The letter has been sent.
  • This can't be true. (active form be with modal).
⚠️ Perfect Continuous and Future Continuous passives are rarely used.
int_u43.exc: Modals in passive: modal + be + V3: must be done, can be seen, should be repaired.
mistakes: The road is repairing now. → The road is being repaired now. — Continuous Passive = is being + V3.
The letter has sent. → The letter has been sent. — Perfect Passive = has been + V3.
It must done. → It must be done. — after a modal — be + V3.

int_u44.note: I was told. He was given a book.
int_u44.exp:
Unit 44 — passive of verbs with two objects.

Verbs give, tell, show, offer, ask, send, teach, pay, promise, lend have two objects (to whom + what). In the passive, either can become the subject:

Active: They gave Anna a present.
Passive 1 (more common): Anna was given a present. — Anna was given a present.
Passive 2: A present was given to Anna. (preposition to is needed).

More examples:
  • I was told the truth. — I was told the truth.
  • He was offered a job. — He was offered a job.
  • She was shown the results.
⚠️ Special verbs:
  • explain, describe, suggest, say, mention — the person does NOT become the passive subject. Cannot say I was explained → It was explained to me.
Frequency choice: native speakers more often passivize the person (human subject), since he/she is who the sentence is about.
int_u44.exc: The verb explain doesn't passivize to a person: cannot say I was explained — only It was explained to me.
mistakes: I was explained the rule. → The rule was explained to me. / I was told the rule. — explain is special.
A book was given me. → A book was given to me. / I was given a book. — to is needed.
He was offered by a job. → He was offered a job. — no by before a direct object.

int_u45.text: Unit 45 – It is said… / He is said to be…
int_u45.note: It is said that he is rich. He is said to be rich.
int_u45.exp:
Unit 45 — passive reportive constructions: It is said that… / He is said to….

Verbs of opinion/reporting: say, think, believe, report, know, expect, consider, understand, assume. Used in impersonal constructions when we convey general opinion, rumors, news.

Two ways of translating the same meaning:
  • It is said that he is very rich. (impersonal)
  • He is said to be very rich. (personal with infinitive)
Formulas:
  • It + is/was + said + that + clause
  • Subject + is/was + said + to-infinitive
Present vs. past:
  • Present: He is believed to live in Spain.
  • Past: He is believed to have lived in Spain. (perfect infinitive)
Examples:
  • The painting is thought to be genuine.
  • She is known to have been a spy.
int_u45.exc: Past in this construction: He is said to have been — perfect infinitive: She is believed to have left the country.
mistakes: He is said he is rich. → He is said to be rich. / It is said that he is rich. — proper construction.
She is known has been rich. → She is known to have been rich. — to have + V3 is needed.
It said that he is clever. → It is said that he is clever. — is is needed.

int_u46.exp:
Unit 46 — causative construction have/get something done: hire someone to do the work.

Formula: have/get + object + V3.

Meaning: we don't do it ourselves — we arranged for someone to do it for us (or something happened to us).

Examples:
  • I had my hair cut. — I had my hair cut (I went to the hairdresser).
  • She's having her house painted. — She's having her house painted (hired someone).
  • Where do you get your car serviced? — Where do you get your car serviced?
  • I need to get my eyes tested. — I need to get my eyes tested.
have vs. get: meanings are identical; get is more informal.

Second meaning — unpleasant event: He had his wallet stolen. — His wallet was stolen.

Contrast:
  • I repaired my car — I fixed it myself.
  • I had my car repaired — I had it fixed.
⚠️ Order: object strictly before V3.
int_u46.exc:
In informal style get something done = have something done: I got my hair cut.
In idioms have — about unpleasant events: He had his wallet stolen.
mistakes: I cut my hair yesterday. (actually — at the hairdresser's) → I had my hair cut yesterday. — didn't do it myself.
She is having painted her house. → She is having her house painted. — order: have + object + V3.
I had repaired my car. (arranged) → I had my car repaired. — word order.

int_u47.note: She said she was tired. He told me he would come.
int_u47.exp:
Unit 47 — reported speech: rules of tense shift.

Main shifts (if the reporting verb is in the past):
  • Present Simple → Past Simple
  • Present Continuous → Past Continuous
  • Present Perfect → Past Perfect
  • Past Simple → Past Perfect
  • will → would, can → could, may → might, must → had to
Changes in pronouns and adverbs:
  • I → he/she, my → his/her, we → they
  • today → that day, tomorrow → the next day, yesterday → the day before, here → there, this → that
Examples:
  • "I'm tired." → She said (that) she was tired.
  • "I'll call you." → He said he would call me.
say vs. tell:
  • say (to smb) that…: She said that she was late.
  • tell + somebody + that…: She told me that she was late. Cannot say tell that.
Shift is not needed if what was said remains true: She said the Earth is round.
int_u47.exc:
If what was said is still true, tense shift is optional: She said the Earth is round.
After tell, indication of the addressee is mandatory: She told me…
mistakes: She said me she was tired. → She told me she was tired. / She said she was tired. — after say — without addressee.
He said he will come. → He said he would come. — tense shift: will → would.
She told that she was late. → She said that she was late. / She told me that… — tell requires an object.

int_u48.text: Unit 48 - Reported Speech (Lesson 2): Questions and Orders
int_u48.note: He asked me where I lived. She told me to wait.
int_u48.exp:
Unit 48 — reported questions, orders, requests.

Reported questions:
  • Wh-questions: ask + wh-word + direct order: "Where do you live?" → He asked where I lived.
  • Yes/no questions: ask + if/whether + direct order: "Are you ready?" → She asked if I was ready.
⚠️ In a reported question:
  • No inversion (where I lived, not where did I live).
  • No do/does/did.
  • No question mark.
  • There is a tense shift.
Reported orders/requests: tell/ask/order/warn + object + (not) to-infinitive:
  • "Wait!" → She told me to wait.
  • "Please help me." → He asked me to help him.
  • "Don't go." → She told me not to go.
Reporting verbs: tell, ask, order, beg, advise, warn, remind, invite, encourage.
int_u48.exc: In a reported question direct word order — without do/does/did: He asked where I lived (not where did I live).
mistakes: He asked where did I live. → He asked where I lived. — direct order in a reported question.
She asked me wait. → She told me to wait. / She asked me to wait. — to-inf is needed.
He asked if am I ready. → He asked if I was ready. — direct order + tense shift.

int_u49.note: Do you? Is she? Have they? Will it?
int_u49.exp:
Unit 49 — forming all types of questions.

Yes/no questions: inversion of auxiliary/modal verb and subject.
  • Are you ready?
  • Do you speak French?
  • Has she arrived?
  • Can he swim?
Wh-questions: wh-word + aux + subject + verb:
  • Where do you live?
  • What is she doing?
  • When did he leave?
⚠️ Subject questions — no inversion, no do/does/did:
  • Who called? (not Who did call?)
  • What happened?
  • Which bus goes to the centre?
Questions with a preposition: preposition is usually at the end: Who are you waiting for? What are you talking about? Where does she come from?
Negative questions (Don't you know? Isn't it strange?) express surprise or invite agreement.
int_u49.exc: In subject questions there is no inversion and no do/does: Who called? What happened?
mistakes: Who did call you? → Who called you? — subject question without did.
Where she lives? → Where does she live? — inversion with does is needed.
What means this word? → What does this word mean? — does is needed.

int_u50.text: Unit 50 - Questions in simple and complex sentences (lesson 2)
int_u50.note: Do you know where she lives?
int_u50.exp:
Unit 50 — embedded (indirect) questions in complex sentences.

After introductory phrases Do you know…?, Can you tell me…?, I wonder…, I'd like to know…, Have you any idea…?, Could you tell me…? — use direct word order, without inversion and without do/does/did.

Comparison:
  • Direct: Where does she live?
  • Embedded: Do you know where she lives?
  • Direct: What is the time?
  • Embedded: Can you tell me what the time is?
For yes/no questions — if/whether:
  • I wonder if he is coming.
  • Do you know whether they are open?
Examples:
  • Have you any idea how much this costs?
  • I'd like to know when the meeting starts.
⚠️ This rule also extends to reported speech (Unit 48). Question mark is used only if the whole sentence is a question.
int_u50.exc:
After I wonder, Do you know, Can you tell me — direct word order.
For yes/no questions — if/whether: I wonder if she is coming.
mistakes: Do you know where does she live? → Do you know where she lives? — direct order.
Can you tell me what is the time? → Can you tell me what the time is? — without inversion.
I wonder does he know. → I wonder if he knows. — if/whether is needed.

int_u51.text: Unit 51 - Auxiliary verbs in a sentence
int_u51.note: So do I. Neither can she. I do like it!
int_u51.exp:
Unit 51 — auxiliary verbs: short answers, agreement, emphasis, avoiding repetition.

1. Short answers: we use the auxiliary verb of the question.
  • Are you tired? — Yes, I am. / No, I'm not.
  • Has she left? — Yes, she has.
2. So/Neither + auxiliary + subject — short agreement/disagreement:
  • I'm tired. — So am I. — Me too.
  • I can't swim. — Neither can I. / Nor can I.
3. Emphatic do/does/did — emphasis in affirmation:
  • I do love you! — I really love you!
  • She does know the answer.
  • He did call, I swear.
4. Avoiding repetition:
  • I haven't read the book, but Jane has.
  • A: Who can cook? B: I can.
⚠️ With So/Neither — inversion (aux before subject). Me too / Me neither — informal options.
int_u51.exc: So am I / Neither am I — inversion. Me too / Me neither — informal option.
mistakes: I like coffee. — So I do. → So do I. — inversion: so + aux + subject.
I don't like it. — Neither I do. → Neither do I. — proper order.
I do liked it. → I did like it. / I do like it. — emphatic do/did + V1.

int_u52.note: It's cold, isn't it? She didn't call, did she?
int_u52.exp:
Unit 52 — tag questions for confirmation or checking.

Rule: affirmative sentence → negative 'tag'; negative → affirmative.

Formula: auxiliary + (n't) + subject pronoun.

Examples:
  • You're tired, aren't you?
  • She can swim, can't she?
  • He hasn't called, has he?
  • They don't know, do they?
Special cases:
  • I am → aren't I? (not amn't I): I'm late, aren't I?
  • Let's → shall we?: Let's go, shall we?
  • Imperative → will you / won't you / would you: Close the door, will you?
  • After nothing, nobody, no one — use it/they: Nobody called, did they?
Intonation:
  • Falling ↓ — the speaker is sure, expects agreement.
  • Rising ↑ — a real question, uncertain.
int_u52.exc:
I am → aren't I?
Let's → shall we?
Imperative → will you / won't you?
mistakes: I'm late, amn't I? → I'm late, aren't I? — exception for I am.
Let's go, will we? → Let's go, shall we? — after let's — shall we.
She can swim, can she? → She can swim, can't she? — affirmative → negative tag.

int_u53.note: I enjoy swimming. She finished working.
int_u53.exp:
Unit 53 — verbs followed by a gerund (-ing).

Key verbs with -ing: enjoy, finish, stop, mind, avoid, suggest, recommend, admit, deny, consider, imagine, risk, practise, postpone, delay, keep, give up, put off, can't stand, can't help, miss, feel like.

Examples:
  • I enjoy reading. — I enjoy reading.
  • She suggested going for a walk. — She suggested going for a walk.
  • He denied stealing the money. — He denied stealing the money.
  • I can't help laughing. — I can't help laughing.
  • Would you mind waiting?
Negation of gerund: not + V-ing: She admitted not telling the truth.

Common mistakes: be especially careful with suggest and recommend — only -ing, not to-inf: He suggested going (not to go).
Gerund as a noun: can be a subject: Swimming is good for you.
int_u53.exc: After suggest, recommend — only gerund (not to-inf): He suggested going by train.
mistakes: I enjoy to swim. → I enjoy swimming. — after enjoy — gerund.
She suggested to go to the cinema. → She suggested going to the cinema. — after suggest — -ing.
I avoid to speak to him. → I avoid speaking to him. — after avoid — gerund.

int_u54.note: I want to go. She decided to stay.
int_u54.exp:
Unit 54 — verbs followed by an infinitive with to.

Key verbs with to-inf: want, need, hope, plan, decide, expect, agree, refuse, offer, promise, threaten, manage, learn, afford, pretend, seem, appear, arrange, tend, fail.

Examples:
  • I want to go home. — I want to go home.
  • She decided to stay. — She decided to stay.
  • They promised to help. — They promised to help.
  • He managed to escape. — He managed to escape.
  • We can't afford to buy a new car.
Negation: not + to-infinitive: He decided not to go.

Help — special: can go with to or without: help (to) carry.
⚠️ Contrast with Unit 53: you need to remember which verb requires which form. Some allow both options (see Unit 56/57).
int_u54.exc:
Hope, decide, plan, manage — only infinitive with to.
After help, with to and without are both possible: help (to) do.
mistakes: She decided going. → She decided to go. — after decide — to-inf.
I hope seeing you. → I hope to see you. — after hope — to-inf.
He managed escaping. → He managed to escape. — after manage — to-inf.

int_u55.note: She told me to wait. He asked her not to go.
int_u55.exp:
Unit 55 — construction verb + object + to-infinitive (verb + whom + to do what).

Key verbs: want, ask, tell, expect, allow, advise, encourage, order, remind, warn, invite, persuade, force, teach, help, enable.

Examples:
  • She asked me to wait. — She asked me to wait.
  • They expect us to be on time.
  • He persuaded her to come.
  • I warned you not to go there. (negation: not to)
  • She taught me to drive.
⚠️ Exceptions (bare infinitive — without to):
  • make smb do sth — to force: She made me laugh.
  • let smb do sth — to allow: Let me think.
But in the passive, make requires to: I was made to wait. Let's passive — via allowed: I was allowed to leave.

Negation: I want you not to worry. Don't confuse with want that… (not used!).
int_u55.exc:
After make, let — infinitive without to: She made me wait. Let me go.
But in the passive: I was made to wait.
mistakes: She made me to wait. → She made me wait. — after make — without to.
He let me to go. → He let me go. — after let — without to.
I want that you come. → I want you to come. — construction want smb to do.

int_u56.text: Unit 56 - Infinitive or Gerund: General Rule
int_u56.note: I like to swim / I like swimming - difference?
int_u56.exp:
Unit 56 — verbs that allow both infinitive and gerund.

1. No difference in meaning (begin, start, continue, intend, propose, bother):
  • It started raining / to rain.
2. Difference in nuance (like, love, hate, prefer):
  • like + -ing — enjoy the process: I like swimming (I enjoy it).
  • like + to-inf — consider useful/right: I like to exercise in the morning (habit, consider it important).
  • In AmE — more often to-inf.
3. After would like/love/hate/prefer — only to-infinitive:
  • I'd like to go to Japan.
  • She'd love to meet you.
Examples:
  • I love cooking (process) vs. I'd love to cook for you tonight (specific).
⚠️ With meaning like = find pleasure — gerund; like = think it's good — to-inf. But in modern English this distinction is increasingly less strict.
mistakes: I'd like swimming. → I'd like to swim. / I like swimming. — with would — to-inf.
I started to doing yoga. → I started doing yoga. / I started to do yoga. — both options, not together.
He continued to playing. → He continued playing / to play. — choose one form.

int_u57.text: Unit 57 – Try, need, help: infinitive or gerund
int_u57.note: try doing vs. try to do / need cleaning
int_u57.exp:
Unit 57 — verbs with different meaning depending on form.

remember/forget + to-inf — remember/forget to do (future/upcoming):
  • Remember to lock the door! — Don't forget to lock the door.
remember/forget + -ing — remember/forget that you did (past):
  • I remember locking the door. — I remember locking it.
try + to-inf — try (with effort):
  • I tried to open the window, but it was stuck.
try + -ing — try (experiment):
  • Try turning it off and on again.
stop + to-inf — stop in order to:
  • He stopped to smoke (stopped to have a smoke).
stop + -ing — quit doing:
  • He stopped smoking (quit smoking).
need + to-inf — need to do: I need to rest.
need + -ing — need action (passive meaning): My car needs washing = needs to be washed.
regret + to-inf — regret to inform (formal). regret + -ing — regret having done.
int_u57.exc: Need + -ing has a passive meaning: My shoes need cleaning = need to be cleaned.
mistakes: I remember to lock the door yesterday. → I remember locking the door yesterday. — past action — -ing.
She stopped to smoke (quit). → She stopped smoking. — to quit — -ing.
The car needs to clean. → The car needs cleaning. / needs to be cleaned. — passive meaning.

int_u58.text: Unit 58 – Like / would like: infinitive or gerund
int_u58.note: I like swimming. / I'd like to swim.
int_u58.exp:
Unit 58 — difference between like vs. would like.

like/love/hate/prefer + -ing (or to-inf) — general preference, habit, feeling as a whole:
  • I like swimming. — I like swimming (in general).
  • She hates getting up early.
would like/love/hate/prefer + to-infinitive — specific wish now or in the future (more polite than want):
  • I'd like to go home. — I'd like to go home (now).
  • Would you like to come with us? — Would you like to come with us?
Meaning contrast:
  • I like dancing — I like to dance (in general).
  • I'd like to dance with you — I'd like to dance with you (now/soon).
⚠️ Iron rule: after would — only to-infinitive, never -ing.
Negation: I wouldn't like to live there.
Offer: Would you like + noun/to-inf: Would you like tea? / Would you like to sit down?
int_u58.exc: In American English after like more often to-inf: I like to swim.
mistakes: I would like swimming. → I would like to swim. — after would like — to-inf.
I'd love meeting her. → I'd love to meet her. — with would — to-inf.
Would you like go to the cinema? → Would you like to go to the cinema? — to is needed.

int_u59.note: I prefer reading. I'd rather stay home.
int_u59.exp:
Unit 59 — expressing preferences: prefer, would prefer, would rather.

prefer — general preference:
  • prefer + -ing + to + -ing: I prefer reading to watching TV.
  • prefer + noun + to + noun: I prefer tea to coffee.
  • prefer + to-inf + rather than + V1: I prefer to walk rather than drive.
would prefer + to-infinitive — specific moment:
  • I'd prefer to stay home tonight.
would rather + V1 (without to) — would rather (specific):
  • I'd rather stay home.
  • I'd rather not go. (negation)
  • I'd rather coffee than tea. (with a noun)
would rather + subject + Past Simple — want someone else to:
  • I'd rather you didn't smoke here. — I'd rather you didn't smoke here.
⚠️ Remember: prefer A to B (not than); would rather + V1 without to.
int_u59.exc:
Would rather + subject + past: I'd rather you didn't smoke (I want you not to smoke).
Prefer A to B (not than): I prefer tea to coffee.
mistakes: I'd rather to stay. → I'd rather stay. — after would rather — without to.
I prefer reading than watching TV. → I prefer reading to watching TV. — with prefer — to.
I'd rather you don't tell her. → I'd rather you didn't tell her. — Past Simple after subject.

int_u60.exp:
Unit 60 — gerund after prepositions: hard rule.

Rule: after ANY preposition (in, on, at, of, for, from, about, with, without, before, after, by, to (as a preposition), instead of, despite, besides) — always gerund (-ing), never infinitive.

Typical constructions:
  • adjective + preposition + -ing: good at cooking, afraid of flying, interested in learning, tired of waiting, keen on dancing.
  • verb + preposition + -ing: think about doing, succeed in passing, apologise for being late, insist on paying.
  • noun + preposition + -ing: chance of winning, reason for coming.
Examples:
  • She's good at solving problems.
  • Thanks for helping me.
  • He left without saying a word.
  • By practising daily, she improved.
⚠️ Special attention — to: when to is part of the infinitive (want to go), V1 follows. When to is a preposition (look forward to, be used to, object to, get used to), gerund follows: I look forward to seeing you.
int_u60.exc: After all prepositions (in, on, at, of, for, about, before, after, without, by) — only gerund, not infinitive.
mistakes: I'm interested in to learn French. → I'm interested in learning French. — after a preposition — -ing.
Thanks for to help me. → Thanks for helping me. — after for — gerund.
He left without to say goodbye. → He left without saying goodbye. — after without — -ing.

int_u61.note: I'm used to waking up early.
int_u61.exp:
Unit 61 — the construction be/get used to + -ing describes a habit formed through practice: the action no longer feels difficult or strange.

Formula: be/get used to + noun / pronoun / -ing.
  • I'm used to waking up early.
  • She isn't used to the cold weather.
  • He's getting used to living alone.

It is important to distinguish from used to + infinitive (a past regular habit that no longer exists): I used to smoke = I smoked in the past. But I'm used to smoking = I am accustomed to being around smoking. In the first case used is a past-tense verb; in the second, an adjective after be. Signals: get used to = the process of becoming accustomed; be used to = the state of being accustomed.
int_u61.exc: The forms be/get used to require -ing, not an infinitive. Used to do (without be) is a different tense (past habit).
mistakes: I'm used to wake up early. → I'm used to waking up early. — after be used to always a gerund.
I used to living here. → I'm used to living here. — a present habit requires be.
He's getting used to drive on the left. → getting used to driving — after to here a gerund.

int_u62.note: look forward to / feel like / succeed in
int_u62.exp:
Unit 62 — after a number of verbs with prepositions a gerund always follows, because to or another preposition here is not an infinitive particle but a preposition.

Groups of expressions:
  • look forward to, object to, be/get used to — deceptive to-preposition: I look forward to meeting you.
  • feel like, give up, take up, carry on, go on, put off, keep on, succeed in: I don't feel like going out.
  • She succeeded in passing the exam.

Check: if you can substitute a noun (look forward to the trip), then to is a preposition, and -ing is needed. Memorise the expression as a whole together with the preposition.
int_u62.exc: To in look forward to / object to / be used to is a preposition, not an infinitive particle. Therefore -ing follows.
mistakes: I look forward to see you. → I look forward to seeing you. — to here is a preposition.
She gave up to smoke. → gave up smoking — give up requires a gerund.
I don't feel like to go out. → feel like going out — after feel like only -ing.

int_u63.note: insist on / accuse of / apologise for
int_u63.exp:
Unit 63 — continuation of the topic: verb + preposition + object + gerund. The preposition is attached to the verb, and the action is expressed through -ing.

Common formulas:
  • accuse somebody of + -ing: They accused him of stealing.
  • apologise for + -ing: She apologised for being late.
  • insist on + -ing: I insist on paying.
  • prevent somebody from + -ing: Rain prevented us from going out.

Also: thank for, blame for, congratulate on, suspect of, rely on, concentrate on, dream of. These verb+preposition pairs should be learned as a single unit, so you automatically use a gerund.
int_u63.exc: Memorise the preposition together with the verb as a single block: accuse of, apologise for, insist on, prevent from.
mistakes: She accused me to steal. → accused me of stealing — accuse of + -ing.
He apologised for be late. → apologised for being late — after a preposition a gerund.
They prevented him to leave. → prevented him from leaving — the preposition from + gerund is needed.

int_u64.text: Unit 64 - Infinitive to indicate purpose (so that)
int_u64.note: I came to help. / She left so as not to argue.
int_u64.exp:
Unit 64 — ways of expressing the purpose of an action: why something is done.

Simple purpose (one subject): to-infinitive or in order to / so as to (formal).
  • I called to tell you the news.
  • She left early in order to catch the train.
Negative purpose: only so as not to / in order not to, not not to on its own.
  • She whispered so as not to wake the baby.
Different subjects: so that + subject + can/could/will/would.
  • I'll lend you my notes so that you can study.

For + -ing denotes the function of a thing: a knife for cutting bread, not the purpose of a person's action.
int_u64.exc: Negative purpose — only so as not to / in order not to, not not to alone.
mistakes: I called for tell you. → called to tell you — purpose uses a to-infinitive, not for.
She whispered not to wake the baby. → so as not to wake — negative purpose requires so as/in order.
He left so that catch the train. → so that he could catch — after so that a subject and a modal verb are needed.

int_u65.note: It's nice to meet you. She's easy to talk to.
int_u65.exp:
Unit 65 — infinitive after adjectives in evaluative constructions.

Formulas:
  • It is + adj + to-infinitive: It's difficult to understand him.
  • It is + adj + of somebody + to-inf (about character): It was kind of you to help.
  • Subject + be + adj + to-inf (property of the object): She's easy to talk to.
  • After first, next, last, only: She was the first to arrive.

Feature of easy/hard/impossible: the object of the action is moved into the subject, the preposition is kept at the end (talk to). Kind/nice/generous require of somebody, not for/from.
int_u65.exc: After nice/kind/generous of somebody — an infinitive: It was kind of you to help. After easy/hard + to the object becomes the subject: She's easy to talk to.
mistakes: It's difficult understanding. → It's difficult to understand. — after an adjective an infinitive, not a gerund.
She's easy to talk. → easy to talk to — the preposition cannot be omitted.
It was nice from you to come. → nice of you to come — of is used, not from.

int_u66.text: Unit 66 – Afraid / interested / sorry: infinitive or gerund
int_u66.note: afraid of doing / afraid to do
int_u66.exp:
Unit 66 — adjectives that combine with both a gerund and an infinitive, but change nuance.

Afraid:
  • afraid of + -ing = general phobia, fear that something may happen: She's afraid of flying.
  • afraid to + inf = does not do deliberately because of fear: She was afraid to tell him the truth.
Interested: interested in + -ing (interest in an activity), interested to hear/know (a specific reaction): I was interested to hear the news.
Sorry: sorry for/about + -ing (about a past action), sorry to do (regret at the moment of speaking): Sorry to disturb you. vs. Sorry for disturbing you yesterday.

The choice depends on whether we are speaking about a reaction in the moment or about a continuing feeling/past event.
int_u66.exc: Sorry about/for + -ing — about a past action; sorry to do — regret about the present/future.
mistakes: I'm afraid to fly. (about a general fear) → I'm afraid of flying. — general phobia requires of + -ing.
I'm interested to learn French. (about a hobby) → interested in learning — interest in an activity: in + -ing.
Sorry to wake you up yesterday. → Sorry for waking you up — about a past action: for + -ing.

int_u67.text: Unit 67 — See somebody do or do
int_u67.note: I saw him fall. / I saw him falling.
int_u67.exp:
Unit 67 — after verbs of perception the object combines with a bare infinitive or with -ing.

Verbs: see, hear, watch, feel, notice, listen to.
  • bare infinitive — the action as a whole, from beginning to end: I saw him cross the road.
  • -ing — action in progress, partial: I saw him crossing the road.
  • I heard her sing the whole song. vs. I heard her singing in the shower.
Make/let/have + object + bare infinitive: She made me wait. Let me go. I had him fix the car.
In the passive with make: to returns: He was made to wait.

Compare: help somebody (to) do allows both variants.
int_u67.exc: After make/let — infinitive without to. In the passive: He was made to wait — to returns.
mistakes: I saw him to cross the road. → saw him cross — after verbs of perception infinitive without to.
She made me to wait. → made me wait — after make bare infinitive.
I heard him singing (the whole song) → heard him sing — a completed action — bare infinitive.

int_u68.note: Feeling tired, I went to bed.
int_u68.exp:
Unit 68 — participial phrases with -ing for the compact expression of simultaneous or sequential actions.

Types:
  • Simultaneous action: Feeling tired, I went to bed.
  • Parallel: She walked along the road singing to herself.
  • Cause: Being ill, he stayed at home.
  • Preceding action — Having + past participle: Having finished lunch, we left.

Main rule: the subject of the participle must coincide with the subject of the main clause, otherwise it becomes nonsense (a dangling participle). The style is more bookish; in speech a full subordinate clause with when/because/after is more common.
int_u68.exc: The subject of a participial phrase must coincide with the subject of the main clause, otherwise a 'dangling participle' arises.
mistakes: Walking in the park, the flowers were beautiful. → Walking in the park, I saw beautiful flowers. — the flowers weren't walking; a matching subject is needed.
Having finishing, she left. → Having finished, she left. — after having — past participle.
Being tired, so I went to bed. → Being tired, I went to bed. — after a participial phrase so is not needed.

int_u69.exp:
Unit 69 — the basic distinction between countable and uncountable nouns.

Countable: have a plural, combine with a/an, one, two, many, few: a book → two books.
Uncountable: no plural, no a/an, combine with some, much, little, a piece of: water, music, money.
Typical uncountables that are countable in Russian: advice, information, news, furniture, luggage, baggage, work, progress, research, traffic, weather, bread, money.
  • I need some advice.
  • The news is bad. (verb in singular)
  • She has a lot of luggage.

For individual units: a piece of advice, a bit of news, an item of furniture.
int_u69.exc: Uncountable in English but countable in Russian: advice, information, news, furniture, luggage, progress, research. You cannot say an advice, advices.
mistakes: I need an advice. → some advice / a piece of advice — advice is uncountable.
The news are bad. → The news is bad. — news requires a singular verb.
I bought new furnitures. → new furniture — furniture has no plural.

int_u70.note: hair / a hair / paper / a paper
int_u70.exp:
Unit 70 — nouns that change meaning depending on countability.

Examples of pairs:
