  • hair (hair in general) vs. a hair (one hair): She has long hair. There's a hair in my soup.
  • paper (paper) vs. a paper (newspaper, article, paper/essay): I need some paper. I read the paper.
  • experience (experience) vs. an experience (an event): She has a lot of experience. It was a strange experience.
  • time (time) vs. times (occasions): I have no time. I've been there three times.
  • room (space) vs. a room (a room): There isn't much room. Book a room.

Also: light, work/a work of art, glass, iron, coffee (a coffee = a cup). Context and the presence of an article indicate the meaning.
int_u70.exc: One word — two meanings: experience (experience, uncount.) vs. an experience (an event, count.); time (time, uncount.) vs. times (occasions, count.).
mistakes: Can you give me a paper to write on? → some paper / a sheet of paper — a paper = a newspaper.
I have a long hair. → long hair — hair in general is uncountable.
I had many experiences in my job. (about skills) → a lot of experience — work experience is uncountable.

int_u71.text: Unit 71 – Article a/an and the word some
int_u71.note: a book / some books / some milk
int_u71.exp:
Unit 71 — some acts as an 'article' for plural countables and for uncountables, when a/an does not fit.

Parallel:
  • sing.: a book — pl.: some books
  • uncount.: some milk, some information
Some vs. any: some — in statements and polite offers/requests; any — in questions and negatives.
  • I bought some apples.
  • Do you have any questions?
  • Would you like some tea? (offer = some)
  • Could I have some water, please? (expecting agreement)

Any in statements means 'any whatever': Take any book you like. The quantity some is indefinite but real.
int_u71.exc: In polite offers and requests some is used, not any: Would you like some tea? Could I have some water?
mistakes: I have some questions? (question) → Do you have any questions? — in a neutral question any.
Would you like any coffee? → some coffee — in an offer some.
I don't have some money. → don't have any money — in a negative any.

int_u72.exp:
Unit 72 — transition from a/an to the: how an object's familiarity is determined.

First mention (new information) — a/an. Repeated mention or known from context — the.
  • I saw a film yesterday. The film was great.
  • Can you shut the door, please? — The door is known from the situation.
Immediately the:
  • unique objects: the sun, the moon, the world, the Internet;
  • superlative: the best, the tallest;
  • with a specifying relative clause: the man who called.

A/an is also used for classification: She's a doctor. It's a nice day. Logic: a = one of many; the = this particular one, known to both interlocutors.
int_u72.exc: Unique objects of their kind immediately take the: the sun, the moon, the sky, the Internet. First mention does not override this rule.
mistakes: I saw the film yesterday. A film was great. → The film was great. — on repetition — the.
Sun is shining. → The sun is shining. — a unique object requires the.
She's a best student in class. → the best student — with the superlative the.

int_u73.note: the radio / the only / the same
int_u73.exp:
Unit 73 — a systematic look at cases of obligatory the.

With the:
  • unique objects: the Earth, the sky, the environment;
  • after same, only, first, next, last, right, wrong, main: the same problem, the only solution;
  • an adjective as a group of people: the rich, the poor, the elderly, the unemployed;
  • the the more… the more construction: The more you practise, the better you get.
  • musical instruments: play the piano;
  • radio, cinema: listen to the radio, go to the cinema.
Without the (general sense): go to bed, go to school, go to work, go to church, go home, at home, have breakfast/lunch/dinner.

Signal: if we are speaking about the typical function of a place — without an article; if about the building as an object — with the: go to the school to meet the teacher.
int_u73.exc: Without the: go to bed, go to school, go to church, go to work, go home — in the general sense of 'studying/sleeping/praying'.
mistakes: She has same problem. → the same problem — with same always the.
He's only person I trust. → the only person — with only always the.
Rich should help poor. → The rich should help the poor. — groups of people via the + adj.

int_u74.note: in the morning / on the way / at the same time
int_u74.exp:
Unit 74 — fixed collocations with and without the article the, which should be memorised as a whole.

With the:
  • in the morning / afternoon / evening: I work out in the morning.
  • on the way: I met him on the way home.
  • at the same time: They arrived at the same time.
  • in the end = in the end, ultimately: In the end, we decided to stay.
  • by the way: By the way, did you call him?
Without the article: at night, at lunchtime, at weekends, by chance, by mistake, on foot, on holiday, on time, at work, at home.
Contrast: in the end (finally) vs. at the end of (at the end of something): At the end of the film I cried.

These expressions are fixed; deviations change the meaning or sound wrong.
int_u74.exc: At night — without the, but in the morning/afternoon/evening — with the.
mistakes: In morning I drink coffee. → In the morning — a fixed collocation with the.
At the night we sleep. → At night — without the article.
At the end of the day I was tired. (= finally) → In the end — in the end = finally.

int_u75.text: Unit 75 - Article the (lesson 3): general vs. specific
int_u75.note: I like music. / I like the music in this café.
int_u75.exp:
Unit 75 — the choice between the zero article (generalisation) and the (specifics).

Without an article — when we speak about a concept, substance or group in general:
  • Music is a universal language.
  • I love coffee.
  • Children need love.
With the — when we mean a specific instance, known from context:
  • The music in this café is great.
  • The coffee I had this morning was awful.
  • The children in this class are smart.

Abstract concepts (life, nature, society, history, education) — without an article in the general sense: Life is short. But: The life of a writer is lonely. — specification via of.
int_u75.exc: Life, nature, society, history in the general sense — without an article. But the life of a writer — a specific life.
mistakes: I like the music. (in general) → I like music. — generalisation without an article.
<s>Life is short.</s> (general) — correct. <s>The life is short</s> — incorrect.
<s>Children like playing.</s> (all children) — correct; <s>The children like playing</s> only about specific ones.

int_u76.text: Unit 76 – The article the (lesson 4): with adjectives and nationalities
int_u76.note: the French / the elderly / the rich
int_u76.exp:
Unit 76 — the before adjectives denoting social and national groups.

The + adjective = a group of people (plural):
  • the rich, the poor, the young, the old, the elderly, the unemployed, the homeless, the sick, the blind, the deaf, the disabled, the dead, the injured.
  • The rich get richer.
  • The government should help the elderly.
Nationalities ending in -sh, -ch, -ese with the for the people as a whole: the French, the English, the Chinese, the Japanese, the British, the Dutch.
  • The French are proud of their cuisine. (verb in plural)
  • For one person — a Frenchman / a French person.

These nouns do NOT take -s (you cannot say the Frenches). The verb is always plural.
int_u76.exc: The verb with the + adj is always plural: The poor are…, not is.
mistakes: The French is famous for wine. → The French are famous… — verb in plural.
The poor needs help. → The poor need help. — plural agreement.
A French person — correct for one, but A French are polite incorrect — for a group you need the French.

int_u77.text: Unit 77 - The article the (lesson 5): with names and place names
int_u77.exp:
Unit 77 — articles with geographical names and proper names.

Without the:
  • continents, countries, cities, states, islands (one): Europe, Japan, Paris, Texas, Sicily;
  • individual mountains and lakes: Mount Everest, Lake Baikal;
  • people's names: Anna, Mr Smith.
With the:
  • rivers, oceans, seas, canals: the Nile, the Atlantic, the Black Sea, the Suez Canal;
  • mountain ranges, archipelagos: the Alps, the Andes, the Bahamas;
  • countries in the plural or with Republic, Kingdom, States, Union: the Netherlands, the UK, the USA, the Russian Federation;
  • regions and cardinal points: the Middle East, the north of France;
  • families: the Smiths (the Smith family).

Deserts and most gulfs — with the: the Sahara, the Gulf of Mexico.
int_u77.exc: Lakes without the: Lake Baikal, Lake Como. Individual mountains without the: Mount Everest. But mountain ranges — the Alps, the Andes.
mistakes: The Paris is beautiful. → Paris is beautiful. — cities without the.
Mount the Everest → Mount Everest — an individual mountain without the.
Nile is long. → The Nile is long. — rivers with the.

int_u78.text: Unit 78 – Article the (lesson 6): streets, establishments, organizations
int_u78.exp:
Unit 78 — articles with names of streets, buildings, organisations.

Without the:
  • streets, squares, parks: Oxford Street, Times Square, Central Park;
  • airports, stations: Heathrow Airport, Victoria Station;
  • universities without of: Harvard, Cambridge, Oxford;
  • shops and restaurants by owner's name: McDonald's, Harrods.
With the:
  • museums, theatres, cinemas: the British Museum, the Odeon;
  • hotels, restaurants: the Hilton, the Ritz;
  • newspapers, organisations: the Times, the BBC, the EU, the UN;
  • names with of: the University of Oxford, the Tower of London, the Bank of England;
  • historical buildings: the White House, the Kremlin, the Vatican.

The 'of' rule: the X of Y construction almost always requires the.
int_u78.exc: Exception: the White House, the Kremlin, the Vatican. With of almost always the: the University of Oxford.
mistakes: I study at the Oxford. → at Oxford — a university without of goes without the.
Let's meet at Hilton. → at the Hilton — hotels with the.
The Oxford Street is busy. → Oxford Street is busy. — streets without an article.

int_u79.text: Unit 79 - Unit. and many more number of nouns
int_u79.exp:
Unit 79 — the plural of nouns and agreement with the verb.

Regular forms: +s, +es (after -s/-sh/-ch/-x), -y→-ies.
Irregular: man→men, woman→women, child→children, foot→feet, tooth→teeth, mouse→mice, person→people.
Identical singular/plural forms: sheep, fish, deer, aircraft, species, series.
Nouns always plural (plural verb): police, people, cattle, clothes, scissors, trousers, jeans, glasses.
  • The police are investigating.
  • My trousers are new.
Look plural, but are singular: news, mathematics, physics, economics, athletics, politics: The news is bad. Physics is difficult.

Person in the plural = people; persons only in formal/legal contexts.
int_u79.exc: Person usually becomes people in the plural. Persons only in an official/legal context.
mistakes: The police is coming. → The police are coming. — police is always plural.
Three sheeps → three sheep — the form is the same for singular and plural.
Mathematics are difficult. → Mathematics is difficult. — sciences ending in -ics are usually singular.

int_u80.text: Unit 80 – Two nouns next to each other (word formation)
int_u80.note: a car door / a music teacher / a bus stop
int_u80.exp:
Unit 80 — the 'noun + noun' construction: the first specifies the type or function of the second.

Formula: noun1 + noun2, where noun1 plays the role of an adjective.
  • a car door — a car door (a type of door);
  • a bus stop;
  • a music teacher;
  • a kitchen table;
  • a credit card.
The first noun is singular, even if plural in meaning: a shoe shop (not shoes shop), a book shop.
Exceptions (take the plural): a sports car, a clothes shop, a sales manager, a news report, a customs officer.
Comparison with of: a car door (a type in general) vs. the door of the car (a specific door of a specific car).

Compound adjectives with a numeral: a three-year-old boy, a ten-minute walk — without -s.
int_u80.exc: Exceptions — the first word is plural in meaning: a sports car, a clothes shop, a sales manager.
mistakes: A books shop → a bookshop — the first noun is singular.
A three-years-old boy → a three-year-old boy — without s in a compound adjective.
The door of the car (just 'car door') → a car door — for a type of object the compound form is preferred.

int_u81.note: Tom's car / the children's room / yesterday's news
int_u81.exp:
Unit 81 — the possessive case with apostrophe 's.

Forms:
  • singular: Tom's car, the girl's bag;
  • regular plural (already in -s): only an apostrophe — the boys' room, my parents' house;
  • irregular plural: 's — the children's toys, the men's room.
When 's, and when of:
  • 's — with people, animals, countries, organisations: Mary's book, the cat's tail, Russia's economy, the company's policy;
  • of — with things: the top of the page, the end of the film.
Time: yesterday's news, today's paper, this week's meeting.
Distance/duration: a day's journey, two weeks' holiday.

Double possessive for one of several: a friend of my brother's = one of my brother's friends.
int_u81.exc: Double apostrophe: plural in -s takes only an apostrophe: the boys' room, while irregular plural takes 's: the children's room.
mistakes: The car of Tom → Tom's car — with people's names 's is used.
The childrens' room → the children's room — children is an irregular plural.
My parent's house (about both parents) → my parents' house — plural possessive.

int_u82.note: I hurt myself. She enjoyed herself.
int_u82.exp:
Unit 82 — reflexive pronouns, when subject and object coincide.

Forms: myself, yourself, himself, herself, itself, ourselves, yourselves, themselves.
  • I cut myself while cooking.
  • She enjoyed herself at the party.
For emphasis — 'personally':
  • I did it myself.
  • The president himself opened the ceremony.
By + reflexive = alone, without help: I live by myself.
Verbs WITHOUT reflexive (differs from Russian!): wash, shave, dress, feel, concentrate, relax, meet.
  • I washed and got dressed. (not washed myself)
  • We met at 5.

Each other ≠ ourselves: They looked at each other (at each other), not at themselves (each at themselves).
int_u82.exc: The verbs wash, shave, dress, feel, concentrate, relax, meet are usually WITHOUT a reflexive: I washed and got dressed, not washed myself.
mistakes: We met ourselves at 5. → We met at 5. — meet with each other does not require ourselves.
They helped themself. → themselves — plural self → selves.
<s>I did it by myself.</s> (= alone, without help) — correct; <s>I did it myself</s> (personally). The nuance is confused.

int_u83.note: Is this yours? my own room
int_u83.exp:
Unit 83 — absolute possessive pronouns and the expression own.

Absolute forms (without a noun): mine, yours, his, hers, ours, theirs.
  • Is this your book? — Yes, it's mine.
  • Their house is bigger than ours.
A friend of mine — one of my friends (double possessive): not a friend of me.
My own + noun emphasises that it is precisely mine, not someone else's:
  • I want my own room.
  • She has her own business.
On my own = by myself = alone, independently:
  • She lives on her own.
  • I did it on my own.

After own there is no preposition of: my own of car → my own car.
int_u83.exc: After own a noun always follows: my own car. You cannot say my own as a pronoun without context.
mistakes: Is this book your? → Is this book yours? — a standalone pronoun is yours.
A friend of me → a friend of mine — after of the absolute variant is needed.
I have my own of car. → my own car — after own there is no of.

int_u84.note: There is a problem. It's raining.
int_u84.exp:
Unit 84 — the formal subjects there and it.

There is / there are — existence, presence:
  • There is a problem with the car.
  • There are many reasons.
  • There was an accident.
It — for time, weather, distance, general evaluations:
  • It's cold today.
  • It's 10 o'clock.
  • It's 5 km to the station.
  • It's nice to see you.
Contrast: we report existence — there; we characterise a situation — it.
Idioms: there's no point, there's no need; it takes, it seems, it looks like.

Agreement: there is + sing./uncount.; there are + pl.
int_u84.exc: It for weather, time, distance; there — for existence. Mixing them changes the meaning.
mistakes: It is a problem with the engine. (meaning existence) → There is a problem…
There is cold today. → It is cold today. — weather through it.
It has many people here. → There are many people here. — existence through there.

int_u85.note: some milk / any questions / anyone / anything
int_u85.exp:
Unit 85 — some, any and their derivatives at the Intermediate level.

Main distribution:
  • some — statements, polite offers/requests;
  • any — questions, negatives, conditionals;
  • any in a statement = 'any': Take any seat.
Derivatives:
  • people: someone/somebody, anyone/anybody, no one/nobody, everyone/everybody;
  • things: something, anything, nothing, everything;
  • places: somewhere, anywhere, nowhere, everywhere.
Examples:
  • Is there anyone at home?
  • I didn't see anybody.
  • You can sit anywhere.

Agreement: singular derivatives — Everybody is here. Somebody has taken my bag. No double negatives allowed: I don't know anything, not nothing.
int_u85.exc: Any in statements = 'any': Take any book you want. This is not a negation.
mistakes: I have any questions. (statement) → some questions — in a statement some.
Somebody know the answer? → Does anybody know…? — in a general question anybody.
There isn't nobody here. → There isn't anybody / There is nobody here. — without double negation.

int_u86.text: Unit 86 - Nobody, no-one, nothing, nowhere, none
int_u86.exp:
Unit 86 — negative pronouns with no-.

Forms: nobody / no one, nothing, nowhere, none.
Main rule: the verb is always affirmative, since the negation is already in the pronoun.
  • Nobody came to the party.
  • Nothing happened.
  • There was nowhere to sit.
No + noun vs. none:
  • no + noun: I have no money. No cars were parked here.
  • none without a noun (alone or with of): How much? — None. None of us knew.
Double negation is forbidden: Nobody didn't come. → Nobody came or Anybody didn't come (but the first is better).

After nobody/nothing further any- is used: Nobody said anything.
int_u86.exc: No + noun (no money); none — without a noun (None of us knew). The verb is affirmative.
mistakes: Nobody didn't come. → Nobody came. — with nobody the verb is affirmative.
How much? No. → None. — a standalone answer — none.
Nobody said nothing. → Nobody said anything. — after a negative any-.

int_u87.note: much water / many people / little time / few friends
int_u87.exp:
Unit 87 — quantifiers much, many, little, few and the nuance of a little / a few.

Distribution by countability:
  • many, few, a few — with plural countables;
  • much, little, a little — with uncountables.
Key nuance — tone:
  • little/few (without a) = little, almost none (negative tone): I have little time. Few people came.
  • a little / a few = some, but enough (positive): I have a little time. A few friends helped.
Much in statements sounds formal; in speech a lot of / lots of is preferred: I have a lot of money. In questions and negatives much is fine: Do you have much time? Not much.

Compare: I have a few friends (some, good) vs. I have few friends (few, sad).
int_u87.exc: Much in statements sounds formal; in colloquial speech a lot of is more common: I have a lot of money, not much money.
mistakes: I have many money. → much money / a lot of money — money is uncountable.
A few water → a little water — for uncountables — little/a little.
I have little friends. (neutral, 'some') → a few friends — without a the tone is 'almost none'.

int_u88.note: most of us / some of the students / none of them
int_u88.exp:
Unit 88 — the 'quantifier + of' construction before determined nouns and pronouns.

With of before the, these, those, my, your, us, them:
  • most of the students, some of these books, all of my friends, none of us, half of them, both of them, each of us.
Without of — generalisation, without a determiner:
  • most people, some students, all children, many cars.
Contrast:
  • Most people like music. — Most people (in general).
  • Most of the people at the party were young. — Most of those people.
Half: half of the cake or half the cake are allowed; half + number: half an hour.
All/both before a noun may omit of: all (of) my friends, both (of) the books.

Before a personal pronoun of is obligatory: all of us, not all us.
int_u88.exc: Without of: most people, some students (generalisation). With of: most of the people, some of the students (a specific group).
mistakes: Most of people like music. → Most people like music. — generalisation without of the.
Some the students → some of the students — before the of is obligatory.
Half cake → half of the cake / half the cake — before a specific group.

int_u89.note: Both of them / Either road / Neither is correct.
int_u89.exp:
Unit 89 — pronouns for denoting two objects.

Both — affirmative:
  • Both sisters are tall.
  • Both of them came. / They both came.
Either (any of two; either... or):
  • Either road leads to town.
  • You can take either.
Neither (neither one nor the other):
  • Neither answer is correct.
  • Neither of them came.
Paired conjunctions:
  • both… and: Both Tom and Anna were there.
  • either… or: You can have either tea or coffee.
  • neither… nor: Neither Tom nor Anna came.
Agreement: either/neither formally require singular (Neither of them knows), in speech plural is allowed.

Both — only for two; for three and more — all.
int_u89.exc: Both — only for two. For three and more — all. After neither the verb is formally singular, in speech plural.
mistakes: Both of answers are correct. → Both answers / Both of the answers — of requires the/pronoun.
Either answers are right. → Either answer is right. — either requires singular.
Neither of them don't know. → Neither of them knows. — without double negation.

int_u90.note: all day / the whole day / every day
int_u90.exp:
Unit 90 — distinguishing all, whole, every.

All + noun (all):
  • all day, all the time, all students;
  • with a determiner: all my friends, all the books.
Whole (entire, completely): the whole day = all the day. Usually with the/my/a:
  • She ate the whole cake.
  • a whole week.
Every — each individually, with singular:
  • every day, every student, every time;
  • Every student has a book. (verb in singular)
Contrast: all day (all day, continuously) vs. every day (each day, regularly).
Everybody/everyone/everything/everywhere — singular verb: Everybody is ready.

Whole does not combine with uncountables: not the whole water → all the water.
int_u90.exc: After every/everybody/everything — singular verb: Everybody is ready, not are.
mistakes: All day and every day are confused: all day = all day, every day = each day.
All the people is here. → All the people are here. — people is plural.
Everybody have a phone. → Everybody has a phone. — after everybody — has.

int_u91.exp:
Unit 91 — distinguishing each and every.

Each — an individual view of each member (often a small group):
  • Each student received a certificate.
  • She gave each of us a present.
  • They each have a key. — Can stand after the subject.
Every — generalisation, all without exception:
  • Every student passed the exam.
  • Every day I go jogging.
Key differences:
  • each — for two and more; every — only for three and more;
  • each of us/them/the + noun, but every does not combine with of — you must say each of or every one of;
  • every has a fixed word order: every + noun; each is more flexible: each student, each of the students, the students each.

With two objects — only each, not every.
int_u91.exc: Every is not used for two objects — only each or both.
mistakes: Every of students → Each of the students / Every student — every without of.
Every two students have a book. (about two) → Each of the two students has a book.
They every have a key. → They each have a key. — after the subject only each.

int_u92.note: the man who / the book that / the place where
int_u92.exp:
Unit 92 — relative clauses, which specify a noun.

Main conjunctions:
  • who — people (subject/object): the man who called;
  • that — people and things: the man that called, the book that I read;
  • which — things: the book which I read;
  • where — places: the town where I was born;
  • when — time: the day when we met.
Position: immediately after the noun being defined.
Omission of the conjunction: when the conjunction is an object in the clause, it can be omitted: the book (that/which) I read. If the conjunction is the subject, it cannot be omitted: the man who called (you cannot say the man called).
Preposition at the end: the girl (who) I spoke to. Formal style: to whom I spoke.

The Russian 'который' is covered by several conjunctions at once depending on context.
int_u92.exc: A conjunction can be omitted only if it is an object in the clause: the book (that) I read. If it is the subject — it cannot be omitted: the man who called.
mistakes: The man which lives here → the man who/that lives here — for people — who/that.
The book who I read → the book which/that I read — for things — which/that.
The man lives here is my uncle. → The man who lives here is my uncle. — you cannot omit the subject.

int_u93.text: Unit 93 – Relative Clauses with which, that, who
int_u93.note: people who / things which / using that
int_u93.exp:
Unit 93 — in-depth discussion of who, which, that in relative clauses.

Who — people, as subject or object of the clause: the doctor who treats me, the girl who I met.
Which — for things and animals: the computer which broke down.
That — universal for people and things, but only in defining clauses (without commas): the man that called, the book that I bought.
Whom — the object form of who, formal: the person whom I met. In speech replaced by who or omitted.
Omission rule:
  • can be omitted if the conjunction is an object: the film (that) I saw;
  • cannot be omitted if the conjunction is the subject: the film that was on TV.
A pronoun cannot be repeated in the clause: the man who I saw him → the man who I saw.

That is not allowed in non-defining clauses: My brother, who lives in Moscow, … — only who.
int_u93.exc: That cannot be used in non-defining clauses with commas.
mistakes: My brother, that lives in Moscow, is a doctor. → My brother, who lives in Moscow… — in non-defining that is not allowed.
The car who I bought → the car which/that I bought — not who for objects.
The man who I saw him yesterday → the man who I saw yesterday — without repeating the pronoun him.

int_u94.text: Unit 94 – Relative Clauses with whose, whom, where
int_u94.note: the man whose car / the woman whom I met
int_u94.exp:
Unit 94 — special relative pronouns: whose, whom, where.

Whose (whose) — possession, for people and things:
  • the man whose car was stolen;
  • the book whose cover is torn.
Whom — the object form of who, formal style, especially after prepositions:
  • the colleague whom I mentioned;
  • the person to whom I wrote (formal) = the person (who) I wrote to (informal).
Where — for places:
  • the hotel where we stayed;
  • equivalent: the hotel at which we stayed / the hotel (that) we stayed at.
When / why — time/reason: the day when we met, the reason why she left.
Preposition + whom/which in formal writing: the company for which she works. In speech: the company (that) she works for.

Don't confuse whose with who's (= who is/has).
int_u94.exc: Whose — for people and things: the book whose cover is torn. Whom is hardly ever used in speech.
mistakes: The man who's car is red → the man whose car is red — who's = who is.
The company who I work for (formally) → the company for which I work — in formal style the preposition is moved to the front.
The place where I went there → the place where I went — without repeating there.

int_u95.note: My sister who lives in Paris / My sister, who lives in Paris
int_u95.exp:
Unit 95 — distinguishing defining and non-defining clauses.

Defining — specifies which exactly; no commas; the information is essential:
  • The man who called was a doctor. — The one who called.
  • Can use that; an object conjunction can be omitted.
Non-defining — gives additional information; commas are obligatory; can be deleted without loss of meaning:
  • My father, who is 60, runs marathons. — I have one father, a fact about him.
  • Only who/which/whose/where/when, not that;
  • Cannot omit the conjunction.
Key test: if the information specifies which one out of several — defining (no commas); if it gives an incidental fact about a known thing — non-defining (commas).

Pronunciation: a non-defining clause is read with pauses in place of the commas.
int_u95.exc: In non-defining clauses commas are obligatory, that is forbidden, and the conjunction cannot be omitted.
mistakes: My father who is 60 runs marathons. (one father) → My father, who is 60, runs marathons. — commas are needed.
My sister, that lives in Paris → My sister, who lives in Paris — that is not allowed.
My car which is new is red. (one car) → My car, which is new, is red. — additional information = commas.

int_u96.note: She passed the exam, which surprised everyone.
int_u96.exp:
Unit 96 — advanced work with non-defining clauses.

Which can refer to the whole preceding sentence, not just to a single noun:
  • She passed the exam, which surprised everyone. — The fact of passing surprised.
  • He was late, which annoyed me.
Rules of non-defining:
  • always with commas;
  • only who, which, whose, where, when; NOT that;
  • the conjunction cannot be omitted.
Informational role: a non-defining clause adds a comment, reaction, addition. Often used with which to express the speaker's attitude.
  • My car, which I bought last year, broke down. — An additional fact about the car.
  • He refused to help, which made me angry. — A comment on his action.

You cannot use what instead of which to refer to a sentence.
int_u96.exc: Which in non-defining clauses can refer to the whole thought: He was late, which annoyed me.
mistakes: She got the job, that was great. → which was great — in non-defining only which.
He was late, what annoyed me. → which annoyed me — what is not allowed here.
She passed the exam which surprised everyone (no comma) → …exam, which surprised everyone — a comma before a non-defining.

int_u97.note: the woman talking to Tom / the boy injured
int_u97.exp:
Unit 97 — participial phrases -ing and -ed as compact replacements for relative clauses.

-ing (active meaning) replaces a clause in the active voice with Present/Past Continuous or with the meaning of an action in progress:
  • the man sitting by the window = the man who is sitting by the window;
  • Anyone wanting to leave should do it now. = anyone who wants to leave.
-ed (passive meaning) replaces passive clauses:
  • the car stolen last week = the car that was stolen;
  • The money found in the street was returned. = which was found.
Irregular verbs keep the third form: stolen, broken, written, taken, made, seen.
Style: more concise, more frequent in written and formal speech, in newspaper headlines.

A participial phrase should modify the nearest noun, otherwise ambiguity arises.
int_u97.exc: -ing-phrase replaces an active clause, -ed-phrase — a passive one. Irregular verbs: stolen, broken, written.
mistakes: The man sat by the window (participle) → the man sitting by the window — -ing is needed.
The car stealing last week → the car stolen last week — passive requires past participle.
The boy injuring → the boy injured — passive meaning: -ed/III form.

int_u98.exp:
Unit 98 — pairs of adjectives with the suffixes -ing and -ed.

-ing — describes the source of the sensation (what causes the emotion):
  • The film is boring.
  • The news was shocking.
-ed — describes the one who experiences the sensation:
  • I'm bored.
  • She was shocked by the news.
Common pairs: interesting/interested, exciting/excited, tiring/tired, surprising/surprised, amazing/amazed, annoying/annoyed, confusing/confused, embarrassing/embarrassed, disappointing/disappointed, frightening/frightened.
Memory aid: -ed is me (I feel), -ing is it (it causes).
  • The teacher is interesting; the students are interested.

After -ed typically follow in/by/with/at/about: interested in, surprised by, bored with, shocked at.
int_u98.exc: The pairs work for almost all emotional verbs: surprising/surprised, shocking/shocked, amazing/amazed, annoying/annoyed.
mistakes: I'm very boring. → I'm very bored. — if you feel it yourself — -ed.
The film was bored. → The film was boring. — source of emotion — -ing.
I'm interesting in history. → I'm interested in history. — experience — -ed.

int_u99.note: a beautiful big old Italian stone house
int_u99.exp:
Unit 99 — the order of several adjectives before a noun.

Standard order:
  1. opinion/evaluation: lovely, beautiful, awful;
  2. size: big, small, tiny;
  3. age: old, new, young;
  4. shape: round, square, long;
  5. colour: red, black;
  6. origin: Italian, French;
  7. material: wooden, silk, stone;
  8. purpose/type (often as part of a compound noun): kitchen, sports.
Example of a full chain: a lovely little old round black Italian wooden box. In practice usually 2–3 adjectives.
Examples:
  • a big old house — size + age;
  • a beautiful Italian painting — opinion + origin;
  • a round wooden table — shape + material.

Commas between adjectives of the same level (opinion): a tiring, boring day.
int_u99.exc: Between adjectives of the same type a comma or and is placed: a big, beautiful house.
mistakes: A black old small bag → a small old black bag — order: size → age → colour.
An Italian big restaurant → a big Italian restaurant — size before origin.
A wooden round table → a round wooden table — shape before material.
